source.title: Add tiered batching dashboards (latency/queue/batch size) with cardinality-safe dimensions
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.release_notes: Adds tier-aware batching dashboards for runtime-1.22: latency (p50/p95/p99) by tier/model/region, queue depth + admission/backpressure signals, and batch size distribution with cardinality controls. Includes regression panels and alert-ready queries.
source.description: Summary: This PR adds/updates Grafana dashboards to support the tiered batching defaults launch (fast/standard/cost-efficient). The goal is to make it easy to answer: (1) which tier is active and why, (2) whether latency SLOs are being met per tier, (3) whether we are building unsafe queue depth / wait time under load, and (4) whether batch formation is behaving as expected (size and wait budget). Dashboards added: (A) Tiered Batching — Overview: high-level latency (p50/p95/p99), request rate, error rate, and saturation widgets with tier selector; includes a ‘canary vs control’ comparison row (for staged rollout) keyed off rollout cohort label when present. (B) Tiered Batching — Latency & Streaming: end-to-end latency and time-to-first-token panels, split by tier; includes separate panels for streaming vs non-streaming routes to avoid blending distributions. (C) Tiered Batching — Queue & Backpressure: queue depth, queue wait time, admission control rejects, shed/load-shed counts, and scheduler overload mode indicator (when enabled). (D) Tiered Batching — Batch Formation: batch_size_tokens histogram heatmap, batch_size_requests distribution, max_wait_ms utilization, and prefill/decode occupancy (when exported). (E) Tiered Batching — Regression Triage: side-by-side ‘current vs baseline’ p95/p99 deltas for top models, plus a panel for top offenders by (tier, model_family, region). Metrics and tags: This PR assumes runtime emits a tier label and reason codes per ENG-18426 / PR-28473; dashboards are built to work even when reason codes are missing (panels gracefully hide that row). Cardinality constraints are enforced by default: panels use model_family and route_group where possible; model is a template var with a safe allowlist and defaults to ‘top 10 by traffic’. Region is a template var but panels aggregate to region_group when high-cardinality environments are detected. Also adds guidance text within dashboards: “If you need per-route debugging, switch to the Route Debug dashboard and pin a single route to avoid unbounded series.” Alert readiness: No paging alerts are added in this PR (those live in alert-rules repo), but queries were shaped to be portable to PromQL recording rules. Includes draft threshold guidance in dashboard annotations (e.g., fast tier p99 regression > 10% for 15m at constant QPS) aligned with the SRE rollout checklist. Notable changes since first draft: (1) removed direct breakdown by tenant_id and full model name from the default view after review; (2) added a ‘series count’ panel to quickly spot label explosions; (3) renamed panels to match the SLO tier naming in the customer-facing docs (fast/standard/cost-efficient). Testing/validation: Exported dashboards loaded into staging Grafana; verified query performance under typical prod cardinality by replaying a 30m window in the metrics sandbox. Confirmed that all panels render with only tier labels enabled (no reason codes) and do not error when streaming labels are absent. Related: runtime metrics tier labels (redwood PR-28492), reason codes (redwood PR-28473), rollout flagging (redwood PR-28480), internal ticket INT-33012 (missing tier breakdown labels) resolved by aligning label keys used in dashboards with the runtime’s final taxonomy.
commits: dashboards: add Tiered Batching Overview (templating + annotations)
dashboards: add latency + TTFT panels split by tier and streaming flag
dashboards: add queue depth/backpressure/admission control panels
dashboards: add batch formation histograms + wait budget utilization
dashboards: add regression triage row + top offenders table
chore: standardize label keys to tier/model_family/region_group; drop tenant_id defaults
docs: add dashboard usage notes and cardinality warning callouts
review_conversation: Allison Grant (requested changes): Please remove tenant_id and full model breakdown from default panels; this will blow up series. Also add a panel/annotation that helps operators notice cardinality spikes early.
Aisha Bello (author): Done — default breakdown is now tier x model_family x region_group. Added a ‘Series count (approx)’ panel and annotations with debugging guidance for per-route drilldowns.
Caleb Johnson (comment): TTFT panels should be streaming-only; otherwise they’ll confuse non-streaming E2E latency. Also confirm the label is `batching_tier` and not `tier` to match runtime taxonomy.
Aisha Bello (author): Split TTFT to streaming-only and renamed vars to match `batching_tier` (with backward-compat fallback to `tier` for older staging builds).

Sean Gallagher (comment): Add a row that maps to rollout cohort / feature flag state so on-call can compare canary vs control quickly during staged rollout. Doesn’t need to be perfect, but make it obvious.
Aisha Bello (author): Added a comparison row using `rollout_cohort` when present; it hides automatically if the label isn’t in the time range.
Logan Wright (approval): Query shapes look sane; thanks for adding allowlists and dropping tenant_id defaults. Approving once CI is green.
Allison Grant (approval): Looks good after cardinality changes. Please keep the note about pinning a single route for debugging — operators will miss this otherwise.
Aisha Bello (author): Kept the note + added a short ‘How to triage’ text panel at top of each dashboard.

source.title: Add metering drift Grafana dashboard + alert rules (drift, freshness, duplicates)
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.body:
Context
We’re standardizing a metering data-quality view so Finance/RevOps + Eng can quickly answer: (a) are usage events in sync with the billing ledger, (b) is data arriving on time for close/invoice previews, and (c) are we seeing duplicate usage events consistent with idempotency regressions/backfills.

This PR adds a Grafana dashboard (and initial alerting rules) for metering drift, freshness, and duplicate rates. It is intended to pair with the in-product Console sanity dashboard work (ENG-4415) and the scheduled drift job in the redwood monorepo (ENG-4421).

What’s included
- Dashboard: "Metering Sanity: Drift + Freshness" (folder: Billing/Metering)
  - Drift panels (usage_events vs billing_ledger aggregates)
    - Drift % by day (global)
    - Top-N accounts by absolute drift ($ and tokens) over last 24h / 7d
    - Drift by dimension: region, model, endpoint (chat/completions/embeddings)
    - Breakdown of drift causes when available (late_events, missing_dims, duplicates) from the drift job’s tagged metrics
  - Freshness panels
    - p50/p95 ingestion lag for usage_events (event_time -> available_in_warehouse)
    - Latest partition timestamps for key tables/exports (usage_events, usage_rollups, ledger_export)
    - "Freeze window" annotations (month-end close) based on the Finance agreed schedule
  - Duplicate rate panels
    - Duplicate usage_event_id rate (raw)
    - Duplicate dedupe_key collision rate (post-dedupe)
    - Retries/fallback signal overlays (when emitted) to correlate spikes

- Alerts (initial thresholds; tuned to avoid paging on small-account noise)
  - MeterDriftHigh (page): 24h drift % > 0.50% AND absolute drift > $2,000 OR > 5M billed_tokens, scoped per (region, endpoint)
  - MeterDriftModerate (ticket): 24h drift % > 0.25% AND absolute drift > $500
  - MeterFreshnessSLOBreach (page): p95 ingestion lag > 90 minutes for 30 minutes
  - MeterDuplicateRateHigh (page): duplicates > 0.10% of events for 20 minutes OR sustained > 0.05% for 2 hours
  - All alerts include runbook links + "known incident" annotation guidance

Metrics / data sources
- Prometheus series produced by drift job (redwood):
  - metering_drift_ratio{dimension=..., region=..., endpoint=...}
  - metering_drift_absolute_usd{...}
  - metering_drift_absolute_tokens{...}
  - metering_usage_ingestion_lag_seconds{quantile="0.95"}
  - metering_duplicate_rate{type="event_id"|"dedupe_key"}

Notes:
- This PR does not change metering logic. It only wires dashboard panels and rules to existing signals emitted by the job/export pipeline.
- For accounts with very small spend, percentage drift is noisy. Alerts gate on both % and absolute magnitude.

Rollout plan
1) Merge with alerts disabled by default (rule groups present; routing muted in prod for 48h).
2) Observe baseline noise during ongoing shadow billing compare.
3) Enable Moderate alerts to ticket first; then enable paging on DriftHigh + Freshness after threshold tuning.

Follow-ups
- Add a dedicated "embeddings batch" panel once ENG-4412 backfill completes (missing cache-hit/attribution fields).
- Add alert label for "meter_version" once shadow-billing compare exports meter_version consistently.

Screenshots
- Not included in PR (dashboards are generated from jsonnet). Use the rendered preview link in CI artifacts.

Refs
- Linear: ENG-4421 (alert on meter drift), ENG-4415 (Console dashboard)
- Slack threads: meter drift alert tuning, freshness SLO discussion
- Runbooks: Confluence runbook will be linked once published (placeholder link included).
commits: Add metering sanity dashboard jsonnet + folder wiring
Add recording rules for ingestion lag percentiles + top-N drift queries
Add alert rule group for drift/freshness/duplicates with severity gating
Document dashboard variables and drilldowns
review_comments: Caleb Johnson: Drift panels look good. Can we add a variable for warehouse (prod vs staging) to make debugging easier?
Aisha Bello: Added datasource/cluster variable in targets.libsonnet; defaults to prod but supports staging.
Mei Lin: For Freshness, can we show both p95 lag and "latest partition timestamp" for the ledger export table? That’s what Finance checks during close.
Aisha Bello: Added a stat panel for latest_export_partition_ts and a small table panel for the three key tables (usage_events, rollups, ledger_export).
Logan Wright: Alert thresholds: 0.5% seems okay, but can we gate on absolute USD/tokens to prevent paging on tiny tenants? Also please route duplicates as ticket first.

Aisha Bello: Implemented % + absolute gating (usd OR tokens) and set Moderate/Ticket vs High/Page. Duplicate alert stays page only for high sustained rate; added ticket-level condition for moderate duplicates.
Allison Grant: Please include runbook links in annotations and make sure rule labels include owner/team for on-call routing.
Aisha Bello: Added runbook_url annotation and labels: team=eng-sre, service=metering, owner=Aisha. The runbook URL is a placeholder until Confluence page is published.
source.release_notes: Internal: New Grafana dashboard + alert rules for metering drift/freshness/duplicates to support billing reconciliation and month-end close readiness.

source.title: Tiered SLO dashboards + deploy/incident annotations (Hosted vs Dedicated views)
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.release_notes: Adds tiered SLO dashboards (Hosted + Dedicated) with a comparison view, plus deploy/incident annotations for faster correlation during budget burn.
source.description: Summary: This PR adds a tiered SLO dashboard set to the observability-pack, including (1) Hosted SLO overview, (2) Dedicated SLO overview (tier-wide; no customer-level by default), and (3) a Dedicated vs Hosted comparison view. It also adds Grafana annotations for deploys and incidents so we can correlate latency spikes / streaming disconnects / 5xx bursts with changes and incident timelines. Motivation: Part of the Q1 reliability milestone to formalize tiered SLOs + error budgets, we need canonical, tier-aware views that match the new SLI tagging at ingress and avoid metric cardinality blowups. This PR is intended to be the “default” internal dashboard surface linked from burn-rate alerts and runbooks. Out of scope: customer-facing Dedicated per-account dashboards and access control (tracked in ENG-4946 follow-ups) and any changes to SLI definitions/recording rules (those land in the monorepo + telemetry repos). Dependencies/assumptions: Requires tier/deployment mode labels to be present on the primary request/latency/streaming metrics (see redwood PR-28411) and burn-rate recording rules to exist (redwood PR-28492). Key changes: (A) New dashboards: 1) SLO Overview — Hosted (tier=hosted, region filter, endpoint_class filter; panels for availability/success rate, p50/p95/p99 latency, streaming disconnect rate, and top contributors by endpoint_class + region). 2) SLO Overview — Dedicated (tier=dedicated; same core panels; includes headroom hints panel that links to fleet dashboards but does not embed per-customer dimensions). 3) SLO Compare — Hosted vs Dedicated (side-by-side availability, latency percentiles, and streaming reliability; uses a shared time range + shared region selection; intended for incident review and target negotiation with PM). (B) Annotation tracks: “Deploys” annotation fed by deploy event series (redwood_deploy_events_total + build SHA label, aggregated to avoid per-service noise), “Incidents” annotation fed by incident-bot exported events (incident_events_total) with severity and incident_id, and a “Budget burn state” annotation derived from burn-rate alert firing series for quick visual overlay. (C) Dashboard conventions: standard variables (region, endpoint_class, tier), consistent SLO links in panel descriptions, and a top-of-dashboard note on SLI exclusions (invalid requests, explicit rate limits, client aborts) to reduce confusion when support/PM teams compare dashboards with raw logs. (D) Cardinality guardrails: no per-model and no per-account breakdowns in the default dashboard; top contributors are capped to N=10 and use endpoint_class + route_group only (route_group is derived/normalized by recording rules). A hidden “__debug” toggle exists for SRE-only use to reveal model breakdown if the underlying metrics are safe; default is off. Validation: ran grafana-lint + jsonnet compilation; loaded dashboards into staging Grafana and verified variables resolve for us-east-1/eu-west-1; spot-checked that streaming disconnect panel does not include client aborts per current metric taxonomy. Screenshots: included in the PR conversation (staging links). Rollout: once merged, this pack is picked up by the weekly observability-pack sync job; SRE will pin alert runbook links to the new dashboard UIDs after deploy.
commits: dashboards/slo: add tiered SLO overview dashboards (hosted + dedicated)
dashboards/slo: add hosted-vs-dedicated comparison dashboard
grafana: add deploy + incident annotation queries and default styling
dashboards: standardize variables/templating and add SLO notes + runbook links
lint: add dashboard UID collision check in CI
conversation: Allison Grant (review): Can we avoid any dependency on per-route raw labels? I’m worried about cardinality if folks start adding route=… panels ad hoc. Also, please add a short note in the dashboard about SLI exclusions (rate limited, invalid, client abort) so Support doesn’t misinterpret the gap vs access logs.

Sergio Costa (author): Good call. I switched all “top contributors” panels to use route_group (normalized) and added the SLI exclusions note + links to the SLI spec. Also added a hard cap topk(10) and set the default to endpoint_class only.

Thomas Baird (review): The deploy annotation query is grouping by service + sha; that can still get noisy in multi-service rollouts. Can we aggregate to a single event per environment and show sha in the annotation text instead?
Sergio Costa (author): Updated: deploy annotations now reduce to env+sha (and include service list as truncated text). Also added a 15m dedupe window so repeated deploy events don’t spam.

Aisha Bello (review): The streaming disconnect panel should explicitly exclude client aborts (we’ve had confusion on SUP-28501). Do we have a metric split we can rely on, or do we need to call that out as a known limitation?

Sergio Costa (author): The panel now uses stream_disconnects_total{reason!="client_abort"} and includes a tooltip note. If the reason label is missing, it will show as “unknown” and still count; that’s consistent with current SLI guidance. Added a link to the runbook section on classifying disconnects.

Felix Schneider (review): Comparison dashboard looks good, but can we align the time series colors across Hosted/Dedicated so screenshots don’t get confusing? Also add an explicit “Tier” legend label.
Sergio Costa (author): Done: hosted is always blue, dedicated always green, and legend labels are prefixed with the tier string.
Logan Wright (review): Please ensure the dashboards still render if burn-rate recording rules aren’t present (some regions lag during rollout).
Sergio Costa (author): Added “or vector(0)” fallback for burn panels and a banner panel that warns when the recording rule series is absent in the selected region/time range.

title: Tiered SLO dashboards + deploy/incident annotations (Hosted vs Dedicated views)
preview: Summary: This PR adds a tiered SLO dashboard set to the observability-pack, including (1) Hosted SLO overview, (2) Dedicated SLO overview (tier-wide; no customer-level by default), and (3) a Dedicated vs Hosted comparison view. It also adds Grafana annotations for deploys and incidents so we can correlate latency spikes / streaming disconnects / 5xx bursts with changes and incident timelines. Motivation: Part of the Q1 reliability milestone to formalize tiered SLOs + error budgets, we need canonical, tier-aware views that match the new SLI tagging at ingress and avoid metric cardinality blowups. This PR is intended to be the “default” internal dashboard surface linked from burn-rate alerts and runbooks. Out of scope: customer-facing Dedicated per-account dashboards and access control (tracked in ENG-4946 follow-ups) and any changes to SLI definitions/recording rules (those land in the monorepo + telemetry repos). Dependencies/assumptions: Requires tier/deployment mode labels to be present on the primary request/latency/streaming metrics (see redwood PR-28411) and burn-rate recording rules to exist (redwood PR-28492). Key changes: (A) New dashboards: 1) SLO Overview — Hosted (tier=hosted, region filter, endpoint_class filter; panels for availability/success rate, p50/p95/p99 latency, streaming disconnect rate, and top contributors by endpoint_class + region). 2) SLO Overview — Dedicated (tier=dedicated; same core panels; includes headroom hints panel that links to fleet dashboards but does not embed per-customer dimensions). 3) SLO Compare — Hosted vs Dedicated (side-by-side availability, latency percentiles, and streaming reliability; uses a shared time range + shared region selection; intended for incident review and target negotiation with PM). (B) Annotation tracks: “Deploys” annotation fed by deploy event series (redwood_deploy_events_total + build SHA label, aggregated to avoid per-service noise), “Incidents” annotation fed by incident-bot exported events (incident_events_total) with severity and incident_id, and a “Budget burn state” annotation derived from burn-rate alert firing series for quick visual overlay. (C) Dashboard conventions: standard variables (region, endpoint_class, tier), consistent SLO links in panel descriptions, and a top-of-dashboard note on SLI exclusions (invalid requests, explicit rate limits, client aborts) to reduce confusion when support/PM teams compare dashboards with raw logs. (D) Cardinality guardrails: no per-model and no per-account breakdowns in the default dashboard; top contributors are capped to N=10 and use endpoint_class + route_group only (route_group is derived/normalized by recording rules). A hidden “__debug” toggle exists for SRE-only use to reveal model breakdown if the underlying metrics are safe; default is off. Validation: ran grafana-lint + jsonnet compilation; loaded dashboards into staging Grafana and verified variables resolve for us-east-1/eu-west-1; spot-checked that streaming disconnect panel does not include client aborts per current metric taxonomy. Screenshots: included in the PR conversation (staging links). Rollout: once merged, this pack is picked up by the weekly observability-pack sync job; SRE will pin alert runbook links to the new dashboard UIDs after deploy.

source.title: Add FP8/INT8 benchmark + rollout Grafana dashboards and canary alerting
source.author: Allison Grant
commits: grafana: add fp8/int8 benchmark run dashboard (TTFT/TPOT/tokens/s + GPU util/mem)
grafana: add rollout/canary comparison dashboard with cohort + baseline overlays
prometheus: recording rules for stable p50/p95 TTFT/TPOT and tokens/s by model/gpu/mode/region
alerting: canary alert rules w/ min-sample gating + noise suppression
docs: add import/provisioning notes + required metric labels
release_notes: New Grafana dashboards for FP8/INT8 benchmark runs and rollout monitoring (TTFT/TPOT/tokens/s, GPU util/mem, error rates).
New canary alert rules for quantized-kernel rollouts with min-sample gating to reduce false positives.
source.body:
## Context
Serving Runtime is running an FP8/INT8 kernel benchmarking + staged rollout sprint. SRE/Runtime asked for a single place to monitor benchmark runs (offline) and the perf-canary rollout (online) with consistent dimensions.

This PR adds a small dashboard pack + alert rules in `observability-pack`:
- Grafana dashboards for benchmark runs and rollout monitoring
- Prometheus recording rules to normalize TTFT/TPOT/tokens/s aggregations and reduce query cost
- Alert rules intended for perf canary gating (and incident-style triage)

## What’s included
### 1) Dashboard: **FP8/INT8 – Benchmark Runs**
Folder: `Serving Runtime / Performance`
Primary purpose: benchmark execution monitoring + sanity checks while running the matrix.
Panels:
- TTFT (ms) p50/p95 by `model`, `gpu_sku`, `kernel_mode` (baseline|fp8|int8), `region`
- TPOT (ms/token) p50/p95 (decode-heavy visibility)
- Tokens/s (effective) and requests/s
- Error rate + error codes (runtime error class breakdown)
- GPU util (%), SM active (% if exported), GPU memory used/total, OOM counters
- Scheduler breakdown panels (if present): prefill vs decode time share, queue depth p95

Benchmark-specific UX:
- Requires `bench_run_id` label when available; otherwise filters by `job` + `git_sha`.
- Adds an annotation query for rollout toggles (if `kernel_mode` flag events are shipped; optional).

### 2) Dashboard: **FP8/INT8 – Rollout / Perf Canary**
Primary purpose: compare canary cohort vs baseline cohort during staged enablement.
Panels:
- Canary vs baseline overlays for TTFT p95, TPOT p95, tokens/s p50 (time-aligned)
- Heatmap panels (TTFT buckets) to catch tail regressions masked by p95
- Slice-by tables (top regressions) grouped by `model`, `gpu_sku`, `region`, `kernel_mode`
- Error budget quick-view: error rate, timeout rate, fallback rate (where emitted)
- GPU saturation + memory headroom (to detect scheduler/packing changes masquerading as kernel wins)

### 3) Dashboard: **FP8/INT8 – GPU Health (Perf Context)**
Primary purpose: quickly rule out “it’s the fleet” during noisy runs.
Panels:
- GPU clocks (when exported), thermal throttle counters (if present), GPU util and mem
- Node-level saturation and queue depth correlation
- Error spikes correlated with GPU OOM / CUDA errors

## Metrics and dimensions
This PR assumes the runtime metrics are tagged with (at minimum):
- `model`
- `gpu_sku`
- `region`
- `kernel_mode` (baseline|fp8|int8)

Where available, dashboards also use:
- `cohort` (baseline|canary) for perf-canary comparisons
- `prefill_decode` (prefill|decode) for scheduler splits

If any of these labels are missing, dashboards will still render but lose key filtering/overlays.

## Alerting (perf canary)
New alerts are scoped to the perf-canary use case (fast detection + low false positives). Summary:
- **PerfCanaryTTFTP95Regression**: TTFT p95 regression >= 8% vs baseline for 10m, gated on minimum sample size.
- **PerfCanaryTPOTP95Regression**: TPOT p95 regression >= 6% vs baseline for 10m, gated on minimum sample size.
- **PerfCanaryTokensPerSecDrop**: tokens/s p50 drop >= 7% vs baseline for 15m.
- **PerfCanaryErrorRateSpike**: error rate > 0.5% for 5m (or +3x vs baseline where comparable).
- **PerfCanaryOOMSpike**: GPU OOM counter increase above threshold.

Noise suppression:
- Recording rules compute rolling rates and quantiles on fixed windows.
- All perf-regression alerts include a `min_requests` gate to avoid small-sample false positives (feedback from #eng-sre).

Routing:
- Alerts default to `#eng-sre` / `#eng-runtime` (per existing alertmanager routing for the folder). No paging is configured by default; SRE can promote to paging per rollout.

## How to test
- `make grafana-render` (renders jsonnet → dashboard JSON)
- `make grafana-lint` (format + schema checks)
- `promtool test rules prometheus/tests/fp8_int8_alerts_test.yaml`

## Rollout / compatibility notes
- No changes to metric emission in this repo.
- If `kernel_mode` isn’t present yet in a given cluster, tables/overlays will show “(missing)” and queries will collapse across modes.
- The canary dashboard expects `cohort` labels if perf-canary is emitting them; otherwise use the `region` + `kernel_mode` filters.

## Screenshots
Attached in PR artifacts: benchmark overview (TTFT/TPOT), canary overlay, and slice-by table examples.

## Review notes / decision points
- Threshold defaults are intentionally conservative. Rafael requested we start with non-paging alerts and tighten once we see real noise levels.
- Marcus requested scheduler queue depth + prefill/decode split panels, included in this revision (panels gracefully hide if missing).

## Related
- Linear: ENG-7427 (dashboards), ENG-7426 (canary thresholds)
- Runtime PRs: metrics tagging for model/gpu/mode is tracked separately; this pack assumes those labels exist.

---
### Timeline
- 2026-02-13: Initial dashboard scaffolding + folder wiring
- 2026-02-16: Added recording rules + first alert pass
- 2026-02-18: Noise suppression + min-sample gating; added scheduler split panels
- 2026-02-19: Final review, promtool tests, merged

source.title: Add Suggestions API + funnel Grafana dashboards and beta alerting
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.description: Summary: Adds a first-pass set of Grafana dashboards + alert rules for the Optimization Suggestions beta, covering (1) Suggestions API health (latency/errors/payload) and (2) the Suggestions view funnel (impression → expand → dismiss → apply-click), including breakdowns by org/model/route/region with conservative cardinality controls. Motivation: during dogfood we had a p95 regression + early customer reports of “empty despite traffic” and “estimates look off”; we need a single place for SRE/Platform + product to validate the pipeline (compute freshness), the API (error/latency), and the UX funnel (are people seeing/using suggestions). Scope notes: This PR only adds dashboards/alerts in observability-pack; it assumes the backend/UI already emits the metrics/events (ENG-19402) and the Suggestions API exists (ENG-19341). No changes to application code here. Dashboards added: (A) Optimize / Suggestions API Health: request rate, p50/p95 latency, 4xx/5xx, top error codes, payload size, cache hit/miss (if available), and per-route hot spots. Includes links to the runbook for triage. (B) Optimize / Suggestions Funnel (Beta): counts + conversion rates for suggestions_impression, suggestions_row_expand, suggestions_dismiss, suggestions_apply_click; time series + cohort table, with optional filters (org_id, project_id, model, route, region) and a “minimum volume” guardrail. (C) Optimize / Suggestions Compute Freshness: staleness/lag from the compute job (stale_as_of age), recompute success/failure, and lag percentiles so support can tell if “insufficient telemetry” is real vs pipeline delay. Alerting: Adds lightweight beta alert rules with conservative thresholds and paging only on clear system issues. SuggestionsAPIHigh5xxRate (page): 5xx > 1% for 10m (per env) with a minimum request volume gate. SuggestionsAPIP95LatencyHigh (page): p95 > 350ms for 15m (same volume gate). SuggestionsComputeLagHigh (page): compute freshness lag p95 > 6h for 30m. SuggestionsEventIngestionDrop (ticket): impressions drop to ~0 while API request volume is non-zero (helps catch client-side event pipeline breaks). All alerts include runbook links and labels for routing to eng-sre/eng-platform. Privacy/cardinality: Funnel breakdowns use org_id/project_id (numeric/UUID) and do not include org name; we cap table panels to top-N by volume and add a $__min_volume variable to reduce noisy / low-signal slices. Notes for rollout: Dashboards are safe to merge before the beta flag expands; alerts should be enabled in staging immediately and in prod with “ticket” severity for 24h before paging (see checklist).
commits: Add suggestions API health dashboard (latency/errors/payload panels)
Add suggestions funnel dashboard (events + conversion panels, filters, top-N tables)
Add compute freshness dashboard (stale_as_of lag + job outcomes)
Add alert rules for suggestions API + compute lag + event ingestion drop
Wire dashboards into mixin/provisioning and docs links
review_conversation: Allison Grant (review): The dashboards look good, but we need to be careful with cardinality on the funnel breakdown panels. Can we (1) default to no org_id breakdown, (2) cap top-N to 20, and (3) add a minimum volume guard to prevent noisy slices? Also please ensure no org names appear anywhere in queries/legends.

Aisha Bello (author): Updated funnel dashboard: top-N tables capped to 20, added $__min_volume (default 200 events/24h) and moved org_id/project_id breakdown into an opt-in row. Queries use org_id/project_id only (no slugs/names).

Sean Gallagher (review): Alert thresholds: p95>300ms might be too tight given we’ve seen cold-cache spikes; can we bump to 350ms and require >50 rpm to page? Also add runbook links in annotations/labels.
Aisha Bello (author): Done. p95 threshold set to 350ms for 15m, added min volume gates (requests_per_min > 50) for both latency + 5xx alerts. Runbook links added to annotations.

Logan Wright (review): For the ingestion drop alert, check the metric source. If we’re using UI event counts from the analytics pipeline, it can lag. Make the severity ticket-only and compare vs API request volume to avoid false positives.

Aisha Bello (author): Adjusted ingestion drop alert to ticket severity, gating on non-zero API request volume and a 30m window; description notes analytics lag and points to compute freshness dashboard for correlation.

Dylan Brooks (review): Can we add a panel to correlate API 5xx spikes with error_code/status_code and a link to the suggestions API triage runbook? This was missing when we debugged the last p95 regression.
Aisha Bello (author): Added “Top error codes (5xx)” panel and dashboard link-out to the triage runbook section. Also added a small annotation row with deploy markers (where available).
Allison Grant (approval): Approved with the cardinality guardrails. Please keep an eye on query cost in prod once enabled.
Sean Gallagher (approval): Approved. Let’s enable prod alerts as ticket-only for 24h then promote to paging.
source.release_notes: Internal: Added Grafana dashboards + beta alerting for Optimize Suggestions (API health, compute freshness, and UI funnel).

SuggestionsAPIHigh5xxRate (page): 5xx > 1% for 10m (per env) with a minimum request volume gate. SuggestionsAPIP95LatencyHigh (page): p95 > 350ms for 15m (same volume gate). SuggestionsComputeLagHigh (page): compute freshness lag p95 > 6h for 30m. SuggestionsEventIngestionDrop (ticket): impressions drop to ~0 while API request volume is non-zero (helps catch client-side event pipeline breaks). All alerts include runbook links and labels for routing to eng-sre/eng-platform. Privacy/cardinality: Funnel breakdowns use org_id/project_id (numeric/UUID) and do not include org name; we cap table panels to top-N by volume and add a $__min_volume variable to reduce noisy / low-signal slices. Notes for rollout: Dashboards are safe to merge before the beta flag expands; alerts should be enabled in staging immediately and in prod with “ticket” severity for 24h before paging (see checklist).
text: Summary: Adds a first-pass set of Grafana dashboards + alert rules for the Optimization Suggestions beta, covering (1) Suggestions API health (latency/errors/payload) and (2) the Suggestions view funnel (impression → expand → dismiss → apply-click), including breakdowns by org/model/route/region with conservative cardinality controls. Motivation: during dogfood we had a p95 regression + early customer reports of “empty despite traffic” and “estimates look off”; we need a single place for SRE/Platform + product to validate the pipeline (compute freshness), the API (error/latency), and the UX funnel (are people seeing/using suggestions). Scope notes: This PR only adds dashboards/alerts in observability-pack; it assumes the backend/UI already emits the metrics/events (ENG-19402) and the Suggestions API exists (ENG-19341). No changes to application code here. Dashboards added: (A) Optimize / Suggestions API Health: request rate, p50/p95 latency, 4xx/5xx, top error codes, payload size, cache hit/miss (if available), and per-route hot spots. Includes links to the runbook for triage. (B) Optimize / Suggestions Funnel (Beta): counts + conversion rates for suggestions_impression, suggestions_row_expand, suggestions_dismiss, suggestions_apply_click; time series + cohort table, with optional filters (org_id, project_id, model, route, region) and a “minimum volume” guardrail. (C) Optimize / Suggestions Compute Freshness: staleness/lag from the compute job (stale_as_of age), recompute success/failure, and lag percentiles so support can tell if “insufficient telemetry” is real vs pipeline delay. Alerting: Adds lightweight beta alert rules with conservative thresholds and paging only on clear system issues. SuggestionsAPIHigh5xxRate (page): 5xx > 1% for 10m (per env) with a minimum request volume gate. SuggestionsAPIP95LatencyHigh (page): p95 > 350ms for 15m (same volume gate). SuggestionsComputeLagHigh (page): compute freshness lag p95 > 6h for 30m. SuggestionsEventIngestionDrop (ticket): impressions drop to ~0 while API request volume is non-zero (helps catch client-side event pipeline breaks). All alerts include runbook links and labels for routing to eng-sre/eng-platform. Privacy/cardinality: Funnel breakdowns use org_id/project_id (numeric/UUID) and do not include org name; we cap table panels to top-N by volume and add a $__min_volume variable to reduce noisy / low-signal slices. Notes for rollout: Dashboards are safe to merge before the beta flag expands; alerts should be enabled in staging immediately and in prod with “ticket” severity for 24h before paging (see checklist).

source.title: Audit log pipeline dashboards + alerts (ingestion latency, drop rate) with runbook links
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.release_notes: Adds a dedicated Audit Log Pipeline Grafana dashboard and Prometheus alert rules for ingestion latency and drop rate; alerts link directly to the audit log pipeline incident runbook.
source.description:
Context: As part of the audit logging standardization effort we’re tightening operational visibility for the audit event ingestion/export path. We’ve had a couple of near-misses where ingestion lag spiked without a clear on-call landing page, and early alert drafts lacked enough context to triage quickly.

This PR adds:
- A Grafana dashboard for the audit-log ingestion pipeline (end-to-end delivery latency, ingest backlog, drop rate, retry/429/5xx, DLQ growth).
- Alert rules for delivery latency and drop rate with sane defaults + per-environment routing.
- Runbook links embedded in alert annotations and dashboard panel descriptions.

Key signals / definitions:
- delivery_latency_seconds: time from event emission at producer to ack at ingestion sink (measured via audit-ingest gateway metrics + exporter timestamps).
- drop_rate: fraction of events not successfully delivered after retry budget (from ingest and exporter counters).
- backlog: queued events in buffer (when enabled) + ingestion consumer lag.

Alert thresholds (initial):
- AuditLogDeliveryLatencyP99High: p99(delivery_latency_seconds) > 60s for 10m (page: enterprise tiers; ticket: others).
- AuditLogDropRateHigh: drop_rate > 0.10% for 5m (page; includes per-region breakdown).
- AuditLogIngestBacklogGrowing: backlog > 50k AND increasing for 15m (ticket; pages if > 200k).

Cardinality guardrails:
- Dashboard panels group primarily by region, deployment_mode (hosted|dedicated|private), and service. We intentionally do not group by raw tenant_id/project_id. event_type is only used in a single table panel with top-N + allow-listed types.

Runbooks:
- Incident runbook: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/audit-log-pipeline-incident-runbook.json
- Coverage regression runbook is linked from the dashboard “Coverage” row for follow-up when events are missing but pipeline is healthy.

Testing:
- jsonnetfmt + dashboards render in CI.
- promtool rules tests added for the new alert expressions.

Notes:
- This is intended to be the “default landing page” linked from paging alerts. We can iterate thresholds after a week of production data; see INT-3984 for noise tuning follow-ups.
commits: observability: add audit-log pipeline dashboard jsonnet + README
alerts: add audit log delivery latency/drop rate rules with runbook annotations
tests: add promtool unit tests for audit-log alert rules
chore: factor shared recording rules for delivery latency histogram buckets
source.review_conversation:
Sean Gallagher (SRE): Thresholds look OK but we should avoid paging on backlog alone. Can we gate backlog alert on backlog increasing + delivery latency elevated?

Aisha Bello (author): Updated AuditLogIngestBacklogGrowing to require backlog increasing for 15m and changed paging behavior: backlog alert is ticket-only unless backlog > 200k. Delivery latency + drop rate remain paging.

Allison Grant (Observability standards): Please add panel descriptions for metric definitions and ensure runbook links use the canonical Confluence path. Also, avoid grouping by tenant_id.

Aisha Bello: Added metric definitions to the top row and removed the one panel that was grouping by tenant_id (replaced with deployment_mode + region). Runbook links updated to the canonical path.

Kevin Osei (SIEM/export): Can we include exporter-side 429/5xx and retry saturation signals? That’s what we saw on VectorGrid.

Aisha Bello: Added a new “Exporter health” row with 429/5xx rate, retry queue depth, and a panel for “duplicates detected (dedupe key collisions)” where supported.

Rafael Mendes (Runbooks): Please include direct links to the incident comms template section in the runbook and add an annotation that we treat delivery as at-least-once (duplicates possible).

Aisha Bello: Added alert annotation: “Delivery is at-least-once; dedupe on event_id in downstream SIEM.” Added deep link to runbook comms templates.

Approvals:
- Sean Gallagher: Approved
- Allison Grant: Approved
- Kevin Osei: Approved
- Rafael Mendes: Approved
source.final_outcome: Merged via squash. Deployed with observability-pack v2026.02.19. Alerts enabled in staging immediately; production paging enabled for enterprise routes after 24h bake-in.

source.title: Quality gates: dashboards + alert rules (pass rate, failure spikes, latency, flake rate)
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.description: Adds first-class observability for Optimize quality gate runs. This PR introduces (1) a Grafana dashboard for the full gate-run funnel (created → running → passed/failed/overridden), (2) Prometheus alert rules for gate failure spikes, flake rate regressions, and gate execution latency/SLO breaches, and (3) runbook links + consistent labels so we can slice by config_type (batching/cache/quant_profile), model_family, route_id, account_tier, and region. Motivation: we’re rolling out mandatory gating ahead of applying Optimize recommendations; we need to catch (a) real quality regressions, (b) eval-harness instability/flaky suites causing false blocks, and (c) infrastructure constraints causing timeouts (notably EU-West). Design notes: alerts avoid paging on single-run failures; they trigger on burn-rate / sustained increases and include a ‘possible flake’ annotation when rerun aggregation indicates high variance. Dashboard includes deep links back into Console (gate run id) and the gate failure runbook for oncall. This aligns metric names/tags with the quality-gates observability standard and uses the new gate_run_id trace correlation field emitted by the Optimize service. Rollout: rules are initially deployed in ‘warning’ severity for 1 week; paging thresholds are enabled after we confirm baseline noise levels in limited production rollout.
commits: dashboards: add Quality Gates Overview dashboard (funnel, breakdowns, latency)
alerts: add gate failure spike + pass rate drop alert rules with burn-rate windows
alerts: add flake-rate regression rule (quarantine signal) and annotation text
alerts: add gate execution latency SLO rule + non-paging warning stage
docs: wire runbook links + dashboard metadata + owners
chore: unify label keys (config_type/model_family/account_tier/region) across panels
source.implementation_details: Dashboard panels: Gate runs (count) by status; pass/fail/override rate over time; failures by config_type + model_family; failures by route_id (top-k); flake score distribution; eval runtime (p50/p95/p99) split by region; queue time vs execution time; cost-of-gates proxy (gpu_seconds) when available; canary correlation panel (gate outcomes vs rollout promotion halts). Alert rules: (1) quality_gates_failure_spike: triggers when failed_rate over 15m is >3x 7d baseline and absolute failed_count >= 10, severity=page after the rollout period; (2) quality_gates_pass_rate_drop: burn-rate style, compares 1h vs 24h for pass_rate by config_type; (3) quality_gates_flake_rate_regression: warns when flake_score_p95 > threshold for a prompt_set_id cohort (no paging; directs to quarantine workflow); (4) quality_gates_latency_slo_breach: warns on p95 gate_duration_seconds > 20m for 30m window and pages only when paired with timeout_count increase (prevents noisy pages during GPU scarcity). All alerts include annotations with runbook URL + suggested triage tags and a pre-filled Grafana explore link.
source.review_conversation: Allison Grant: The panels look good, but please align naming with the observability standard (gate_run_status vs status) and make sure all alerts include route_id/account_tier labels for enterprise drill-down. Also: can we avoid paging on flake spikes until quarantine workflow is live? Sergio Costa: Updated label keys to match standard (gate_status, config_type, model_family, account_tier, region) and added route_id/account_tier breakdown variables. Flake rule remains warning-only and now includes ‘quarantine_candidate=true’ annotation. Sean Gallagher: For paging rules, we need multi-window burn-rate, otherwise we’ll page on brief eval-harness blips. Also add a guard that avoids paging when total_runs is low (new routes). Sergio Costa: Added two-window condition (5m + 1h) for failure spike and min total_runs >= 50 over 1h for paging. Also added a rollout-phase toggle: severity=warning by default, can be flipped to page via values file. Caleb Johnson: Do we have trace correlation? I’d like the alert to link to traces by gate_run_id. Sergio Costa: Added annotation gate_run_id and query template for Tempo trace search; also included a panel that shows trace sample links for top failures. Connor O'Brien: Please ensure rules compile under our promtool CI and don’t exceed label cardinality limits (route_id can explode). Sergio Costa: Limited the rule group label sets to config_type/model_family/region/account_tier; route_id is dashboard-only with top-k queries. promtool passes.
source.ci_summary: Checks: lint (pass), jsonnetfmt (pass), grafana-dashboard-validate (pass), promtool (pass), unit (pass). One transient failure on grafana-dashboard-validate due to upstream schema fetch; rerun succeeded.
source.release_notes: Adds Quality Gates Overview dashboard and initial alert rules (warning-only by default) for gate failures, latency SLO breaches, and flake-rate regressions. Alerts include runbook links and are tuned to avoid paging on single-run failures.

source.title: Add SDK retry/timeout/streaming disconnect dashboard panels + rollout alerts
source.author: Nadia Rahman
source.description:
Context: As part of the cross-language SDK parity rollout (standard retries/backoff, timeout model, and streaming disconnect handling), SRE requested first-class dashboards and conservative alerts to monitor load impact and customer-facing failure rates during beta/stable releases. This PR adds (1) new panels to the existing "SDK Client Health" Grafana dashboard for retry rate, timeout rate, and streaming disconnect reasons, (2) companion alert rules with rollout-friendly thresholds and burn-rate style paging, and (3) a small set of recording rules to make the queries cheaper/consistent across environments.

What’s included: - Dashboard panels: Retry rate (by sdk_language, endpoint, model, status_code), Retry attempts distribution (p50/p95 of retry_count per request), Timeout rate (by sdk_language, timeout_scope=overall|connect|read), Timeout duration (histogram heatmap where available), Streaming disconnect rate (by sdk_language, endpoint, reason), Top streaming disconnect reasons (table), and a rollout view grouped by sdk_version (when the label is present). - Alerts (disabled-by-default in prod, enabled in beta + dogfood): (A) High SDK retry rate (warn), (B) High SDK timeout rate (warn), (C) Streaming disconnect spike (warn), (D) Potential retry storm indicator (page) using (retry_total / request_total) + 429 rate + concurrency as gates. - Recording rules: pre-aggregate sdk_client_request_total, sdk_client_retry_total, sdk_client_timeout_total, sdk_client_stream_disconnect_total into 5m rate series with a bounded label set.

Metrics used (existing / recently standardized fields): - sdk_client_request_total{sdk_language, sdk_version?, endpoint, model, status_code} - sdk_client_retry_total{sdk_language, endpoint, reason} - sdk_client_timeout_total{sdk_language, endpoint, timeout_scope} - sdk_client_stream_disconnect_total{sdk_language, endpoint, reason} - sdk_client_request_duration_seconds_bucket (optional; used when emitted)

Notes / assumptions: - Streaming disconnect reasons follow the parity taxonomy being rolled out in SDKs (e.g., server_fin, read_timeout, client_cancel, proxy_reset, tls_error, unknown). Panels will still render if reason is missing (falls into reason="unknown" in the dashboards). - To avoid alert noise from a small number of clients, alerts include a minimum volume gate (requests_per_min) and are scoped to the rollout dashboards folder. - This PR intentionally does NOT add new instrumentation; it only consumes the standardized fields being added in the SDK repos. If a given SDK release doesn’t yet include sdk_version label, the dashboard will still work (the sdk_version view will simply be empty).

Rollout plan: - Merge now; ship to dogfood + beta monitoring stacks. - Enable alerts in beta immediately; keep prod alerts muted until stable releases are out (target: after ENG-5131 go/no-go). - During the first week of stable: watch retry_rate, timeout_rate, and disconnect_reason mix for proxy_reset/read_timeout regressions; use request_id sampling from SDK logs to correlate.

Testing: - Rendered dashboards via jsonnet/grafonnet compile locally; validated queries against staging Prometheus with synthetic load. - Ran alert rule unit tests (promtool) with fixtures covering 429 spikes and elevated read timeouts.

Screenshots: Included in the PR conversation (Grafana panels: Retry rate by language, Streaming disconnect reasons table).

Follow-ups: - Add a dedicated panel for Retry-After compliance once the gateway header adoption is >90% (separate PR). - Consider a dashboard annotation hook for SDK release cut times (Connor coordinating in ENG-RELEASES).
commits: dashboards: add retry/timeout/disconnect panels to SDK Client Health
alerts: add rollout alert rules for retry/timeout/disconnect spikes
rules: add recording rules to bound label cardinality and reduce query cost
docs: update README with dashboard import + alert enablement notes
changed_files: dashboards/sdk-client-health.jsonnet (new panels + variables: sdk_language, endpoint, model, sdk_version)
scripts/validate-dashboards.sh (ensure compiled artifacts match jsonnet sources)
README.md (how to import dashboard + enable alerts in beta)
source.ci_checks: CI summary: ✅ lint (jsonnetfmt + yamllint) ✅ unit (promtool rule tests) ✅ build (dashboard compile) ✅ verify (git diff compiled dashboards clean). Notable run: build failed once on 2026-02-10 due to grafonnet version pin mismatch; rerun after updating the lockfile passed.
source.conversation:
2026-02-03 Nadia Rahman (author): Posted initial dashboards + alerts; called out that sdk_version is optional and alerts are gated by volume.

2026-02-04 Allison Grant: Query feedback: "For retry rate, can we normalize by request volume and avoid status_code label on the top-level chart to reduce cardinality? Also suggest splitting timeouts by timeout_scope and keeping endpoint grouping to a short allowlist." 
2026-02-04 Nadia Rahman: Ack. Updated panels to: (1) default view aggregates status_code, with a drilldown panel for status_code, (2) timeout_scope split panels, (3) endpoint variable defaults to top 20 by traffic.

2026-02-06 Logan Wright: "Can we align metric names/labels with the telemetry schema doc (retry_count vs retry_total)?" Also requested adding explicit mention that we do not log API keys in any dashboards.
2026-02-06 Nadia Rahman: Confirmed we’re consuming sdk_client_retry_total (not retry_count) and that retry_count is derived as a per-request distribution only when the histogram is available; added README note about label hygiene + no secrets.

2026-02-08 Sean Gallagher: Alerting feedback: "No paging on day 1; we only page if we see a potential retry storm. Add concurrency/429 gates and a 15m sustain window. Also please link runbook sections for each alert." 
2026-02-09 Nadia Rahman: Implemented: paging alert requires (retry_rate > 0.25 AND 429_rate > 0.05 AND request_rpm > 200) for 15m; warn alerts remain for 10m; added runbook anchors + suggested next actions.

2026-02-10 Monica Patel: "Can we add a panel that correlates disconnect reason with sdk_language so DevEx can see if this is only Go streaming vs others?" 
2026-02-10 Nadia Rahman: Added stacked time series: stream_disconnect_rate by reason, split by sdk_language.

2026-02-12 Allison Grant: Requested change: "Recording rules should drop model for the top-level aggregates; keep model only on the drilldown dashboard." 
2026-02-12 Nadia Rahman: Updated recording rules to record two series: (a) no-model aggregate for alerts/top panels, (b) optional model dimension used only by drilldown panels.

2026-02-14 Sean Gallagher: Approved after verifying alert gates + runbook links. 
2026-02-14 Allison Grant: Approved. 
2026-02-14 Logan Wright: Approved.
release_notes: Added Grafana panels to monitor SDK retry rate, timeout rate, and streaming disconnect reasons during SDK parity rollout.
Added rollout-friendly alert rules (warn + gated page) with runbook links for triage.

text:
Context: As part of the cross-language SDK parity rollout (standard retries/backoff, timeout model, and streaming disconnect handling), SRE requested first-class dashboards and conservative alerts to monitor load impact and customer-facing failure rates during beta/stable releases. This PR adds (1) new panels to the existing "SDK Client Health" Grafana dashboard for retry rate, timeout rate, and streaming disconnect reasons, (2) companion alert rules with rollout-friendly thresholds and burn-rate style paging, and (3) a small set of recording rules to make the queries cheaper/consistent across environments.

What’s included: - Dashboard panels: Retry rate (by sdk_language, endpoint, model, status_code), Retry attempts distribution (p50/p95 of retry_count per request), Timeout rate (by sdk_language, timeout_scope=overall|connect|read), Timeout duration (histogram heatmap where available), Streaming disconnect rate (by sdk_language, endpoint, reason), Top streaming disconnect reasons (table), and a rollout view grouped by sdk_version (when the label is present). - Alerts (disabled-by-default in prod, enabled in beta + dogfood): (A) High SDK retry rate (warn), (B) High SDK timeout rate (warn), (C) Streaming disconnect spike (warn), (D) Potential retry storm indicator (page) using (retry_total / request_total) + 429 rate + concurrency as gates. - Recording rules: pre-aggregate sdk_client_request_total, sdk_client_retry_total, sdk_client_timeout_total, sdk_client_stream_disconnect_total into 5m rate series with a bounded label set.

Metrics used (existing / recently standardized fields): - sdk_client_request_total{sdk_language, sdk_version?, endpoint, model, status_code} - sdk_client_retry_total{sdk_language, endpoint, reason} - sdk_client_timeout_total{sdk_language, endpoint, timeout_scope} - sdk_client_stream_disconnect_total{sdk_language, endpoint, reason} - sdk_client_request_duration_seconds_bucket (optional; used when emitted)

Notes / assumptions: - Streaming disconnect reasons follow the parity taxonomy being rolled out in SDKs (e.g., server_fin, read_timeout, client_cancel, proxy_reset, tls_error, unknown). Panels will still render if reason is missing (falls into reason="unknown" in the dashboards). - To avoid alert noise from a small number of clients, alerts include a minimum volume gate (requests_per_min) and are scoped to the rollout dashboards folder. - This PR intentionally does NOT add new instrumentation; it only consumes the standardized fields being added in the SDK repos. If a given SDK release doesn’t yet include sdk_version label, the dashboard will still work (the sdk_version view will simply be empty).

Rollout plan: - Merge now; ship to dogfood + beta monitoring stacks. - Enable alerts in beta immediately; keep prod alerts muted until stable releases are out (target: after ENG-5131 go/no-go). - During the first week of stable: watch retry_rate, timeout_rate, and disconnect_reason mix for proxy_reset/read_timeout regressions; use request_id sampling from SDK logs to correlate.

Testing: - Rendered dashboards via jsonnet/grafonnet compile locally; validated queries against staging Prometheus with synthetic load. - Ran alert rule unit tests (promtool) with fixtures covering 429 spikes and elevated read timeouts.

Screenshots: Included in the PR conversation (Grafana panels: Retry rate by language, Streaming disconnect reasons table).

Follow-ups: - Add a dedicated panel for Retry-After compliance once the gateway header adoption is >90% (separate PR). - Consider a dashboard annotation hook for SDK release cut times (Connor coordinating in ENG-RELEASES).

source.title: Add audit log lag dashboard + alerts and wire runbook links (ENG-4824)
source.author: Allison Grant
source.description:
Context: Support + CS escalations (notably around audit log delivery latency) highlighted that we lacked a first-class, Support/CS-friendly view of end-to-end audit log pipeline lag and an alert that aligns to enterprise expectations. This PR adds a dedicated dashboard and alerting rules for audit log lag, and ensures panels/alerts link to a canonical runbook. It is intended to reduce time-to-triage during tickets like SUP-1846 and to support the known-issues sprint playbook work by providing an authoritative signal.

What’s included: (1) New Grafana dashboard: "Audit Logs — Delivery Lag" with panels for ingestion → transform → delivery stages, p50/p95/p99 lag, backlog depth (where available), per-region breakdown, and a quick "is this customer-impacting" section (current lag vs expected SLO). (2) New Prometheus alerts: audit-log-lag warning + critical, scoped to production namespaces and grouped by region, with annotations that link directly to the runbook and dashboard. (3) Runbook updates: new section for audit log lag triage, plus explicit "Support-facing guidance" (what we can safely confirm, what data we should avoid sharing, and what mitigation knobs exist). (4) Routing/ownership: alerts are routed to SRE on-call; annotations include escalation guidance for Platform ownership boundaries.

Alert thresholds (aligned to enterprise expectations): - Warning: p95 delivery lag > 10m for 10m (per region) - Critical: p95 delivery lag > 30m for 10m (per region) Notes: These thresholds intentionally do not page on transient spikes; they aim to catch sustained backlog that will breach enterprise expectations. Runbook describes how to validate whether this is isolated to a subset of tenants vs global/regional.

Key design choices: - Use p95 as the primary paging signal to avoid single-tenant tail behavior dominating. Dashboard still shows p99 for investigation. - Region grouping matches how Support/CS communicate impact (us-east/eu-west/etc.) and how status events are typically framed. - Runbook contains customer-safe language; no tenant identifiers or customer names are present in dashboard queries.

Testing/validation: - Verified dashboard JSON imports cleanly into staging Grafana and renders for both "prod" and "staging" datasources. - Verified alert rule evaluation against a replayed backlog scenario (staging) triggers warning/critical as expected and resolves cleanly. - Verified links in alert annotations resolve (runbook, dashboard, and on-call channel).

Rollout: - Merge to main + apply via standard observability-pack deploy pipeline (next scheduled deploy window). - SRE on-call to monitor for noisy alerts during first 48 hours; if noisy, adjust the for-duration before adjusting thresholds.

Screenshots: Included in PR checks artifacts (Grafana render export) for quick review.

Follow-ups (out of scope for this PR): - Add a dedicated metric for per-tenant lag that can be surfaced only in restricted dashboards (requires security review). - Add an explicit "expected delivery SLO" panel driven by tier metadata (would require joining tier labels into metrics).
commits: observability: add audit log lag dashboard JSON + folder wiring
alerts: add audit_log_delivery_lag warning/critical rules with runbook annotations
runbooks: document audit log lag triage + support-safe guidance
ci: fix dashboard linter failures and normalize panel UIDs
review_conversation: Rafael Mendes: Can we avoid paging on p99? We’ve seen single-tenant tail behavior create noise. Also suggest extending warning 'for' duration to 10m if we keep 10m threshold.
Allison Grant: Updated to page on p95 (p99 remains on dashboard) and set warning/critical to for: 10m. Added note in runbook on tail behavior and tenant-specific investigations.
Sean Gallagher: Please add a “Support/CS quick confirm” section at top of runbook (what to check first, where to link in SUP tickets). Also ensure alert annotations include dashboard link.
Allison Grant: Added "Quick confirm" section with exact steps + links and added dashboard_url annotation to both alerts.
Naomi Feldman: Confirming: no tenant/customer identifiers in dashboard queries? Also keep mitigation steps customer-safe (no internal hostnames).

Allison Grant: Confirmed queries are aggregate by region/cluster only; removed an example query that referenced internal pod labels, and rewrote mitigation steps to reference generic pipeline backlog actions + escalation to Platform.
Sean Gallagher: LGTM after changes.
Rafael Mendes: Approved. Thanks for switching to p95; should be much less noisy.
Naomi Feldman: Approved with note: future per-tenant visibility must go through restricted dashboards.
source.merge_outcome: Merged (squash) by Sean Gallagher into main; deploy scheduled for next observability-pack release window.

source.title: Add pseudonymous token-cost summarizer and privacy-hash bridge
source.author: Priya Mehta
source.body: Context/Motivation: We need per-invocation token cost visibility without retaining plaintext prompt or response tokens in observability backends. This PR introduces a privacy-first pipeline: (1) deterministic, salted token-hash bucketing at the edge; (2) a compact per-invocation cost summarizer that attributes per-bucket token counts and tokenized-costs; (3) a bridge service that joins hashed token buckets with model cost profiles for rollups and dashboards. Goals: provide accurate cost attribution for SLOs and billing heuristics while ensuring PII/PHI is never logged or exported. Changes are designed to be reversible (config toggles) and auditable. Main changes: - collector: add pseudonymization middleware that applies a per-tenant salt and BLAKE3-based chunked hashing to token fragments, exposing only bucket_id and hash_prefix_size metadata (no token text). - summarizer: a streaming aggregator that emits per-invocation compact summaries: total_tokens, generation_tokens, prompt_token_buckets (list of bucket_id:count), and model_cost_approx_usd (aggregated using model profile). - bridge service: lightweight joiner that maps bucket-level aggregates to model cost model to compute per-route and per-tenant cost metrics used by dashboards/alerts. - dashboards: add a new Observability Pack dashboard panel group 'Pseudonymous Cost Explorer' with per-route aggregated cost and a new 'quiet-surge' alert rule that triggers only when cost growth is sustained across at least 3 hours and >75th percentile of bucket skew. - telemetry: add metrics and labels for pseudonymization_version, salt_id, and summarizer_latency_ms; tracing spans annotated with sample_rate and anonymized_bucket_count. Backwards compatibility: collector can run in dual-mode emitting both legacy plaintext token telemetry (for fast migration) and pseudonymized summaries behind a feature flag. Checklist: [x] design review (ENG-4321), [x] privacy review with security, [x] unit tests for hashing and summarizer, [x] integration test with bridge, [x] dashboard screens, [x] docs draft. Migrations/deploy: - rollout plan: enable pseudonymization off-by-default; stage enable on a small subset of tenants (canary 1%), monitor summarizer latency and cost deltas for 48h, then ramp to 100% over 5 days. - rollback plan: flip feature flag to legacy mode (no schema migration required). - data retention: summarized rows kept 90d; raw hashed metadata kept 7d and then rotated. Security/privacy notes: salts are tenant-scoped and stored in KMS-backed vault; hashing performed in-process so unhashed tokens never leave the collector process. Commit summary: 1) add privacy middleware and deterministic token hashing; 2) streaming summarizer service and proto changes for compact summary; 3) bridge joiner service and query adapters for dashboards; 4) observability pack dashboard panels and alert rule; 5) e2e tests and runbook. Key files changed: observability-pack/pkg/collector/pseudonymizer.go, observability-pack/pkg/summarizer/stream_aggregator.go, observability-pack/services/bridge/joiner.go, observability-pack/dashboards/pseudonymous_cost_explorer.jsonnet, observability-pack/alerts/quiet_surge.yaml, docs/observability/privacy-token-summarizer.md, test/e2e/pseudonymizer_integration_test.go, scripts/bench/summarizer_bench.go, go.mod, go.sum. Tests: added unit tests for hash stability and collision resistance, integration test that validates summarizer output vs synthetic tokenized inputs, benchmark for 99th-pctl summarizer latency. Observability/Alerting: - New metric: pseudonymizer.tokens_hashed_total{tenant,salt_id} - New histogram: summarizer.latency_ms - New alert: observability.quiet_surge_high_cost (suppresses noisy short spikes; logic included in alert yaml) Release notes (short): Adds a privacy-preserving token-cost summarizer that allows cost attribution without storing token text; dashboards and alerts submitted with observability pack. Rollout and monitoring instructions included. Rationale and alternatives considered: we evaluated full differential privacy vs deterministic hashing; deterministic salted hashing was chosen for lower operational complexity and deterministic joins. We added salt rotation support as a safety mechanism. Open questions: - how to present per-bucket explanations to end-users without leaking token content? We opted for statistical bucket descriptors (e.g., average token length, token_type_entropy) rather than examples. - future: consider integrating cryptographic PPE techniques for smaller tenants. Observability runbook excerpt: On spike after enabling, check summarizer.latency_ms, look for high anonymized_bucket_count, and if latency > 200ms for 5m trigger rollback. Compliance: privacy review approved; KMS usage logged.

source.title: perf-canary: add paging (Sev2/Sev1) alert rules for sustained per-model regressions
source.author: Allison Grant
source.description: Summary: This adds paging-tier perf-canary alert rules (Sev2 + Sev1) for sustained regressions on top models. These are intentionally stricter than the warning rules in pr-19179 (higher minimum sample size, longer sustain window, and stricter anti-flap) and route to the on-call rotation channel via incident-bot. Motivation: During phase-1 rollout we saw warning-level regressions that were actionable when they sustained (real tail-latency impact), but the existing rules were designed to be noisy-tolerant and non-paging. This PR implements the paging path that SRE approved in the paging policy thread and is required for rollout gates integration. Alerting model: We page only for top-model + sustained regressions where we have enough canary volume to reduce false positives, and we gate on a canary-health signal (telemetry freshness + request loop healthy) to avoid paging on broken canary. Key behaviors: 1) Severity levels: Sev2 (page) for sustained p95 latency regression OR sustained tokens/sec drop on top models; Sev1 (page) only if regression is severe and sustained across consecutive windows. 2) Attribution-friendly annotations: every alert includes model, model_version, runtime_build, kernel_tag (if present), region, hardware_sku, baseline id, observed vs baseline deltas, and deep links to the Grafana dashboard + runbook. 3) Anti-flap + dedupe: minimum sample size, sustain windows, and incident-bot dedupe keys based on (model, region, hardware_sku, signal, severity). 4) Routing: Sev2/Sev1 route to #eng-oncall and create an incident-bot thread tagged perf-canary; warning-level continues to #eng-runtime. Thresholds (initial defaults, tuned for us-east phase-1): p95_e2e_latency_ms regression: Sev2 if >= +15% over baseline for 20m AND samples >= 200 AND canary_health=ok; Sev1 if >= +30% for 30m AND samples >= 300 AND canary_health=ok. tokens_per_sec regression: Sev2 if <= -12% for 25m AND samples >= 150; Sev1 if <= -20% for 35m AND samples >= 250. Note: These are defaults; per-model overrides live in control-plane config and will be adjusted during threshold calibration. Rollout: These rules are initially scoped to top models only (via label matcher on canary_model_tier=top). They are disabled by default in non-prod; in prod they are deployed but will be set to "dry-run" (notify-only) for 48h in us-east until SRE sign-off, then flipped to paging. Testing/validation: - Unit: alert rule lint + promtool rules tests; - Integration: rendered rules in staging Grafana; - Manual: replayed recent canary time series (including known regression from SUP-3841) to confirm expected firing behavior and no false triggers on short spikes. Links: - Linear ENG-4878 (alerting policy + routing), ENG-4880 (phase-1 us-east rollout), PM-642 (rollout gates integration) - Runbook: confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert - Dashboard: grafana/perf-canary/per-model (added in pr-19174) - Prior art: pr-19179 (warning-level rules) Checklist: - [x] rules have sustain window + min sample size - [x] rules gated by canary health and telemetry freshness - [x] incident-bot routing + dedupe key added - [x] annotations include dashboard/runbook links - [x] promtool tests added - [x] reviewed by SRE for paging hygiene
commits: perf-canary/alerts: add sev2 paging rules for p95 latency + tokens/sec regressions
perf-canary/alerts: add sev1 rules (high-confidence severe sustained regressions)
incident-bot annotations: include perf canary dashboard + runbook deep links
anti-flap: add min-samples and sustain windows; gate on canary health
tests: promtool rule tests + snapshot of rendered rule groups
docs: update alert severity routing notes
source.review_conversation: Sean Gallagher (SRE): The sustain windows look reasonable, but I want a hard gate on canary_health + telemetry freshness. We had late/partial ingestion last week and I don't want pages for stale series. Also, please ensure routing is #eng-oncall only for paging; no broad channels. Allison Grant (author): Added canary_health==1 AND telemetry_fresh==1 matchers to all paging alerts, plus a "Canary unhealthy" separate warning rule (non-paging) so we still get signal when canary is broken. Routing is now incident-bot -> #eng-oncall only for Sev2/Sev1. Aisha Bello: Can we tighten the min sample size for tokens/sec? We saw variance on low volume models in staging. Also, please include baseline id + observed/baseline numeric values in annotations, not just percent. Allison Grant: Updated tokens/sec minsamples to 150 (Sev2) / 250 (Sev1) and added explicit observed_tps, baseline_tps, delta_tps_pct, and baseline_artifact fields to annotations. Rafael Mendes: Dedupe key needs to include severity, otherwise Sev1 will get deduped under Sev2 and we won't see escalation. Also confirm the incident-bot handler uses the same key. Allison Grant: Updated annotation `incident_dedupe_key` to include severity and signal; verified incident-bot expects that key (see link in description). Logan Wright: Please avoid including high-cardinality labels in the alert grouping. I see model_version and runtime_build in annotations (fine) but they shouldn't be in `group_by`. Allison Grant: Ack—removed model_version/runtime_build from group_by; kept in annotations only. Sean Gallagher: Final ask: add a "burn-in" switch to run notify-only before paging. Allison Grant: Added `perf_canary_paging_enabled` feature flag label matcher; default false in prod values; releases can enable per-region via config. Approvals: Sean Gallagher approved. Aisha Bello approved. Rafael Mendes approved. Logan Wright approved.
source.release_notes: Adds production paging alerts (Sev2/Sev1) for sustained perf-canary regressions on top models, gated on canary health and tuned to reduce false positives. Routes paging to #eng-oncall via incident-bot; includes dashboard/runbook deep links and improved dedupe keys.

title: perf-canary: add paging (Sev2/Sev1) alert rules for sustained per-model regressions
preview: Summary: This adds paging-tier perf-canary alert rules (Sev2 + Sev1) for sustained regressions on top models. These are intentionally stricter than the warning rules in pr-19179 (higher minimum sample size, longer sustain window, and stricter anti-flap) and route to the on-call rotation channel via incident-bot. Motivation: During phase-1 rollout we saw warning-level regressions that were actionable when they sustained (real tail-latency impact), but the existing rules were designed to be noisy-tolerant and non-paging. This PR implements the paging path that SRE approved in the paging policy thread and is required for rollout gates integration. Alerting model: We page only for top-model + sustained regressions where we have enough canary volume to reduce false positives, and we gate on a canary-health signal (telemetry freshness + request loop healthy) to avoid paging on broken canary. Key behaviors: 1) Severity levels: Sev2 (page) for sustained p95 latency regression OR sustained tokens/sec drop on top models; Sev1 (page) only if regression is severe and sustained across consecutive windows. 2) Attribution-friendly annotations: every alert includes model, model_version, runtime_build, kernel_tag (if present), region, hardware_sku, baseline id, observed vs baseline deltas, and deep links to the Grafana dashboard + runbook. 3) Anti-flap + dedupe: minimum sample size, sustain windows, and incident-bot dedupe keys based on (model, region, hardware_sku, signal, severity). 4) Routing: Sev2/Sev1 route to #eng-oncall and create an incident-bot thread tagged perf-canary; warning-level continues to #eng-runtime. Thresholds (initial defaults, tuned for us-east phase-1): p95_e2e_latency_ms regression: Sev2 if >= +15% over baseline for 20m AND samples >= 200 AND canary_health=ok; Sev1 if >= +30% for 30m AND samples >= 300 AND canary_health=ok. tokens_per_sec regression: Sev2 if <= -12% for 25m AND samples >= 150; Sev1 if <= -20% for 35m AND samples >= 250. Note: These are defaults; per-model overrides live in control-plane config and will be adjusted during threshold calibration. Rollout: These rules are initially scoped to top models only (via label matcher on canary_model_tier=top). They are disabled by default in non-prod; in prod they are deployed but will be set to "dry-run" (notify-only) for 48h in us-east until SRE sign-off, then flipped to paging. Testing/validation: - Unit: alert rule lint + promtool rules tests; - Integration: rendered rules in staging Grafana; - Manual: replayed recent canary time series (including known regression from SUP-3841) to confirm expected firing behavior and no false triggers on short spikes. Links: - Linear ENG-4878 (alerting policy + routing), ENG-4880 (phase-1 us-east rollout), PM-642 (rollout gates integration) - Runbook: confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert - Dashboard: grafana/perf-canary/per-model (added in pr-19174) - Prior art: pr-19179 (warning-level rules) Checklist: - [x] rules have sustain window + min sample size - [x] rules gated by canary health and telemetry freshness - [x] incident-bot routing + dedupe key added - [x] annotations include dashboard/runbook links - [x] promtool tests added - [x] reviewed by SRE for paging hygiene
