source.title: Add dashboard panels for hardware profile selection + tail latency regressions
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.release_notes: Dashboards now segment latency/throughput by selected hardware profile (name/version/source) and highlight p95/p99 regressions during staged rollouts.
source.description:
Context: As we roll out the hardware tuning profile pack (H100/L40S first; B200 gated), oncall and perf owners need to answer two questions quickly: (1) which profile was actually selected (and why), and (2) did enabling the profile cause a tail-latency regression in a specific pool/region/model/workload slice. This PR updates our Grafana dashboards in observability-pack to add segmentation by `hardware_profile` (selected profile name + version) and `hardware_profile_source` (fleet default vs per-pool vs per-deployment override), and adds focused panels for tail behavior (p95/p99) with rollout-friendly breakdowns.

What’s included (high level): Adds a new dashboard row “Hardware profile selection” to the runtime/inference overview dashboard, showing selected profile distribution, profile source distribution, and a table of “top offenders” (pool/region/model) where p95/p99 is above thresholds while profile is enabled. Adds a dedicated “Rollout: Hardware Profiles” dashboard intended for staged rollouts with side-by-side comparison panels (profile-enabled vs profile-disabled) and a ‘regression highlight’ view for p95 TTFT and end-to-end latency. Updates existing tail-latency panels to include profile labels in legends and to prefer quantile histograms (vs gauge-like summaries) to reduce false confidence during canaries.

Dependencies/assumptions: This PR assumes the runtime exports the following labels on request latency and throughput metrics (added in runtime PRs referenced from the hardware profiles project): `hardware_profile`, `hardware_profile_version`, `hardware_profile_source` and that they are present on `redwood_inference_request_latency_seconds_bucket` (and the corresponding `*_count`/`*_sum`) and `redwood_inference_tokens_generated_total`. If a pool is running an older runtime (no labels), panels will still render but will show `hardware_profile="unknown"` via label_replace fallback. The queries are written to degrade gracefully.

Key dashboard changes:
- Runtime / Inference Overview:
  - New row: “Hardware profile selection”
    - Panel: Selected profile distribution (time series, stacked) by `hardware_profile`.
    - Panel: Override source distribution (time series, stacked) by `hardware_profile_source`.
    - Panel: “Top p99 latency (profile-enabled)” table filtered to `hardware_profile_source!=none` with columns (pool, region, model, profile, p99, req/s).
  - Existing tail latency panels updated:
    - Legends now include `{hardware_profile}/{hardware_profile_source}`.
    - Added a dashboard variable `profile` (regex/all) and `profile_source` for quick filtering during incidents.

- New dashboard: “Rollout: Hardware Profiles (SRE/Perf)”
  - Variables: pool, region, model, gpu_sku, profile, profile_version, profile_source.
  - Panels:
    - p50/p95/p99 end-to-end latency (histogram_quantile) split by profile.
    - TTFT (time-to-first-token) p95/p99 split by profile.
    - Tokens/sec (rate of tokens) split by profile.
    - Queue wait p95 (if present) split by profile.
    - “Regression highlight” stat: ratio of p95 (profile-enabled / profile-disabled) over a selectable window.
  - Notes panel: operational guidance + links to the rollout runbook and perf-canary gates.

PromQL notes (representative snippets):
- End-to-end p95 latency by profile:
  - histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, hardware_profile, hardware_profile_source) (rate(redwood_inference_request_latency_seconds_bucket{route=~"$route",pool=~"$pool",region=~"$region",model=~"$model"}[$__rate_interval])))
- TTFT p99 by profile:
  - histogram_quantile(0.99, sum by (le, hardware_profile) (rate(redwood_inference_ttft_seconds_bucket{pool=~"$pool",region=~"$region",model=~"$model"}[$__rate_interval])))
- Tokens/sec by profile:
  - sum by (hardware_profile) (rate(redwood_inference_tokens_generated_total{pool=~"$pool",region=~"$region",model=~"$model"}[$__rate_interval]))

Operational intent: During canary rollouts, SREs should filter to the canary pool + region and compare ‘profile-enabled’ vs ‘unknown/none’. The regression panels are meant to provide a fast signal, not a guarantee. We’re intentionally using p95/p99 and not just p50 because the profile pack can trade throughput for tail stability (or vice versa), and tail is what tends to page.

Out of scope: This PR does not change alert rules (Prometheus alerts) directly; it only adds/updates dashboards and documentation notes. Alert threshold tuning remains tracked under ENG-4828 and in the perf-canary repo.

Testing: Rendered dashboards locally via jsonnet, validated queries against staging Prometheus (us-east, H100 pool) and confirmed panels don’t error when `hardware_profile` labels are missing. Also verified that label cardinality remains within acceptable bounds by keeping profile/version labels on a small set of dashboards rather than globally duplicating panels.

Links: Linear ENG-4828/4829. Related runtime metrics: redwood PR-22027 (profile selection metrics) and perf-canary PR-731 (gates). Support context: SUP-29410 (customer-reported latency spike correlated with config rollout).
commits: dashboards: add hardware profile selection row to runtime overview
dashboards: new rollout dashboard for hardware profiles (SRE/perf)
promql: add label fallback for unknown profile labels
grafana: add variables for profile/version/source and clean up legends
docs: add dashboard notes + links to rollout runbook
source.review_conversation:
Allison Grant: “Can we avoid exploding cardinality by including profile_version in every legend? I’m worried this will make graphs unusable during frequent pack iterations.” Aisha Bello: “Agreed. I moved version into a variable + tooltip/table columns; main time series legends now default to profile + source only. Version is available via drilldown panels.”

Sean Gallagher: “For oncall, I want a single panel that answers: ‘did enabling profile change p95 TTFT in this pool right now?’ The ratio stat is good but please add clear wording and a link to the rollout runbook.” Aisha Bello: “Added ‘Regression highlight (p95 TTFT ratio)’ with thresholds guidance and a notes panel linking to the rollout runbook + perf-canary gate doc.”

Kira Volkov: “Query uses $__rate_interval but our canary windows are short; can you ensure the panel doesn’t get too noisy at 5m?” Aisha Bello: “Set min interval to 30s and used a 10m default window for ratio; left overrideable via dashboard time picker.”

Paula Mendes: “Please make sure we can filter by gpu_sku for mixed pools and multi-vendor SKU strings.” Aisha Bello: “Added gpu_sku variable as optional; if the label is missing the dashboard behaves normally. We can refine once infra normalizes the label names.”

Final approvals: Allison Grant approved after legend/version change; Sean Gallagher approved after runbook link + panel wording; Kira Volkov approved after interval/noise tweaks; Paula Mendes approved after gpu_sku variable.
source.merge_outcome: Merged into main; dashboards published via standard observability-pack sync job to Grafana folders (Runtime, Rollouts).

title: Add dashboard panels for hardware profile selection + tail latency regressions
preview:
Context: As we roll out the hardware tuning profile pack (H100/L40S first; B200 gated), oncall and perf owners need to answer two questions quickly: (1) which profile was actually selected (and why), and (2) did enabling the profile cause a tail-latency regression in a specific pool/region/model/workload slice. This PR updates our Grafana dashboards in observability-pack to add segmentation by `hardware_profile` (selected profile name + version) and `hardware_profile_source` (fleet default vs per-pool vs per-deployment override), and adds focused panels for tail behavior (p95/p99) with rollout-friendly breakdowns.

What’s included (high level): Adds a new dashboard row “Hardware profile selection” to the runtime/inference overview dashboard, showing selected profile distribution, profile source distribution, and a table of “top offenders” (pool/region/model) where p95/p99 is above thresholds while profile is enabled. Adds a dedicated “Rollout: Hardware Profiles” dashboard intended for staged rollouts with side-by-side comparison panels (profile-enabled vs profile-disabled) and a ‘regression highlight’ view for p95 TTFT and end-to-end latency. Updates existing tail-latency panels to include profile labels in legends and to prefer quantile histograms (vs gauge-like summaries) to reduce false confidence during canaries.

Dependencies/assumptions: This PR assumes the runtime exports the following labels on request latency and throughput metrics (added in runtime PRs referenced from the hardware profiles project): `hardware_profile`, `hardware_profile_version`, `hardware_profile_source` and that they are present on `redwood_inference_request_latency_seconds_bucket` (and the corresponding `*_count`/`*_sum`) and `redwood_inference_tokens_generated_total`. If a pool is running an older runtime (no labels), panels will still render but will show `hardware_profile="unknown"` via label_replace fallback. The queries are written to degrade gracefully.

Key dashboard changes:
- Runtime / Inference Overview:
  - New row: “Hardware profile selection”
    - Panel: Selected profile distribution (time series, stacked) by `hardware_profile`.
    - Panel: Override source distribution (time series, stacked) by `hardware_profile_source`.
    - Panel: “Top p99 latency (profile-enabled)” table filtered to `hardware_profile_source!=none` with columns (pool, region, model, profile, p99, req/s).
  - Existing tail latency panels updated:
    - Legends now include `{hardware_profile}/{hardware_profile_source}`.
    - Added a dashboard variable `profile` (regex/all) and `profile_source` for quick filtering during incidents.

- New dashboard: “Rollout: Hardware Profiles (SRE/Perf)”
  - Variables: pool, region, model, gpu_sku, profile, profile_version, profile_source.
  - Panels:
    - p50/p95/p99 end-to-end latency (histogram_quantile) split by profile.
    - TTFT (time-to-first-token) p95/p99 split by profile.
    - Tokens/sec (rate of tokens) split by profile.
    - Queue wait p95 (if present) split by profile.
    - “Regression highlight” stat: ratio of p95 (profile-enabled / profile-disabled) over a selectable window.
  - Notes panel: operational guidance + links to the rollout runbook and perf-canary gates.

PromQL notes (representative snippets):
- End-to-end p95 latency by profile:
  - histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, hardware_profile, hardware_profile_source) (rate(redwood_inference_request_latency_seconds_bucket{route=~"$route",pool=~"$pool",region=~"$region",model=~"$model"}[$__rate_interval])))
- TTFT p99 by profile:
  - histogram_quantile(0.99, sum by (le, hardware_profile) (rate(redwood_inference_ttft_seconds_bucket{pool=~"$pool",region=~"$region",model=~"$model"}[$__rate_interval])))
- Tokens/sec by profile:
  - sum by (hardware_profile) (rate(redwood_inference_tokens_generated_total{pool=~"$pool",region=~"$region",model=~"$model"}[$__rate_interval]))

Operational intent: During canary rollouts, SREs should filter to the canary pool + region and compare ‘profile-enabled’ vs ‘unknown/none’. The regression panels are meant to provide a fast signal, not a guarantee. We’re intentionally using p95/p99 and not just p50 because the profile pack can trade throughput for tail stability (or vice versa), and tail is what tends to page.

Out of scope: This PR does not change alert rules (Prometheus alerts) directly; it only adds/updates dashboards and documentation notes. Alert threshold tuning remains tracked under ENG-4828 and in the perf-canary repo.

Testing: Rendered dashboards locally via jsonnet, validated queries against staging Prometheus (us-east, H100 pool) and confirmed panels don’t error when `hardware_profile` labels are missing. Also verified that label cardinality remains within acceptable bounds by keeping profile/version labels on a small set of dashboards rather than globally duplicating panels.

Links: Linear ENG-4828/4829. Related runtime metrics: redwood PR-22027 (profile selection metrics) and perf-canary PR-731 (gates). Support context: SUP-29410 (customer-reported latency spike correlated with config rollout).

source.title: Anti-flap logic (windows + hysteresis) for status-component health signals
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.description: Motivation: Our current component health rollups (used to recommend public status changes and to populate status-page dashboards) are too twitchy. Short-lived spikes (e.g., 2–3 minutes of elevated 5xx or a brief p95 regression) can flip a component between Operational ↔ Degraded multiple times, which creates noisy on-call workflows and undermines customer trust in the status page. This PR implements anti-flap windows and hysteresis in the status-signal evaluator so we only transition component state when a condition persists long enough, and we require a healthier condition to recover than the one that caused the degradation. Summary of changes: Adds a small state machine around each status signal (errors + latency) with configurable (1) trigger window (min duration / min samples) and (2) recovery window (min duration / min samples) plus (3) hysteresis thresholds (degrade_threshold vs recover_threshold). Also adds per-component overrides and emits evaluator metrics so we can audit how often anti-flap prevented a transition. Behavior details: Degrade requires threshold breach for N consecutive evaluations (default 3 of 3 at 1m cadence) OR for a minimum time window (default 5m) depending on signal type; recover requires the metric to be back under a stricter recovery threshold for a longer window (default 10m). A note on semantics: this is intentionally separate from SLO burn alerts; we’re smoothing the customer-facing component health classification, not masking genuine outages. Rollout plan: guarded behind status_signals.anti_flap_enabled (default off). In dry-run mode we compute both old/new state and publish a diff metric (status_signals_state_diff_total{component_id,signal_type}) to validate no surprises. After 1 week of dry-run we’ll enable for Hosted API components in us-east + eu-west, then expand. Docs: Updated README in the pack and added inline docs for config knobs and default values. Testing: Adds unit tests for threshold crossing, jitter, and recovery hysteresis; adds a regression test that reproduces the us-east streaming ‘toggle’ pattern from early Nov. Related: Status page transparency upgrades project; aligns with internal standard for customer-facing status signals (anti-flap guidance + thresholds).
commits: status-signals: introduce HealthStateEvaluator with hysteresis thresholds
config: add anti-flap knobs + per-component overrides
metrics: emit evaluator transition counters + diff metrics for dry-run
tests: add jitter/regression coverage + golden cases for windowing
docs: update observability-pack README with anti-flap guidance
conversation: Allison Grant (review): Can you clarify the exact transition rules? Specifically, do we require consecutive violations or just X out of Y in the trigger window? Also please confirm we’re not hiding real outages by making recovery too slow.

Aisha Bello (author): Updated description and docs. We currently do consecutive-by-default (3/3 at 1m) for error-rate signals to avoid ‘every other minute’ oscillation; window-based mode is available and used for some latency signals where sampling is noisier. Recovery is intentionally longer (10m default) and uses a stricter threshold (recover_threshold < degrade_threshold). This should reduce toggles without delaying initial incident declaration because degrade window is still short.

Rafael Mendes (review): Two requests: (1) add an explicit metric for “anti-flap prevented transition” so we can measure impact during rollout; (2) make sure per-component overrides exist because Streaming tends to be spikier than Embeddings and we’ll want different defaults.

Aisha Bello (author): Added status_signals_transition_suppressed_total{component_id,signal_type,reason} and wired per-component overrides via config. Defaults are still global but overridden by component_id where needed.

Christine Wong (review): Please ensure component_id labeling matches the status component taxonomy (exact string IDs). I’ve seen mismatches when folks use display names. Also would like a quick snippet in the README showing a typical override.

Aisha Bello (author): Good catch. Enforced component_id validation against our registry keys at config load (fails fast). Added README snippet: components.us-east.streaming.errors.degrade_window=5m / recover_window=15m etc.
Logan Wright (review): Any chance this breaks existing dashboards expecting the old gauge values? Also, do we have backward compatibility for the emitted series names?

Aisha Bello (author): Kept existing series. New metrics are additive (suffix _suppressed_total and _state_diff_total). Existing dashboard queries remain intact. I also added a panel behind a toggle to visualize diff during dry-run.
Caleb Johnson (review): The regression test looks good. One nit: windowing.ts uses Date.now() directly; can we inject a clock for deterministic tests?
Aisha Bello (author): Refactored evaluator to accept a Clock interface in tests; prod uses SystemClock. Tests now deterministic.
Allison Grant (final): Approved. Please coordinate rollout owners; I’d like us to run in dry-run for at least 5 business days and review diffs in the weekly status accuracy audit.
Aisha Bello (author): Sounds good. Will post rollout plan in #eng-sre and link to ENG-4841 checklist.
release_notes: Added anti-flap windowing + hysteresis for status-component health signal evaluation (behind config flag).
New Prometheus metrics for transition suppression and dry-run diffing to support safe rollout.
Expanded unit/regression coverage for jittery error/latency patterns that previously caused component status toggling.

source.title: Link SLO burn alerts to status page update runbook + component taxonomy
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.description: Context: As part of the status page transparency upgrades, on-call needs a fast, consistent path from a page to (a) the correct status-page component taxonomy and (b) the exact runbook steps for posting/updating component statuses. Today, most of our burn-rate alerts include a generic runbook link (or no runbook at all), and nothing points to the canonical taxonomy/mapping guidance. This PR updates alert annotations across component-level SLO burn alerts in this repo to include: (1) a status page update runbook link, and (2) a status page component taxonomy link. It also standardizes annotation keys so incident-bot and human responders can reliably surface the right links in Slack. Scope: alert rule annotations + lint/test updates only; no threshold or routing changes. Notes: we intentionally keep the links to high-level canonical docs rather than incident-specific pages, and we avoid language that implies an SLA/guarantee. Rollout: safe to merge; annotations-only; validated in staging alertmanager and via jsonnet render output. Follow-ups: once merged, we can have incident-bot surface these fields automatically in the intake + reminders (tracked elsewhere).
commits: alerts: add statuspage runbook + taxonomy annotations to component burn alerts
alerts: standardize annotation keys (runbook_url, statuspage_runbook_url, taxonomy_url)
lint/tests: update golden renders for rule output
changed_files: alerts/slo/hosted_api_rules.libsonnet (propagate taxonomy link per component group)
alerts/slo/streaming_rules.libsonnet (add statuspage runbook link; align naming)
alerts/slo/embeddings_rules.libsonnet (add taxonomy link; keep existing runbook_url)
docs/alerting/annotation-keys.md (document new keys and intended usage)
ci/jsonnet-lint.sh (minor: fail on missing required annotation keys)
review_conversation: Allison Grant (review): Can we keep the annotation key names consistent with what incident-bot already parses? I see `taxonomy_url` and `statuspage_runbook_url` introduced here; do we want `statuspage_taxonomy_url` for symmetry?
Aisha Bello (author): Good call. I updated to `statuspage_taxonomy_url` and kept `statuspage_runbook_url`. Also left existing `runbook_url` untouched for backwards compatibility.
Rafael Mendes (review): Please ensure the links point to the canonical Confluence pages, not the working notes, and avoid any language that implies we always update within X minutes.
Aisha Bello (author): Updated links to the canonical runbook + taxonomy pages and removed the 'within 15m' note from docs. No timing language in alert annotations.

Christine Wong (review): Can we add the taxonomy link to the alerts that page for 'Hosted API (all regions)'? On-call tends to pick that alert first, and the taxonomy is where they find the regional component breakdown.
Aisha Bello (author): Added `statuspage_taxonomy_url` to the aggregate Hosted API burn alerts as well. Golden renders updated.
Caleb Johnson (review): Minor: Please keep annotation ordering stable to reduce churn in future diffs.
Aisha Bello (author): Sorted annotation keys consistently in the helper and regenerated golden files.
Allison Grant (approval): LGTM after the key rename + ordering changes.
Rafael Mendes (approval): Approved. Thanks for keeping this annotation-only.
source.release_notes: Alert annotations now include status-page update runbook + component taxonomy links to speed up responder workflows during incidents.

source.title: Add overload shedding dashboards + initial alert rules (breaker opens, shed rate)
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.description: Context: As part of the Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding reliability program, we need first-class visibility into (a) when we are shedding traffic vs failing implicitly, and (b) when breakers are opening/half-opening/closing across regions/models/tiers. This PR adds Grafana dashboards and initial Prometheus alert rules for overload protection signals. Scope: (1) Dashboards: Add "Hosted API • Overload Protection" dashboard group with panels for shed rate, breaker open rate, admission decisions, runtime saturation proxies (queue depth / token saturation / memory pressure proxy), and top shed reasons. Includes recommended drilldowns by region and route family, and a safe set of labels (bounded cardinality). (2) Alerts: Add first-pass paging + ticket alerts for (i) sustained breaker-open rate, (ii) elevated shed rate for paid tiers, (iii) Retry-After header missing on overload responses (via gateway metric), and (iv) mismatch between shed and runtime saturation (possible false positive / bad thresholds). Each alert includes runbook links and suggested next actions. (3) Runbook links: Links to oncall runbook and runtime mitigation guide; includes incident-bot channel references. Notes/guardrails: We intentionally avoid tenant_id and request_id as dimensions. Model is aggregated to a small allowlist via label mapping in the gateway metrics exporter. Dashboards default to last 1h and show p95/p99 lat overlay to correlate shedding with tail latency improvements. Follow-ups: (a) tighten alert thresholds after canary (false positives), (b) add per-tier SLO burn overlays once SLO toolkit classification is rolled out everywhere, (c) add a "top fallback attempts" panel after routing PR-18458 is fully deployed. Screenshots: See internal preview link in the Grafana folder (can be rendered in CI). Runbooks: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ONCALL/Hosted+Overload+Circuit+Breaker+Runbook and https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SERVING/Runtime+Overload+Mitigation. Testing: `make lint`, `make test`, `make dashboards-validate`, `make prom-rules-test`.
commits: dashboards: add Hosted API overload protection dashboard (shed + breaker panels)
alerts: add overload shedding alert group with runbook annotations
metrics: standardize label mapping for route_family/model_family in dashboard queries
docs: add dashboard README with oncall usage notes and links
ci: validate grafana jsonnet compilation and promtool rule tests
conversation: Rafael Mendes (review): Dashboard looks good. Two asks: (1) can we add an explicit panel for breaker state transitions (open->half-open->closed) rather than just open_rate? (2) alert thresholds: shed_rate > 2% for 10m might be noisy during canary; can we gate paging to enterprise tiers only and create a ticket-level alert for everyone else?

Aisha Bello (author): Added a "Breaker state transitions" panel using `increase(breaker_state_changes_total{to_state!="closed"}[5m])` and split alerts: paging only when `tier in ("enterprise_hosted","dedicated")` and 10m sustained; ticket-level for self-serve at a higher threshold. Also added annotations for recommended mitigation toggles + links to the runbook section anchors.

Allison Grant (review): Thanks. Please ensure we are not accidentally querying on high-cardinality labels. I see `model` in a few panels—can we switch to `model_family` everywhere and add a note in the README about why?
Aisha Bello (author): Updated queries to use `model_family` + `route_family` only. Added a README note and a small helper in `overload.libsonnet` so future panels inherit the same grouping.

Logan Wright (review): Alert 3 (Retry-After missing) is great, but what's the source metric? If it depends on a new gateway metric, can we include a fallback so the rule doesn't flap in regions where the metric isn't deployed yet?

Aisha Bello (author): Good call. Added `absent()` guard + `for: 30m` and scoped it to regions where `gateway_overload_retry_after_total` is present. Also added a note that this becomes authoritative after gateway PR-18472 reaches 100%.

Christine Wong (review): Can we add a panel that overlays p99 latency and shed_rate so oncall can see if shedding is actually helping? Also please link to the incident-bot breaker announcements in the runbook section.
Aisha Bello (author): Added overlay panel (p99 latency + shed_rate) and included incident-bot channel references in README + alert annotations (`#incidents` / `#eng-oncall`).
Peter Holtz (review): One minor: recording rule name prefix should match existing `hosted_api:` convention. Also the alert group should be `hosted-api-overload` (hyphenated) per standards.
Aisha Bello (author): Renamed recording rules to `hosted_api:overload_*` and updated group name. Rebased to resolve conflicts with a parallel alerts PR.
Rafael Mendes (final): Approved. Please coordinate rollout with Connor on canary gating; otherwise LGTM.
Aisha Bello (author): Coordinated with Connor; we’ll enable the dashboards immediately and keep paging alerts disabled by default behind the standard alert routing flag until canary week.
source.release_notes: New Grafana dashboards for Hosted API overload protection (shed + breaker visibility) and initial alert rules with runbook links. Paging alerts default to disabled in routing until canary gates are met.

title: Add overload shedding dashboards + initial alert rules (breaker opens, shed rate)
preview: Context: As part of the Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding reliability program, we need first-class visibility into (a) when we are shedding traffic vs failing implicitly, and (b) when breakers are opening/half-opening/closing across regions/models/tiers. This PR adds Grafana dashboards and initial Prometheus alert rules for overload protection signals. Scope: (1) Dashboards: Add "Hosted API • Overload Protection" dashboard group with panels for shed rate, breaker open rate, admission decisions, runtime saturation proxies (queue depth / token saturation / memory pressure proxy), and top shed reasons. Includes recommended drilldowns by region and route family, and a safe set of labels (bounded cardinality). (2) Alerts: Add first-pass paging + ticket alerts for (i) sustained breaker-open rate, (ii) elevated shed rate for paid tiers, (iii) Retry-After header missing on overload responses (via gateway metric), and (iv) mismatch between shed and runtime saturation (possible false positive / bad thresholds). Each alert includes runbook links and suggested next actions. (3) Runbook links: Links to oncall runbook and runtime mitigation guide; includes incident-bot channel references. Notes/guardrails: We intentionally avoid tenant_id and request_id as dimensions. Model is aggregated to a small allowlist via label mapping in the gateway metrics exporter. Dashboards default to last 1h and show p95/p99 lat overlay to correlate shedding with tail latency improvements. Follow-ups: (a) tighten alert thresholds after canary (false positives), (b) add per-tier SLO burn overlays once SLO toolkit classification is rolled out everywhere, (c) add a "top fallback attempts" panel after routing PR-18458 is fully deployed. Screenshots: See internal preview link in the Grafana folder (can be rendered in CI). Runbooks: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ONCALL/Hosted+Overload+Circuit+Breaker+Runbook and https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SERVING/Runtime+Overload+Mitigation. Testing: `make lint`, `make test`, `make dashboards-validate`, `make prom-rules-test`.

source.title: Add component-level SLO dashboards + standard links for status page components
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.description: Context: As part of the Status Page Transparency Upgrades initiative, on-call needs a consistent, component-level view of latency + error impact that matches the public status component taxonomy (component_id x region x capability). Today dashboards are a mix of service names (gateway/scheduler/streamer), inconsistent region labels, and ad-hoc links in runbooks. This PR adds a standardized dashboard set per status component and a link registry so alerts/runbooks/status tooling can point to the same canonical URLs. What’s in this PR: (1) New Grafana dashboards for each public status component, split into latency + errors with consistent panels and templating. (2) Standard naming conventions for dashboards, folders, variables, and panel titles (to reduce “is this the right chart?” time during Sev1). (3) A generated link map (component_id/region/capability -> dashboard URL) committed under observability-pack so it can be referenced from alert annotations and the status-page update runbook. (4) Basic validation to ensure every active component in the registry has a dashboard link entry (fails CI if missing). Scope/Components: Hosted API (chat/completions), Streaming, Embeddings, Reranking, Console (incident surfacing + status endpoints), plus region views for us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1. Notes: Dedicated/Private are intentionally excluded from the public-facing component dashboard set in this PR; we can add internal-only dashboards later under a separate folder with restricted perms. Naming convention: Folder = "Status Components"; Dashboard title = "Status Component: <Display Name> (<region>)"; UID = "statuscomp-<component_id>-<region>"; Variables include component_id, region, capability (where applicable), route_group. Panels standardize on p50/p95/p99 latency, error rate (5m/30m), request volume, saturation proxy (queue depth where available), and top routes contributing to errors. Validation/rollout: This is safe to merge behind the current component registry; new components will require link entries, but the check only enforces for components marked rollout_enabled=true. Follow-ups: The anti-flap logic PR will reference these dashboards in alert annotations; the status-page update runbook will also be updated to prefer the canonical links from this repo.
commits: 1c3f9dd grafana: add base template for status component dashboards (variables + standard panels)
c2a91a7 dashboards: add Hosted API + Streaming component dashboards (us-east-1/us-west-2/eu-west-1)
8b0e6a1 dashboards: add Embeddings + Reranking dashboards; align error queries to component tags
e6f1b58 registry: add status_component_dashboard_links.json + generator script
4c8c2b9 ci: validate every rollout_enabled component has a dashboard link entry
d2b7a24 docs: add README for naming conventions and how to add a new component dashboard
source.checks: build (node 20): pass; lint: pass; typecheck: pass; grafana jsonnet render: pass; validate component links: pass
source.conversation: Allison Grant (review): This is the right direction. Two asks: (a) can we standardize the variable names to match the public taxonomy exactly (component_id/region/capability) and (b) can we add a panel for “top contributing routes” so Comms Lead can sanity check impact scope before posting? Aisha Bello (author): Updated to use component_id/region/capability everywhere; added Top Routes panel based on route_group tags where present, and a fallback “top endpoint” query for gateway-only components. Rafael Mendes (review): Love the CI guardrail. One concern: the dashboards for Streaming are using gateway error rate; we need streaming disconnect/error counters when available, otherwise we’ll mark impact incorrectly. Can we split Streaming into its own error source query and label it accordingly? Aisha Bello (author): Good catch. Streaming dashboard now uses streaming_disconnect_rate + streaming_stream_errors when present; the panel header calls out the metric source and includes a note when falling back to gateway 5xx (only for regions that don’t have the new metric yet). Christine Wong (review): Please align the dashboard titles to what we’ll show on the status page (e.g., “Streaming API” vs “Streaming”). Also can we ensure the UID is stable and won’t churn when display names change? Aisha Bello (author): Titles now include the public display name from the component registry; UID stays derived from component_id + region (display name changes won’t affect it). Mina Haddad (review): Region naming: can we use the canonical region ids (us-east-1) consistently and avoid “use1/usw2” abbreviations? Also, I’d like a per-region overview dashboard for quick triage. Aisha Bello (author): All dashboards standardized to canonical region ids. Added a “Status Components: Region Overview” dashboard with a table of key KPIs per component for the selected region. Allison Grant (final): Approved. Please add a short README section describing how to add a new component and how to fetch the canonical link from the registry. Aisha Bello (author): Added README/status-components-dashboards.md with steps + examples.
source.release_notes: Internal: Adds standardized Grafana dashboards and canonical dashboard links for each public status component (latency + errors), aligned to component_id/region/capability taxonomy.

source.title: Add JSON schema + CI validation for status component registry
source.author: Allison Grant
source.description: This PR introduces a JSON Schema for the public status-page component registry and adds validation in CI so registry changes fail fast when they are malformed or violate taxonomy rules. Motivation: we’re using the registry as a source-of-truth for component ids/display names/ownership and rollout flags as part of the Status page transparency upgrades effort; recent incidents and hotfixes showed that small typos (ids, missing fields, inconsistent region/capability labels) can cause misclassification and customer-facing status inaccuracies. What’s included: (1) New schema file `statuspage/component-registry.schema.json` that defines required fields and constraints for each component entry: `component_id` (kebab-case, stable, unique), `display_name` (human readable), `product_area` (hosted_api|streaming|embeddings|reranking|console|dedicated_control_plane), `regions` (allowed set), `capabilities` (allowed set), `ownership` (team + slack channel), and `rollout` flags (`public_enabled`, `console_link_enabled`, `auto_updates_enabled`) with defaults. (2) Validation script `scripts/validate-status-component-registry.ts` (AJV) that validates `statuspage/component-registry.json` against the schema and additionally enforces a few cross-field rules that JSON Schema can’t express cleanly (e.g., no duplicate display_name within the same product_area+region group, `public_enabled=false` components must not have `console_link_enabled=true`, and region list must be non-empty for region-scoped product areas). (3) CI wiring: adds a `statuspage-registry-validate` job to the existing checks and runs it on PRs that touch `statuspage/**`. (4) Developer ergonomics: `npm run validate:statuspage-registry` command, plus README updates describing how to add a new component safely and how to handle deprecated/renamed ids (no renames; add new id and mark old id `public_enabled=false` only after deprecation window). Notes: This does not change runtime/statuspage behavior directly; it only adds guardrails for future registry edits. Follow-ups will update incident-bot + gateway metrics tagging to rely on the validated ids (tracked in ENG-4822/ENG-4830).
commits: add schema for statuspage component registry
add ajv-based validation script and npm task
enforce cross-field rules (rollout flags + region scoping)
wire validation into CI and path filters
docs: registry editing guidance + deprecation rules
source.checks: CI: build (pass); unit tests (pass); lint (pass); statuspage-registry-validate (pass); typecheck (pass).
source.review_conversation: Rafael Mendes (review): Can we be stricter about `component_id` format? We’ve had camelCase creep in. Also please ensure we document the rename/deprecation policy since customers bookmark component names. Allison Grant (author): Added regex for kebab-case and clarified policy in README: ids are immutable; deprecate via `public_enabled=false` after 30d and keep mapping until downstream tooling is updated. Aisha Bello (review): Cross-field rule: if `public_enabled=false`, should we also require `auto_updates_enabled=false`? Otherwise the automation might try to post. Allison Grant (author): Good call; added rule and test coverage. Christine Wong (review): The allowed `regions` list should match the taxonomy doc; we currently include `ap-south-1` in some working notes but I don’t think it’s live. Suggest locking to the currently public regions only. Allison Grant (author): Updated allowed regions to `us-east`, `us-west`, `eu-west` per current public status page and added a comment on how to expand (schema update + comms review). Naomi Feldman (security/compliance): Ownership fields include Slack channels; ok to keep internal-only but make sure this repo isn’t mirrored publicly. Allison Grant (author): Confirmed internal-only; also added note in README that ownership is internal metadata. Final review: LGTM from Rafael/Aisha/Christine; Naomi approved with note.
source.release_notes: Internal-only: adds schema + CI validation for status component registry to reduce status-page mapping errors.

source.title: Add KMS latency/error dashboards + alert rules (per env/region)
source.author: Nadia Rahman
source.release_notes: Adds a KMS Dependency Health dashboard (latency/errors/throttling/circuit breaker) with per-environment and per-region views, plus recommended alert rules and runbook links for on-call triage.
source.description: Context: As part of the KMS integration hardening initiative we needed first-class dependency visibility for KMS across Hosted/Dedicated/Private paths. This PR adds a KMS Dependency Health dashboard and alert rules that align to the canonical error taxonomy (AccessDenied/NotFound/Throttling/Timeout/InvalidCiphertext/Unknown) and our metric namespacing conventions. Scope: (1) Dashboard: Overview + per env/region panels for request volume, latency (p50/p95/p99), error rate by category, throttling signals, and circuit breaker state; (2) Alerts: page-worthy vs ticket-worthy thresholds, with environment-aware routing (prod pages, staging tickets) and burn-rate style windows where appropriate; (3) Operator ergonomics: runbook links + log/trace pivot hints (trace_id, kms_key_id, provider, region, service); (4) Documentation: short README explaining how to use/tune the alerts and what to check first during an incident. Recommended thresholds (initial): Latency p95: warn >250ms for 10m, page >500ms for 5m (prod only); Timeout error rate: warn >0.25% for 10m, page >0.75% for 5m; Throttling: warn >0.5% for 10m, page >1.5% for 10m (with annotation to check retry storm/backpressure); AccessDenied: page on sustained >10/min for 5m (prod) because it often indicates mis-scoped IAM or a rollout regression; InvalidCiphertext: page on any sustained nonzero (>1/min for 5m) because it can indicate key mismatch/rotation cutover issues. Notes: thresholds are intentionally conservative and will be tuned post-rollout (see ENG-4828 follow-up and the alert noise discussion). This PR assumes the KMS metrics emitted by the runtime/control plane are available (redwood PR-18434 / PR-18515 / PR-18527) and uses the standardized tags: env, region, service, provider, error_category, error_code, kms_key_id (hashed).
commits: observability: add kms dependency health dashboard skeleton + templating
alerts: add kms latency/error/throttle rules with env-aware routing
docs: add runbook links + dashboard usage notes
chore: align metric queries with final namespacing decision (kms_client.*)
source.review_conversation: Allison Grant (review): Panels look good. Two asks: (1) please split prod vs non-prod by default variable so on-call lands on prod first; (2) use the canonical error_category labels (AccessDenied, NotFound, Throttling, Timeout, InvalidCiphertext, Unknown) and avoid mixing raw provider codes in legend. Nadia Rahman (author): Done. Default env=prod, added an env_group variable (prod vs non-prod) + updated legends to error_category; raw codes are still available in a drilldown table. Sean Gallagher (review): Alert thresholds might be noisy in staging. Can we ensure only prod routes to paging and everything else to ticket/Slack? Also link the decrypt failures runbook. Nadia Rahman (author): Updated notification policy: prod pages, staging/dev create tickets + Slack only. Added runbook link placeholder now pointing to the KMS decrypt failures runbook page. Kevin Osei (review): For AccessDenied, can we add a note to check recent IAM module bumps and include principal/key ARN hints? Also prefer adding annotation labels for linked linear + runbook. Nadia Rahman (author): Added alert annotations with runbook_url + linear_key=ENG-4828; expanded the AccessDenied alert description to call out IAM policy/role separation changes and to check audit logs for principal/key arn mismatch. Rafael Mendes (review): Please add a panel for circuit breaker open state + request shed/backpressure if present; this was a gap in the last KMS timeout spike. Nadia Rahman (author): Added circuit breaker open gauge and a timeseries for kms_client.backpressure_dropped_requests_total where available (panel auto-hides if metric missing). Final: Allison approved after the env default + label cleanup; Logan also approved after confirming query costs are reasonable.
timeline: 2025-10-22 Opened by Nadia Rahman; initial dashboard + alerts
2025-10-24 Requested changes from Allison/Sean; env defaults + routing
2025-10-27 CI failure: promtool-check-rules due to duplicate alert name; fixed + reran
2025-10-30 Added circuit breaker + backpressure panels; updated alert annotations

Recommended thresholds (initial): Latency p95: warn >250ms for 10m, page >500ms for 5m (prod only); Timeout error rate: warn >0.25% for 10m, page >0.75% for 5m; Throttling: warn >0.5% for 10m, page >1.5% for 10m (with annotation to check retry storm/backpressure); AccessDenied: page on sustained >10/min for 5m (prod) because it often indicates mis-scoped IAM or a rollout regression; InvalidCiphertext: page on any sustained nonzero (>1/min for 5m) because it can indicate key mismatch/rotation cutover issues. This PR assumes the KMS metrics emitted by the runtime/control plane are available (redwood PR-18434 / PR-18515 / PR-18527) and uses the standardized tags: env, region, service, provider, error_category, error_code, kms_key_id (hashed).
text: Context: As part of the KMS integration hardening initiative we needed first-class dependency visibility for KMS across Hosted/Dedicated/Private paths. This PR adds a KMS Dependency Health dashboard and alert rules that align to the canonical error taxonomy (AccessDenied/NotFound/Throttling/Timeout/InvalidCiphertext/Unknown) and our metric namespacing conventions. Scope: (1) Dashboard: Overview + per env/region panels for request volume, latency (p50/p95/p99), error rate by category, throttling signals, and circuit breaker state; (2) Alerts: page-worthy vs ticket-worthy thresholds, with environment-aware routing (prod pages, staging tickets) and burn-rate style windows where appropriate; (3) Operator ergonomics: runbook links + log/trace pivot hints (trace_id, kms_key_id, provider, region, service); (4) Documentation: short README explaining how to use/tune the alerts and what to check first during an incident. Recommended thresholds (initial): Latency p95: warn >250ms for 10m, page >500ms for 5m (prod only); Timeout error rate: warn >0.25% for 10m, page >0.75% for 5m; Throttling: warn >0.5% for 10m, page >1.5% for 10m (with annotation to check retry storm/backpressure); AccessDenied: page on sustained >10/min for 5m (prod) because it often indicates mis-scoped IAM or a rollout regression; InvalidCiphertext: page on any sustained nonzero (>1/min for 5m) because it can indicate key mismatch/rotation cutover issues. Notes: thresholds are intentionally conservative and will be tuned post-rollout (see ENG-4828 follow-up and the alert noise discussion). This PR assumes the KMS metrics emitted by the runtime/control plane are available (redwood PR-18434 / PR-18515 / PR-18527) and uses the standardized tags: env, region, service, provider, error_category, error_code, kms_key_id (hashed).

source.title: Dashboards: correlate model version change rate with rollout phases and incident/regression signals
source.author: Logan Wright
source.description: Context: As part of the model metadata schema + changelog generator rollout, we now emit structured model change events (promotions, rollbacks, metadata edits) and rollout phase transitions. Support and oncall repeatedly asked for a fast way to answer: “did this latency/regression coincide with a model version promotion or rollout phase change?” This PR adds Grafana panels + recording rules to correlate: (1) model version change rate, (2) rollout phase transitions (canary/A-B/GA/rollback), and (3) incident/regression signals (5xx, timeouts, p95/p99 latency, eval-guardrail failures when present). What’s included: - New dashboard: “Model Rollout Correlation” with panels for change rate over time, rollout phase annotations, and ‘before/after’ latency + error overlays. - Update existing “LLM Serving Overview” dashboard with a compact panel showing model version change rate by deployment_mode and model_name (top 10) plus a click-through to the correlation dashboard. - Prometheus recording rules to normalize and de-cardinalize model change counters for dashboards (avoid high-cardinality labels from raw events). - Standard annotations schema for rollout phase changes (Grafana annotations sourced from Loki logs) to visualize canary/A-B boundaries. Notes/assumptions: - This depends on control-plane emitting model_change and rollout_phase_change events (redwood PR #18435 / #18458). In environments where those logs/metrics aren’t present yet, panels will render ‘No data’ but won’t break dashboards. - We intentionally do not include per-customer/account identifiers in any metric labelset; correlation for account-specific cases should use trace IDs / audit tooling, not dashboards. - Private deployments: these dashboards assume the “control-plane-events” log stream is configured; otherwise skip the annotations panel per docs added here.
commits: Add recording rules for model change event counters (normalized label set)
Add Grafana dashboard: model rollout correlation
Add annotation query templates for rollout phase transitions
Wire model change rate panel into LLM Serving Overview
Docs: troubleshooting + rollout correlation playbook snippet
Chore: dashboard jsonnet formatting + lint fixes
source.implementation_notes: Metrics inputs: - counter: redwood_control_plane_model_change_events_total{action="promote"|"rollback"|"metadata_update", deployment_mode, model_name, model_version} (raw; high-cardinality due to version) - counter: redwood_control_plane_rollout_phase_events_total{phase, deployment_mode, model_name, rollout_id} (raw; rollout_id high-cardinality) This PR adds recording rules to collapse labels for dashboards: - redwood:model_change_events:rate5m{action, deployment_mode, model_name} = sum without(model_version,region,cluster) (rate(redwood_control_plane_model_change_events_total[5m])) - redwood:rollout_phase_transitions:rate5m{phase, deployment_mode, model_name} = sum without(rollout_id,region,cluster) (rate(redwood_control_plane_rollout_phase_events_total[5m])) Correlation dashboard panels: - “Model version change rate (promote/rollback)” time series, stacked by action - “Rollout phase transition rate” time series (canary/ab/ga) - “Errors & latency overlay” showing requests_error_rate and p95 latency for the same model_name + deployment_mode, with the above series as overlays - “Regression signals” panel: guardrail_fail_rate (if present) and ‘quality_regression_flag’ (0/1) when eval harness emits it (optional) Grafana annotations: - Source: Loki stream {app="control-plane", component="eventing"} | json | event_type in ("rollout_phase_change") - Annotation text includes: model_name, phase, rollout_id (NOT used as a metric label; only shown in the annotation tooltip).
source.testing: Local: jsonnet render + grafana-dashboard-linter, promtool check rules. Staging: validated dashboard renders against staging metrics (rollouts dogfood environment). Verified panels show ‘No data’ gracefully when model change metrics absent by toggling datasource to a sandbox Prometheus. Verified that label cardinality does not grow with model_version or rollout_id by inspecting series count for redwood:model_change_events:rate5m and redwood:rollout_phase_transitions:rate5m.
screenshots: Screenshot (staging): Model Rollout Correlation dashboard showing a promotion spike aligned with canary->A/B annotation and concurrent p95 increase.
Screenshot (staging): LLM Serving Overview new panel with top model change rates and link-out.
source.review_conversation: Jada Williams (requested changes): The change-rate panel is useful, but please ensure the recording rules strip model_version; otherwise dashboards will explode in cardinality for frequent promotions. Also can we align naming with the schema doc: ‘promote’ vs ‘promotion’? Logan Wright (author): Good catch. Updated rules to sum without(model_version, rollout_id) and standardized action values to promote/rollback/metadata_update in the rule expression + legend. Michael Nguyen (requested changes): For rollbacks, we should be able to distinguish “auto-fallback” vs “manual rollback” eventually. Not blocking, but can you at least leave a TODO in the dashboard and avoid encoding that as a label now. Logan Wright: Added a dashboard note + TODO in docs; no label added. Leila Farouk (QA): Can you add a snapshot test (jsonnet render diff) to prevent accidental dashboard churn? Logan Wright: Added a CI step that renders jsonnet and checks in the rendered output hash; also tightened formatting so diffs are stable. Dylan Brooks (approve): This unblocks the Console + Support story; thanks. Please link the runbook from the oncall handbook in a follow-up. Logan Wright: Ack; will file a follow-up PR in docs repo.
source.post_merge_notes: Follow-ups: (1) add oncall handbook link to docs/runbooks/model-rollout-correlation.md, (2) once rollout_phase_change events are fully deployed, switch annotations to the dedicated events index instead of parsing control-plane logs, (3) evaluate adding a lightweight ‘incident overlay’ annotation feed (PagerDuty) for tighter correlation.

source.title: Add Private minimum pack Prometheus alert rules: latency + availability (with SLO burn recording rules)
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.release_notes: Private minimum pack: ships baseline Prometheus recording + alert rules for latency SLO burn and availability (5xx/blackbox) with runbook links and conservative defaults.
source.body:
Context: This PR introduces the baseline alerting + recording rules for the Redwood Private observability minimum pack, focused on the highest-impact failure modes for inference availability and latency regressions. These are intended to be safe-by-default in customer clusters (bounded cardinality, conservative paging) and to map cleanly to the SLO guidance we’ve been using in staging.

What’s included (v1):
- Recording rules (namespace: redwood_private:*) used by dashboards + alerts so queries are consistent and cheaper.
- Latency SLO burn alerts (multi-window, multi-burn) for the API gateway and runtime request path.
- Availability alerts (5xx rate + blackbox reachability) for API ingress and control plane critical services.
- Alert annotations include runbook + first steps links and a canonical dashboard deep link placeholder.
- promtool tests for rule syntax + basic evaluation fixtures.

Alert philosophy / thresholds (rationale):
- We page only on issues that are likely customer-visible and sustained.
- Latency: burn-rate alerts use a 2-window strategy (fast burn for rapid regressions, slow burn for creeping issues). This matches Aisha’s guidance for Private: prefer burn rates over raw p95 spikes to avoid noise in heterogeneous customer traffic.
- Availability: default is request-based (5xx ratio) rather than absolute error count; includes a minimum request rate gate to avoid low-traffic false positives.
- We intentionally avoid high-cardinality labels in alerts (no tenant_id, no raw model name, no full route). Where route-level is needed, we aggregate to route_group and/or service.

Defaults (tuned for min pack):
- Page (Sev2): sustained burn likely to exhaust error budget within 1h OR API is unreachable.
- Ticket (Sev3): slower burn / partial degradation / early warning.
- Warning alerts are created but set to disabled-by-default in values; the helm wiring PR will decide final toggles.

Runbooks:
- Latency burn: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPRIV/Runbook+-+Private+Latency+SLO+Burn
- Availability / 5xx: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPRIV/Runbook+-+Private+Availability+and+5xx
- Prometheus scrape health (support): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGINFRA/Prometheus+and+Grafana+for+Private+Installations

Notes / follow-ups:
- GPU saturation, OOM, and restart-loop alerts are tracked separately (ENG-19148).
- This PR does not wire rules into helm values; that is handled in redwood-helm-charts PR-18405.
- We should revisit the latency objective (currently defaults to 800ms p95 for chat endpoints) after we have 2–3 customer baselines from validation.

Checklist:
- [x] Recording rules included for RPS, error ratio, latency quantiles, and SLI measurements
- [x] Alerts include runbook URLs and concise "what to do first" steps
- [x] promtool rule tests added
- [x] Label cardinality bounded (no per-tenant or per-model alert dimension)
- [x] Reviewed against Private metrics/alerting standards

Screenshots/Examples:
- Included in the conversation: example firing of LatencySLOBurnFast after injected delay; and non-firing for low traffic due to request rate gate.
commits: Add redwood_private SLI recording rules for request rate, errors, and latency windows
Add latency SLO burn alerts (fast/slow) for gateway and runtime
Add availability alerts (5xx ratio + blackbox probe) with request rate gates
Add runbook links + alert annotations and labels (severity, service, component)
Add promtool unit tests + CI job hook for rule validation
Tighten label set to avoid high cardinality; aggregate by service/route_group
conversation:
Allison Grant (review): Can we align naming with the standards doc? Prefer redwood_private:sli_error_ratio:rate5m rather than redwood_private_error_ratio_5m.
Sergio Costa (author): Updated to match the `redwood_private:<thing>:<agg>` convention and added a short note in README-private-min-pack.md.

Aisha Bello (review): For burn rates, please use a multi-window multi-burn pattern and avoid a single p95 threshold alert. Also add a minimum traffic gate.
Sergio Costa (author): Switched to 2-window burn (5m/1h + 30m/6h), added `> 1 rps` gate for the SLI streams, and kept a disabled-by-default "p95 spike" warning only for debugging.

Neha Kapoor (review): We need actionable annotations. Include the 3 first steps in the alert description, not only the runbook link.
Sergio Costa (author): Added annotations: (1) check scrape health, (2) check gateway saturation panels, (3) confirm recent deploys/rollouts. Also added placeholders for the Grafana dashboard deep links.

Tomáš Novák (review): Severity labels need to map to paging policy: page-worthy should be `severity=sev2` and ticket should be `sev3`. Please avoid `critical`/`warning` since Support tooling keys off sev2/sev3.
Sergio Costa (author): Updated severities and added `pager=oncall|none` routing hint label per our standard. Confirmed with Rafael this matches staging routing.

Rafael Mendes (review): I replayed the staging incident from last week (gateway auth misconfig causing 401s + 5xx spikes). The 5xx ratio alert fired correctly; can we add a small note that 401/403 are excluded by design?
Sergio Costa (author): Added note in README + kept alert expression scoped to 5xx. Also added a comment in the rule file to prevent future changes that page on auth failures (Support handles separately).

Neha Kapoor (follow-up): promtool tests are great; can we add at least one test to ensure low-traffic clusters don’t fire availability alerts?
Sergio Costa (author): Added fixture `private_availability_5xx_low_traffic.yaml` and a non-firing assertion.
related_links: Linear ENG-19147: Private alerts - latency and availability

Support ticket SUP-49238: Private alerts too noisy after upgrade (tuning guidance input)

source.title: Add Private minimum pack: versioned Grafana dashboard bundle (runtime + control plane + infra)
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.release_notes: Private installs: adds initial, versioned Grafana dashboard bundle for the Private Observability Minimum Pack (runtime + control plane + infra). Shipped as artifacts only; wiring via Helm/installer follows in subsequent PRs.
source.body: Summary: This PR adds the first versioned Grafana dashboard bundle for the Private Observability Minimum Pack. The dashboards are designed to be shipped as immutable artifacts (checked into this repo) and later installed via `redwood-helm-charts`/`redwood-private-installer` (follow-ups). Scope: 1) Runtime overview dashboard focused on golden signals + inference-specific panels (token throughput, batching, KV cache, GPU utilization). 2) Control plane health dashboard (gateway/auth/policy/config services) with standard service golden signals and dependency panels (DB/redis if present). 3) Infra dashboard for customer cluster basics required to triage most common Private incidents (node pressure, PVC pressure, pod restart loops, scrape status). Design notes / constraints: - Private environments require bounded label cardinality; dashboards avoid templating on high-cardinality labels like raw `route`, `tenant_id`, `request_id`. Where filtering is useful, panels use a small set of safe dimensions (`namespace`, `service`, `cluster`, `instance`), and model/route usage is represented via top-k panels based on recording rules (to be introduced in ENG-19144/PR-18356). - Dashboards are versioned under `dashboards/private/minimum-pack/v1.0.0/` and include stable UIDs prefixed with `rw-private-` to ensure upgrades don’t create duplicates. - Panels include conservative defaults (5m/1h ranges) and annotate ‘expected exporters’ (kube-state-metrics, node-exporter, NVIDIA DCGM exporter) but do not hard-depend on optional exporters (panels degrade gracefully when metrics are missing). What’s included (v1.0.0): - `Runtime / Overview (Private)` (UID: `rw-private-runtime-overview`) - `Control Plane / Health (Private)` (UID: `rw-private-control-plane-health`) - `Private / Cluster Infra` (UID: `rw-private-cluster-infra`) - `Private / Prometheus Scrape Health` (UID: `rw-private-prom-scrape-health`) (small helper dashboard to debug ‘empty panels’ issues) Implementation details: - Adds a bundle manifest (`dashboards/private/minimum-pack/bundle.yaml`) that enumerates dashboards, UIDs, and target folder names for the installer. - Adds lightweight validation tooling in CI (`make dashboards-validate`) to ensure exported JSON is stable, has a UID, and does not embed org-specific datasource IDs. - Standardizes dashboard tags: `redwood`, `private`, `minimum-pack`, and component tag (e.g. `runtime`, `control-plane`, `infra`). Testing: - Ran `make dashboards-validate` locally. - Imported into Grafana 10.4 with a local Prometheus, verified template variables resolve and panels render with a simulated dataset. - Verified dashboards import with no ‘Datasource not found’ errors by using named datasource `Prometheus` and `Loki` placeholders (installer will map). Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Wire into `redwood-helm-charts` values + installer packaging (ENG-19152 / PR-18405). - Add recording rules and alerts referenced by some panels (ENG-19144 / PR-18356). - Add on-prem offline bundle inclusion + checksums/signing (ENG-19154 / PR-18441).
commits: dashboards(private): add v1.0.0 runtime overview dashboard export
dashboards(private): add control plane health dashboard export
dashboards(private): add cluster infra + prometheus scrape health dashboards
packaging: introduce minimum-pack bundle manifest + folder mapping
ci: add dashboard validation script and make target
docs: note UID/tag conventions for private dashboard artifacts
source.review_conversation: Allison Grant (review): Can we confirm we’re not templating on model/route labels anywhere? Private cardinality guidance says no raw route/model variables in dashboard dropdowns. Sergio Costa (author): Confirmed—no dropdown variables for route/model; I removed the initial `$model` variable and replaced with a top-k table panel. The remaining variables are namespace/service/instance only. Rafael Mendes (review): Please add a small ‘Prometheus Scrape Health’ view; Support keeps getting ‘dashboards empty’ tickets where it’s actually a scrape/auth issue. Sergio Costa (author): Added `rw-private-prom-scrape-health` dashboard with target up/down, scrape duration, sample ingestion, and relabel drops. Linked it in the bundle manifest. Chloe Martin (review): UID stability is critical; please ensure all dashboards have explicit UIDs and that exports don’t include numeric datasource IDs. Sergio Costa (author): Set explicit UIDs (`rw-private-*`) and updated validation to fail if datasource is not the named placeholder. Dylan Brooks (review): Folder naming: can we standardize on `Redwood / Private / Minimum Pack` for v1? Sergio Costa (author): Done in `bundle.yaml` with a single folder target. Ethan Park (review): Control plane dashboard should include gateway 4xx/5xx split and auth/RBAC latency panels. Sergio Costa (author): Added panels for gateway status code classes and auth middleware latency histogram (when present). Approvals: Dylan Brooks approved. Chloe Martin approved. Allison Grant approved. Rafael Mendes approved.

Where filtering is useful, panels use a small set of safe dimensions (`namespace`, `service`, `cluster`, `instance`), and model/route usage is represented via top-k panels based on recording rules (to be introduced in ENG-19144/PR-18356). - Dashboards are versioned under `dashboards/private/minimum-pack/v1.0.0/` and include stable UIDs prefixed with `rw-private-` to ensure upgrades don’t create duplicates. - Panels include conservative defaults (5m/1h ranges) and annotate ‘expected exporters’ (kube-state-metrics, node-exporter, NVIDIA DCGM exporter) but do not hard-depend on optional exporters (panels degrade gracefully when metrics are missing). What’s included (v1.0.0): - `Runtime / Overview (Private)` (UID: `rw-private-runtime-overview`) - `Control Plane / Health (Private)` (UID: `rw-private-control-plane-health`) - `Private / Cluster Infra` (UID: `rw-private-cluster-infra`) - `Private / Prometheus Scrape Health` (UID: `rw-private-prom-scrape-health`) (small helper dashboard to debug ‘empty panels’ issues) Implementation details: - Adds a bundle manifest (`dashboards/private/minimum-pack/bundle.yaml`) that enumerates dashboards, UIDs, and target folder names for the installer. - Adds lightweight validation tooling in CI (`make dashboards-validate`) to ensure exported JSON is stable, has a UID, and does not embed org-specific datasource IDs. - Standardizes dashboard tags: `redwood`, `private`, `minimum-pack`, and component tag (e.g. `runtime`, `control-plane`, `infra`). Testing: - Ran `make dashboards-validate` locally. - Imported into Grafana 10.4 with a local Prometheus, verified template variables resolve and panels render with a simulated dataset. - Verified dashboards import with no ‘Datasource not found’ errors by using named datasource `Prometheus` and `Loki` placeholders (installer will map). Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Wire into `redwood-helm-charts` values + installer packaging (ENG-19152 / PR-18405). - Add recording rules and alerts referenced by some panels (ENG-19144 / PR-18356). - Add on-prem offline bundle inclusion + checksums/signing (ENG-19154 / PR-18441).
text: Summary: This PR adds the first versioned Grafana dashboard bundle for the Private Observability Minimum Pack. The dashboards are designed to be shipped as immutable artifacts (checked into this repo) and later installed via `redwood-helm-charts`/`redwood-private-installer` (follow-ups). Scope: 1) Runtime overview dashboard focused on golden signals + inference-specific panels (token throughput, batching, KV cache, GPU utilization). 2) Control plane health dashboard (gateway/auth/policy/config services) with standard service golden signals and dependency panels (DB/redis if present). 3) Infra dashboard for customer cluster basics required to triage most common Private incidents (node pressure, PVC pressure, pod restart loops, scrape status). source.title: Add alert rules + routing for Dedicated max-bound saturation and sustained queue buildup
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.description:
Title: Add alert rules + routing for Dedicated max-bound saturation and sustained queue buildup

Context
Dedicated autoscaling bounds are rolling out to pilot customers. During pilot we’ve seen two recurring failure/impact modes that are easy to miss without dedicated alerting:
1) Deployment hits max bound for an extended period while demand continues increasing (customers see queueing / p95 latency regressions; support tickets like SUP-28411).
2) Sustained queue buildup (even if not max-bound) indicating a scaling issue, forecast signal outage, or model-level throughput regression.

This PR adds:
- Prometheus alert rules for max-bound saturation and sustained queue buildup.
- Alertmanager routing to Dedicated on-call (page) vs #eng-infra (ticket/notify) depending on severity and duration.
- Rule annotations/runbook links aligned with the Dedicated autoscaler troubleshooting runbook.

What’s included
- New alerts (names follow metric naming standards and keep labels low-cardinality):
  - DedicatedMaxBoundSustained (page): bounded at max AND queue depth elevated for >= 10m.
  - DedicatedMaxBoundHitRateHigh (notify): high ratio of bounded_desired == max over a rolling window, even if queue is not yet elevated.
  - DedicatedQueueDepthSustained (page): queue depth above threshold for >= 15m (guarded by traffic floor to avoid noise).
  - DedicatedMinBoundUnmet (notify): min bound cannot be met (region capacity shortage / provisioning failure), intended as early warning to SRE + capacity planning.
- Routing:
  - Severity=page routes to PagerDuty service "dedicated-oncall" with grouping by (deployment_id, region).
  - Severity=notify routes to Slack #eng-infra and creates a ticket via existing webhook (no new integration).
- Documentation pointers:
  - Annotations include dashboard links (from pr-1934) and runbook section anchors.

Thresholds (initial)
- Queue depth signal uses dedicated_autoscaler_queue_depth (already emitted by fleet telemetry) with a traffic floor via req_rate to avoid paging on low-volume deployments.
- Max-bound saturation uses dedicated_autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas == dedicated_autoscaler_max_replicas AND queue depth above a small baseline (to avoid false positives when customers intentionally cap above demand).
- All thresholds were chosen to match pilot expectations and can be tuned post-canary; see inline comments in rules.

Files / areas changed
- alerts/dedicated/autoscaling_bounds_alerts.yaml: new rule group(s) for bounds + queueing.
- alertmanager/routes/dedicated.yaml: new routes + grouping rules.
- alertmanager/receivers/pagerduty.yaml: ensure dedicated-oncall receiver exists and has correct service key reference.
- dashboards/links.yaml: adds canonical URLs used by alert annotations.
- tests/promtool_rules_test.yaml: rule unit tests covering basic firing/non-firing cases.

Rollout plan
- Landing behind existing Dedicated bounds rollout (feature is opt-in per account), but alerting is safe to enable globally since it only evaluates when metrics exist.
- We will initially enable paging routes only for pilot accounts (via matcher on customer_tier=pilot) for 1 week, then broaden to all Dedicated once noise is acceptable.

Checklist
- [x] promtool check passes
- [x] alert rule tests added
- [x] annotations include runbook + dashboard
- [x] routing reviewed by SRE

Notes / follow-ups
- We should consider adding a separate alert for "predictive signal stale" once the predictor staleness metric is stable (tracked in ENG-22142).
- The min-bound unmet alert is notify-only by design; paging policy should be revisited after we collect baseline rate.

Screenshots
N/A (rule changes only).
commits: Add Dedicated autoscaling bounds alert rules (max saturation + queue depth)
Add Alertmanager routing for Dedicated paging vs notify
Add promtool rule tests and fix label matchers
Tighten annotations: runbook links + dashboard URLs
conversation: Allison Grant (review): Can we avoid grouping by customer_id in paging routes? It will explode groups on large enterprise accounts. Suggest grouping by deployment_id + region only, and keep customer_id as an annotation.
Sergio Costa (author): Good call. Updated group_by to (deployment_id, region). Kept customer_id in annotations/labels for search but not grouping.

Rafael Mendes (review): For DedicatedMaxBoundSustained, please ensure we only page when queue depth is elevated too; max-bound alone can be normal if they cap high above demand. Also include runbook link to the "Max bound saturation" section.
Sergio Costa (author): Updated expression to require queue_depth > baseline AND req_rate > floor. Added runbook anchor and dashboard link in annotations.
Aisha Bello (review): Queue depth sustained: do we have a consistent unit/scale across models? If not, consider a relative threshold or use queue wait time if available.

Sergio Costa (author): Queue wait time isn’t consistently emitted yet. For now I added a traffic floor and a longer for=15m to reduce noise. Added TODO comment to revisit once queue_wait_seconds is rolled out.

Miles Thornton (review): promtool test coverage looks good. One nit: please use severity=notify (not warning) to match our routing conventions. Also ensure labels are restricted to {deployment_id, region, environment}.
Sergio Costa (author): Renamed severities to page/notify and removed a stray model_name label matcher. promtool + unit tests updated accordingly.
Allison Grant (approval): LGTM after the grouping fix + severity convention update. Let’s monitor noise during pilot week.
source.release_notes: Internal: Added paging/notify alerts for Dedicated max-bound saturation and sustained queue buildup; includes routing and runbook/dashboard annotations.
