source.title: Private minimum pack: add GPU saturation + OOM/resource pressure alerts for inference pods
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.release_notes: Private minimum pack now includes baseline GPU saturation and OOM/resource pressure alerting for runtime/serving pods; several alerts are shipped disabled-by-default to avoid paging noise until clusters have stable baselines.
source.body:
Context: Private minimum pack needs a minimal, supported set of alerts that catch the highest-impact inference failure modes (GPU saturation, GPU memory pressure, OOM kills, restart loops) without overloading customer on-calls. This PR adds GPU/resource pressure alert rules scoped to Redwood inference pods and wires them into the existing private rules bundle. It includes tuning notes from the alert-noise review and enforces label/cardinality constraints for customer Prometheus.

What’s included: (1) GPU saturation alerts (warning/ticket): sustained high GPU utilization on inference pods aggregated by cluster/namespace/workload; (2) GPU memory pressure (warning): high framebuffer utilization (FB used/total) using DCGM metrics when available; (3) OOM kill alerts (critical/page): repeated OOMKilled terminations for redwood-runtime containers over a short window; (4) restart loop alerts (warning -> critical escalation): excessive restarts for runtime pods; (5) node/pod resource pressure signals (warning): Kubernetes NodeMemoryPressure for nodes running runtime pods, and container memory RSS high (guardrail) where kubelet/cadvisor metrics exist.

Noise tuning / defaults: A few alerts are intentionally shipped disabled-by-default via a `privateMinimumPack.alerts.gpu.enableExperimental` gate in values (see helm wiring in follow-up PRs) because clusters differ significantly in baseline utilization. In particular, the "HighGPUUtilization" warning threshold is conservative and requires a long `for:` to avoid flapping during bursty token workloads. The OOMKilled alert remains enabled by default (impact is high and signal is reliable). All alerts include runbook annotations with first-actions oriented toward Private support/SRE.

Metric dependencies: This assumes (a) kube-state-metrics is present for pod termination reasons and restarts, (b) node-exporter/kubelet metrics for node conditions and container memory, and (c) DCGM exporter for GPU metrics. If DCGM metrics are absent, GPU-specific alerts will stay inactive; the rules are written to avoid hard-failing eval.

Cardinality constraints: Alerts intentionally avoid `model`, `route`, `tenant`, and `request_id` labels. Aggregations are by `cluster`, `namespace`, and `workload`/`deployment` only. For DCGM, we aggregate across GPUs per node to avoid per-GPU paging; detailed per-GPU dashboards remain the place to drill in.

Testing: Validated rules load in Prometheus (promtool), unit tests cover common label sets, and smoke-test harness was run in staging-private with synthetic load + forced OOM scenarios. Included one follow-up note: customers running strict NetworkPolicies may need to allow DCGM scrape targets; that is called out in the runbook.

Follow-ups: (1) Wire `enableExperimental` toggles into `redwood-helm-charts` private values (tracked in ENG-19152); (2) Add a short troubleshooting section to the Day-2 runbook for "DCGM metrics missing" and "OOMKilled alert firing" patterns; (3) Consider a recording rule for GPU mem fraction to simplify dashboards if we keep these long-term.

Alert summary (names may change slightly before GA): - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeGPUUtilizationHigh (warning, disabled-by-default) - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeGPUMemoryPressure (warning, disabled-by-default) - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeOOMKilled (critical, enabled) - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeRestartLoop (warning/critical, enabled) - RedwoodPrivateNodeMemoryPressureForRuntime (warning, enabled)

Notes for reviewers: Please focus on (a) severity mapping (page vs ticket), (b) label usage/cardinality, (c) thresholds and `for:` durations, and (d) runbook annotation clarity (what to do first).
commits: alerts(private): add DCGM-based GPU utilization + memory pressure rules
alerts(private): add OOMKilled and restart-loop detection for runtime containers
alerts: gate noisy GPU utilization alerts behind enableExperimental
tests: promtool rule tests for common label topologies
docs: add inline noise tuning notes + runbook annotations
review_conversation: Allison Grant (review): Can we ensure none of these alerts include `pod` in the final labels? Paging by pod tends to explode cardinality in customer clusters. Also please align severity labels to the private taxonomy (critical=page, warning=ticket).

Sergio Costa (author): Yep. Updated `labels:` blocks to only include `namespace` + `workload` and moved `pod` to annotations as a top-k example (non-label). Also normalized `severity` to `critical|warning` and added `redwood_sev` annotation for the page vs ticket mapping used by Support.

Thomas Baird (review): GPU util at >95% for 30m will page in most clusters (and it's normal to run hot). Recommend disabled-by-default, or make it warning-only with a long `for:` and add a concurrent symptom requirement (latency burn / queue growth).

Sergio Costa (author): Agreed. Set GPU util alert to warning and disabled-by-default behind `enableExperimental`. Also added a note recommending pairing with latency SLO burn alerts from PR-18356; didn’t add multi-signal logic here to keep minimum pack simple.

Rafael Mendes (review): OOMKilled rule looks good, but please add `for: 5m` to avoid transient crash loops during rolling deploys, and include a runbook link that starts with: check recent rollout + memory limits + batch size. Also, do we have an annotation for "customer action vs Redwood action"?

Sergio Costa (author): Added `for: 5m`, and the runbook now starts with rollout check + limit/requests check + batch/KV cache settings. Added `owner_team` and `action_owner` annotations (customer vs redwood) as requested.

Omar El-Sayed (review): The OOMKilled metric is kube-state-metrics dependent; in some on-prem installs they run a different version. Can we make the expression tolerant to missing series? And ensure label selectors match `redwood-runtime` and `redwood-serving` variants?

Sergio Costa (author): Updated selectors to match `app.kubernetes.io/part-of="redwood"` + `component in (runtime,serving)` and added `or vector(0)` patterns where appropriate so rule eval doesn’t error when series are absent.
Allison Grant (approval): Looks aligned with standards now. Please keep the disabled-by-default gating and call it out in the minimum-pack overview docs (separate PR). Approved.
Rafael Mendes (approval): Approved. The runbook annotations are much better now; oncall will thank you.
source.notes: Post-merge: backport requested to release/private-observability-pack-0.9 (see eng-releases thread). Staging validation recorded in smoke-test logs; no additional alert noise observed after gating util/mem-pressure alerts disabled-by-default.

Follow-ups: (1) Wire `enableExperimental` toggles into `redwood-helm-charts` private values (tracked in ENG-19152); (2) Add a short troubleshooting section to the Day-2 runbook for "DCGM metrics missing" and "OOMKilled alert firing" patterns; (3) Consider a recording rule for GPU mem fraction to simplify dashboards if we keep these long-term.

Alert summary (names may change slightly before GA): - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeGPUUtilizationHigh (warning, disabled-by-default) - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeGPUMemoryPressure (warning, disabled-by-default) - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeOOMKilled (critical, enabled) - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeRestartLoop (warning/critical, enabled) - RedwoodPrivateNodeMemoryPressureForRuntime (warning, enabled)

Notes for reviewers: Please focus on (a) severity mapping (page vs ticket), (b) label usage/cardinality, (c) thresholds and `for:` durations, and (d) runbook annotation clarity (what to do first).

source.title: Add Private upgrade health dashboards (prechecks, migrations, error rates, rollbacks)
source.author: Allison Grant
source.body: Summary: Adds a Grafana dashboard pack for Redwood Private upgrade health, focused on the new installer-driven upgrade workflow (prechecks -> backup -> control plane rollout -> data plane rollout -> migrations -> postchecks, with rollback entry points). Dashboard set covers (1) precheck failure rates + breakdown by check_id/severity, (2) DB migration durations and timeouts/lock waits, (3) API/control-plane error rates during upgrade windows, and (4) rollback frequency + outcomes. Motivation: SRE and Release Eng asked for a single place to validate upgrade readiness and spot regressions during beta rollouts; today we rely on scattered logs and ad-hoc queries. This PR makes upgrades observable with consistent panels and variables that work in Hosted VPC Private and on-prem (including air-gapped) where only Prometheus + Loki are available. What’s included: - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Overview" (upgrade success rate, active upgrades, error budget impact proxy, rollbacks). - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Prechecks" (fail rate, top failing checks, blocking vs warning, remediation link rendering). - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Migrations" (p50/p95 duration by migration_name, lock wait time, failed migrations, timeouts). - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Upgrade Window Errors" (HTTP 5xx/429, gRPC error codes if present, control-plane reachability, installer command failure count). - Optional annotation integration: upgrade window markers derived from audit/installer events when present; otherwise uses a manual label/annotation query. Metrics/log sources: - Prometheus metrics emitted by installer/controller-plane components: redwood_private_upgrade_runs_total, redwood_private_upgrade_precheck_results_total{check_id,severity,result}, redwood_private_upgrade_stage_duration_seconds_bucket{stage}, redwood_db_migration_duration_seconds_bucket{migration}, redwood_db_migration_failures_total{migration,reason}, redwood_private_rollback_runs_total{result}. - Loki log-derived counters for older installs missing a subset of metrics (best-effort fallback queries). Installation notes: Dashboards are templated with common labels (cluster, environment, customer, deployment_mode). On-prem guidance included: set prometheus external_labels for customer + cluster; if unavailable, dashboards still work but variables are limited. Screenshots: attached in PR (Overview + Prechecks + Migrations). Risks/rollout: purely additive dashboards/queries; no alert changes in this PR. Follow-ups: add alerts for migration timeout and elevated rollback frequency once beta thresholds stabilize; wire in audit event fields after redwood/pr-29187 lands everywhere.
commits: dashboards(private-upgrades): add overview dashboard with active upgrade + success rate panels
dashboards(private-upgrades): add prechecks dashboard with check_id breakdown + blocking vs warning split
dashboards(private-upgrades): add migrations dashboard (duration histograms, timeouts, lock wait panels)
dashboards(private-upgrades): add upgrade-window error rate dashboard + optional annotation query
chore(grafana): add dashboard validation to CI and standardize templating variables
source.conversation: Connor O'Brien (review): Can we align naming with the installer upgrade-plan stages? I want SREs to correlate stage names with the plan output. Also, please ensure dashboard variables don’t assume hosted-only labels. Allison Grant (author): Updated stage mapping to match the plan vocabulary (prechecks, backup, control_plane, data_plane, migrations, postchecks). Also switched variable defaults to use cluster/environment labels and made customer optional. Sean Gallagher (review): The precheck fail rate panel looks good, but can we separate blocking vs warning and show top failing checks over the last 24h? Also: please add a "no data" hint for air-gapped installs that don’t have Loki wired. Allison Grant (author): Added a row that splits by severity and a Top 10 failing checks table (by check_id). Added panel description text for Loki fallback and a note in README. Rafael Mendes (review): Migrations dashboard: can we include lock timeout count? We saw lock wait issues in staging and it’s a key signal during upgrades. Also ensure we graph p95 by migration for spotting regressions. Allison Grant (author): Added redwood_db_migration_failures_total{reason="lock_timeout"} and included p50/p95 stat panels by migration. Kira Thompson (review): Please add a short operator note in docs on how to interpret "rollback frequency" and not panic on drills; also mention what counts as a rollback run. Allison Grant (author): Added docs section clarifying rollback run semantics (installer rollback command invocation + result label) and how to filter out drills using the run_reason label when present. Sean Gallagher (final): Approved after the stage naming update and lock-timeout panel. Connor O'Brien (final): Approved; please ensure CHANGELOG entry calls out dashboards only (no functional changes).
release_notes: Added Grafana dashboards for Redwood Private upgrade health: precheck failures, migration durations/timeouts, error rates during upgrade windows, and rollback frequency.

source.title: Add Audit Exporter Grafana dashboards + alert rules (lag, delivery errors)
source.author: Caleb Johnson
source.description: Context: As part of the SIEM-ready audit export beta, we need first-class operational visibility for the audit-log-exporter service (lag, retries, adapter failures) and alerting aligned to the SLOs defined by SRE. This PR adds Grafana dashboards and Prometheus alert rules for the exporter, intended to be deployed to hosted + dedicated environments for the beta cohort. Links: Linear ENG-5168 (exporter observability), Confluence SLO doc: "Audit exporter SLOs and alerting" (owner: Sean/Rafael). Scope: (1) Grafana dashboard: Overview (lag, throughput, success rate), (2) Grafana dashboard: Destination adapters (Splunk HEC / Datadog / S3), (3) Alert rules: export lag burn, sustained adapter errors, DLQ growth, auto-paused exports, (4) Recording rules for rate-based panels to reduce query cost. Notes on semantics: Export lag is computed from exporter checkpoint watermark vs event time; alerts use p95 lag by tenant and also a global max with cardinality controls. We intentionally avoid alerting on single-tenant spikes unless they violate the global budget or a configured "beta tenant allowlist" label is present. Rollout: safe to merge; rules are gated behind the existing audit-exporter job selector and will be enabled per cluster by SRE during beta rollout.
commits: Add audit exporter dashboard (overview) with lag/throughput panels
Add destination adapter dashboard and standardize adapter labels (destination_type)
Add recording rules for exporter rates (reduce Grafana query load)
Add alert rules for lag, adapter error budgets, DLQ growth, and paused exports
Tighten alert annotations + runbook links; align to naming conventions
notable_snippets:
Alert rule (excerpt):
- alert: AuditExporterLagHigh
  expr: |
    histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, cluster) (rate(audit_exporter_export_lag_seconds_bucket{job="audit-log-exporter"}[5m]))) > 300
  for: 15m
  labels:
    severity: page
    service: audit-exporter
  annotations:
    summary: "Audit exporter lag p95 > 5m (cluster={{ $labels.cluster }})"
    description: "Exporter is falling behind SLO window. Check downstream throttling (429/5xx), queue depth, and auto-pause state."
    runbook: "https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SRE/Runbook%3A+Audit+Exporter+Delivery+Failures"

Dashboard panel (excerpt):
Panel: "Delivery Success Rate (by destination_type)"
Query: sum(rate(audit_exporter_deliveries_total{status="success"}[5m])) by (destination_type) / sum(rate(audit_exporter_deliveries_total[5m])) by (destination_type)
review_conversation:
Allison Grant: Can we avoid alerting on max lag across all tenants? That tends to page on one noisy tenant.
Caleb Johnson: Agree. Updated rule to use p95 at cluster level and added a separate ticket-level alert for max lag scoped to beta tenants only (label filter: beta_cohort="siem-audit-export").

Sean Gallagher: Please align severity/page vs severity/ticket to the SLO doc. Paging should be for sustained lag > 5m and adapter error rate > 2% for 30m.
Caleb Johnson: Updated thresholds and durations: lag p95 > 5m for 15m => page; adapter error budget burn => ticket unless sustained 30m at >2% => page. Added annotations to match SRE policy.

Logan Wright: The destination adapter dashboard should show retries/backoff so we can correlate with 429s.
Caleb Johnson: Added panels for retry_attempts_total, backoff_seconds (avg), and a heatmap for adapter_http_status_code by destination_type (limited label set).

Aisha Bello: Please add runbook links in annotations and ensure alert names follow our standard prefixing.
Caleb Johnson: Done. Alert names prefixed with AuditExporter*, annotations include runbook URL and dashboard URL.

Rafael Mendes: Looks good; please confirm promtool validation passes in CI after the YAML indentation change.
Caleb Johnson: Re-ran CI; promtool-validate is green.
source.release_notes:
- Adds Grafana dashboards for audit exporter lag, throughput, and destination health
- Adds Prometheus alerting/recording rules aligned to audit exporter SLOs (beta)

source.title: Add onboarding KPIs dashboard (TTFS/TTFP + checklist funnel) and recommended alerts
source.author: Allison Grant
release_notes: New packaged dashboard: Onboarding KPIs (TTFS/TTFP + step funnel + drop-off reasons).
New recommended alerts: onboarding dataset freshness + step drop-off spike detection.
Documentation: metric definitions and how to wire into customer cohort filters (plan/region/sdk).
source.description:
## Summary
Adds a packaged Grafana dashboard + alert rules for the Guided Onboarding / First Production Launch initiative.

This is intended to be imported via the standard `observability-pack` rollout (same mechanism as the latency/cost packs), and targets the onboarding telemetry datasets produced by the gateway/control plane eventing work + derived milestone tables.

## What’s included
Dashboard: **Onboarding KPIs (Hosted API)**
- TTFS (signup → first successful 2xx request) trend
  - Median / p90 / p95 by signup cohort
  - Breakdown by `sdk_language` (via user-agent tags), `region`, and `plan_tier`
- TTFP (signup → first production traffic) trend
  - Median / p90 by signup cohort
  - Includes optional filter for "prod_traffic" classifier (see notes below)
- Step completion funnel
  - % of tenants completing each checklist step (ordered)
  - Step-to-next-step conversion + time-in-step
  - Top drop-off steps (ranked)
- Drop-off reasons / first failure signals
  - First auth failure (401/403) rate by cohort
  - First 429 rate by cohort
  - First streaming disconnect signal rate (from gateway termination reasons)
- Data quality tiles
  - Event ingest freshness (minutes behind)
  - Missing required dimensions rate (null/unknown sdk_language/region)

Alerts (recommended; shipped disabled-by-default)
- **Onboarding KPI dataset freshness**
  - Triggers when `onboarding_events_v1` ingestion is > 30m behind for 2 consecutive evaluation windows
  - Also triggers when derived `tenant_onboarding_milestones` hasn’t updated in > 60m
- **Step drop-off spike**
  - Triggers when a step’s completion rate drops > 20% vs trailing 7d baseline (configurable)
  - Default scope is Hosted API + self-serve cohorts; enterprise/dedicated cohorts can be configured separately

## Data sources and metric definitions
This pack assumes the following tables/views exist in the central analytics workspace (names match the platform telemetry spec as of March 2025):
- `analytics.onboarding_events_v1` (raw / lightly normalized events)
- `analytics.tenant_onboarding_milestones_v1` (derived; 1 row per tenant with timestamps per step)

Metric definitions are aligned to PM-751:
- TTFS: `first_success_at - signup_at`
  - `first_success_at` is the first gateway 2xx for the canonical generate/chat endpoints, excluding health checks
- TTFP: `first_prod_traffic_at - signup_at`
  - `first_prod_traffic_at` is computed by downstream classifier (>= N requests over M minutes OR explicit "prod" tag). The dashboard supports both modes via a variable.

Important: the dashboard does **not** join to billing for these KPIs (to avoid the known usage dashboard lag issue). We only use billing-derived fields when available as optional breakdown dimensions.

## How to install
- `obs-pack apply onboarding-kpis`
- or import the JSON from `packs/onboarding-kpis/dashboards/onboarding_kpis_grafana.json`

After import, set variables:
- `environment` (prod/staging)
- `cohort_window` (default: last 8 weeks)
- `deployment_mode` (default: hosted)

## Screenshots
Attached in internal PR UI (Grafana panels: funnel + TTFS/TTFP timeseries + alert preview).

## Notes / limitations
- Step drop-off alerting uses a simple baseline comparison. This is intentionally conservative for MVP; we can add seasonality-aware detection once we see stable weekly traffic.
- `sdk_language` depends on OpenAI-compat user-agent tagging (redwood-openai-compat#265). If not present, values show as `unknown`.
- This PR ships Hosted API defaults; Dedicated/Private filters are stubbed but not surfaced in the default dashboard nav.

## Testing
- Validated queries against staging analytics (last 14d) and ensured panels render with empty-state handling.
- Dry-run the alert rules with `alerts.test_mode=true` to confirm routing/labels.
- Checked for cardinality regressions (no per-tenant series in alerts; tenant-level only in dashboard table panels).

## Rollout plan
- Merge behind pack version bump only (no automatic enablement).
- SRE/Telemetry will enable alerts for internal dogfood cohorts first (see ENG-5815).

## Checklist
- [x] Dashboard uses standard variable names and folder layout
- [x] Alerts are disabled by default and include runbook links
- [x] No PII fields referenced (tenant_id is only used in table panels and not emitted in alert labels)
- [x] Added docs for metric definitions and known caveats
commits: add onboarding-kpis pack skeleton + dashboard folder layout
add TTFS/TTFP panels and cohort variables
add step funnel panels + time-in-step queries
add recommended alert rules (freshness + drop-off spike) and runbook links
docs: add onboarding-kpis pack README + metric definition notes
chore: normalize dashboard naming + tag conventions
review_conversation:
Logan Wright: The drop-off alert query is grouping by `step_name` only, but the dashboard query includes `deployment_mode`. Can we make sure we don't page on Dedicated/Private noise before those cohorts are stable?
Allison Grant (author): Good call. I scoped the alert rules to `deployment_mode=hosted` by default and left deployment_mode as a dashboard variable only. Added a comment + override example in the alert YAML.

Mei Lin: TTFS definition should exclude `/v1/health` and internal console probes. Also, the time window for "first success" needs to be consistent with PM-751.
Allison Grant (author): Updated `ttfs_ttfp.sql` to filter to canonical generate/chat routes and `source != internal_probe`. Added a note referencing PM-751 and the exclusion list in the pack README.

Aisha Bello: Freshness threshold at 30m might be too tight given batch loads. Can we use 45m and require 2 eval windows?
Allison Grant (author): Adjusted to 30m but requiring 2 consecutive windows, and added a 60m guardrail for the derived milestones table. Also added guidance in the runbook for tuning in low-traffic regions.

Chris Osei: Please ensure alert labels don’t include tenant_id (cardinality + privacy). Dashboard table is fine, but alerts must not carry it.
Allison Grant (author): Confirmed: tenant_id only appears in dashboard table panels, and alert labels are limited to environment + step_name + deployment_mode. Added an explicit unit check in `packs/onboarding-kpis/alerts/` comments.
source.ci_summary: All checks passed: lint (pack), json schema validate, grafana import smoke test, alert rule validation.

- **Onboarding KPI dataset freshness**
  - Triggers when `onboarding_events_v1` ingestion is > 30m behind for 2 consecutive evaluation windows
  - Also triggers when derived `tenant_onboarding_milestones` hasn’t updated in > 60m
- **Step drop-off spike**
  - Triggers when a step’s completion rate drops > 20% vs trailing 7d baseline (configurable)
  - Default scope is Hosted API + self-serve cohorts; enterprise/dedicated cohorts can be configured separately

## Data sources and metric definitions
This pack assumes the following tables/views exist in the central analytics workspace (names match the platform telemetry spec as of March 2025):
- `analytics.onboarding_events_v1` (raw / lightly normalized events)
- `analytics.tenant_onboarding_milestones_v1` (derived; 1 row per tenant with timestamps per step)

Metric definitions are aligned to PM-751:
- TTFS: `first_success_at - signup_at`
  - `first_success_at` is the first gateway 2xx for the canonical generate/chat endpoints, excluding health checks
- TTFP: `first_prod_traffic_at - signup_at`
  - `first_prod_traffic_at` is computed by downstream classifier (>= N requests over M minutes OR explicit "prod" tag). The dashboard supports both modes via a variable.

Important: the dashboard does **not** join to billing for these KPIs (to avoid the known usage dashboard lag issue). We only use billing-derived fields when available as optional breakdown dimensions.

## How to install
- `obs-pack apply onboarding-kpis`
- or import the JSON from `packs/onboarding-kpis/dashboards/onboarding_kpis_grafana.json`

After import, set variables:
- `environment` (prod/staging)
- `cohort_window` (default: last 8 weeks)
- `deployment_mode` (default: hosted)

## Screenshots
Attached in internal PR UI (Grafana panels: funnel + TTFS/TTFP timeseries + alert preview).

## Notes / limitations
- Step drop-off alerting uses a simple baseline comparison. This is intentionally conservative for MVP; we can add seasonality-aware detection once we see stable weekly traffic.
- `sdk_language` depends on OpenAI-compat user-agent tagging (redwood-openai-compat#265). If not present, values show as `unknown`.
- This PR ships Hosted API defaults; Dedicated/Private filters are stubbed but not surfaced in the default dashboard nav.

## Testing
- Validated queries against staging analytics (last 14d) and ensured panels render with empty-state handling.
- Dry-run the alert rules with `alerts.test_mode=true` to confirm routing/labels.
- Checked for cardinality regressions (no per-tenant series in alerts; tenant-level only in dashboard table panels).

## Rollout plan
- Merge behind pack version bump only (no automatic enablement).
- SRE/Telemetry will enable alerts for internal dogfood cohorts first (see ENG-5815).

## Checklist
- [x] Dashboard uses standard variable names and folder layout
- [x] Alerts are disabled by default and include runbook links
- [x] No PII fields referenced (tenant_id is only used in table panels and not emitted in alert labels)
- [x] Added docs for metric definitions and known caveats

source.title: Alert tuning: GPU saturation + queue depth (reduce noise, keep early warning)
source.author: Allison Grant
source.release_notes: Tuned GPU saturation + queue depth alerts to reduce paging from short-lived spikes and maintenance churn; added clearer annotations + links for incident-bot context cards.
source.body:
Context: Over the last ~6 weeks we’ve had repeated pages for brief GPU util spikes and transient scheduler queue growth (especially during deploy waves / regional rebalancing). These pages are noisy, but we still want early warning when we’re genuinely saturating and heading toward latency/5xx SLO burn. This PR tunes the GPU saturation + queue depth alert rules used by Hosted and Dedicated pools, aligning them with the standardized saturation signals that incident-bot v2 surfaces.

What’s changing (high level): (1) GPU util paging now requires persistence and corroboration (queue depth or admission/backpressure) instead of firing on short 1–2 minute spikes. (2) Added a warning tier for early detection (route/regional) and kept a stricter paging tier for sustained saturation. (3) Queue depth alerting is now based on a smoothed p95 + “time-above-threshold” gate and avoids firing during known noisy windows (autoscaler warm-up / drain) via an explicit label-based suppression rule. (4) Improved annotations: alert messages now include consistent deep links (saturation overview, scheduler queue dashboard, error budget burn link generator) and show the key dimensions incident-bot cards rely on (service, region, pool, capacity_tier).

Details: - GPU saturation: switched from avg(gpu_util) > 90% for 5m (page) to a two-tier approach. Warning: gpu_util_p95 > 85% for 10m AND scheduler_queue_depth_p95 > 0 (or admission_backpressure_rate > 0) with severity=warning. Page: gpu_util_p95 > 92% for 20m AND scheduler_queue_depth_p95 > 5 for 10m, scoped to pool_type in {hosted,dedicated} and excluding canary pools by default. - Queue depth: removed the previous “queue_depth > 10 for 5m” page (too spiky / low signal). New: warning on queue_depth_p95 > 8 for 10m, page on queue_depth_p95 > 15 for 15m with an additional condition that request_rate is non-trivial (to avoid alerting during low-traffic drift). - Suppression/inhibition: added inhibition so we don’t page on saturation if the region is in an active incident/maintenance annotation (existing label convention region_state=draining|maintenance), and so queue depth pages are inhibited when a higher-severity GPU saturation page is already firing for the same (service, region, pool).

Why these thresholds: based on the signal inventory + on-call feedback from recent incidents where responders found queue depth and KV cache pressure more actionable than raw GPU util. We also looked at the last 20 saturation-related pages and found >50% resolved in <7 minutes without mitigation; those should be warnings and/or show up in dashboards, not wake people up.

Testing/validation: - promtool rule tests added for key scenarios (spike vs sustained, low-traffic, canary pools). - backtested against 14 days of metrics in us-east + eu-west using the alert preview in Console; expected paging volume reduction ~30–40% while still firing during the known sustained saturation episodes (eu-west capacity event and two dedicated pool crunches).

Rollout plan: merge + ship with next observability-pack sync; SRE will monitor alert volume for 1 week and adjust if we see missed early warnings. This is part of the Incident Bot + diagnostics improvements initiative; the bot’s saturation card links/annotations expect these labels and dashboards.

Related: ENG-5423 (saturation signals into incident-bot), ENG-5424 (error budget burn links), INT-3341 (telemetry query timeouts led us to prefer stable aggregations), and the saturation signals standardization doc.
commits: alerting: introduce two-tier gpu saturation rules (warning/page)
alerting: rework queue depth thresholds + smoothing gates
alerting: add inhibition rules to reduce duplicate pages
annotations: standardize deep links + incident-bot label dims
tests: add promtool coverage for spike vs sustained cases
conversation:
Aisha Bello (review): Can we make sure the paging tier lines up with our latency SLO burn patterns? In prior incidents, util was high but latency was fine until queue depth got real. Suggest requiring queue_depth corroboration for page. Also: please add explicit annotation pointing to the SLO burn link generator for the same labels.

Allison Grant (author): Yep—updated paging tier to require queue_depth_p95 > 5 for 10m, and added the burn link in the page annotation (service/region/pool). Warning tier remains more sensitive for early heads-up.

Logan Wright (review): 👍 on smoothing. One concern: queue_depth_p95 aggregation might be expensive if we keep tenant label around. Please confirm tenant is NOT in the alert expression labels.

Allison Grant (author): Confirmed—alerts only label on service/region/pool/capacity_tier. No tenant dimension; expressions use pre-aggregated series from telemetry pipeline.

Miles Thornton (review): Can we exclude canary pools by default? Otherwise we’ll page during routine canary stress tests.

Allison Grant (author): Added pool_role!=canary to paging rules. Warning tier still includes canary so it shows up as early warning without paging.

Nadia Rahman (review): promtool tests look good. Can we add one case for low-traffic drift so we don’t alert when request_rate is ~0?

Allison Grant (author): Added test case: queue_depth>threshold with request_rate=0 should not fire.

Aisha Bello (final): Approved. Please ping #eng-sre after deploy so we can watch page volume and tweak if needed.
source.final_ci_summary: All checks passed (lint, promtool, unit-tests, dashboard validation).
source.notes: Follow-up: if we still see noisy warnings during autoscaler warm-up, consider adding an explicit warmup_window label from the autoscaler metrics to improve suppression accuracy.

source.title: Add rollout cohort panels (latency + errors) and alert templates
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.description:
Context: As part of Hosted canary rollout GA work, we need a standardized dashboard/alert baseline that makes it easy to compare baseline vs canary cohorts (latency + errors) without every team re-deriving PromQL and without blowing up cardinality. This PR adds a small set of reusable panels + a “rollout overview” dashboard to the observability pack, plus alert templates and recommended default thresholds for auto-stop/rollback wiring. What’s included: (1) New dashboard: “Hosted Rollouts / Cohort Comparison (Latency + Errors)” with panels for request rate, error rate, timeout rate, p50/p95 latency, and a baseline-vs-canary delta view. Panels are parameterized around {route, region, rollout_id} and assume cohort in {baseline, canary}. (2) Library panels (Jsonnet) that can be embedded into other service dashboards: p95 latency by cohort, 5xx rate by cohort, timeouts by cohort, and “canary/baseline ratio” panels (ratio and absolute delta). (3) Alert templates (PrometheusRule) for: a) canary 5xx rate regression (absolute + ratio guard), b) canary timeout regression, c) canary p95 latency regression, and d) “metrics missing / cohort tag missing” signal during an active rollout. Templates intentionally do NOT page by default; they are wired as warning-level with a short runbook link and intended to be used by the rollout orchestrator’s stop conditions / SRE playbooks. (4) Notes on cardinality and query safety: queries avoid grouping on rollout_id by default; most panels are scoped via dashboard variables and have explicit max series safeguards. Where we do present per-rollout series, we restrict via regex variable and add a “topk(20, …)” in examples. Implementation details: Metrics assumed (aligned with data plane tagging work) are: redwood_gateway_requests_total{route,region,cohort,rollout_id,status_class}, redwood_gateway_request_duration_seconds_bucket{route,region,cohort,rollout_id}, redwood_gateway_timeouts_total{route,region,cohort,rollout_id}. If your environment uses the older naming (rw_gateway_*), the dashboard variables include a compatibility toggle; see README section below. PromQL patterns: error_rate = sum(rate(requests_total{status_class=~"5.."}[5m])) / sum(rate(requests_total[5m])); latency_p95 = histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le,cohort) (rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))); regression guards use a combination of ratio (canary/baseline) and absolute delta to avoid false positives at low traffic. Suggested defaults (documented, not enforced): 5xx regression: canary_5xx_rate > baseline_5xx_rate + 0.5% AND canary/baseline > 1.5 for 5m with min canary RPS >= 0.2; timeouts: canary_timeout_rate > baseline_timeout_rate + 0.2% AND ratio > 2.0 for 5m with min canary RPS >= 0.2; p95 latency: canary_p95 > baseline_p95 + 250ms AND ratio > 1.25 for 10m with min canary RPS >= 0.2. These match current SRE beta tuning guidance and should be revisited before broad GA (see follow-ups). Files changed (high-level): grafana/dashboards/hosted_rollouts_cohort_comparison.jsonnet; grafana/lib/panels/rollout_cohort_latency.libsonnet; grafana/lib/panels/rollout_cohort_errors.libsonnet; prometheus/alerts/rollouts_cohort_regressions.yaml; prometheus/alerts/rollouts_metrics_missing.yaml; docs/rollouts/COHORT_DASHBOARDS.md; mixin/rollouts-mixin.jsonnet; mixin/rollouts-mixin.libsonnet; scripts/render_dashboards.sh (small updates). Screenshots: Included in docs/rollouts/COHORT_DASHBOARDS.md (rendered examples for us-east and eu-west). Rollout plan: ship in observability-pack v0.31.0; Redwood monorepo will vendor this version as part of the canary GA release checklist. Testing: jsonnet fmt + jsonnet render validation; promtool check rules; Grafana dashboard JSON schema validation in CI. Follow-ups (not in this PR): (a) Add a small “quality metrics” section when those signals become stable; (b) Expand panels for tokens/sec and cost per 1k tokens once telemetry joins those dimensions; (c) Confirm final threshold defaults after beta exit data is compiled.

Commits: 1) mixin(rollouts): add cohort comparison dashboard shell and variables 2) panels(rollouts): add latency cohort panels + delta/ratio views 3) panels(rollouts): add errors + timeouts cohort panels 4) alerts(rollouts): add regression templates + missing-metrics rule 5) docs(rollouts): cohort dashboard usage + cardinality guidance 6) ci: validate rendered dashboards + promtool checks

Review notes / conversation: Allison Grant: “Please make sure the default dashboard doesn’t accidentally enumerate rollout_id across tenants; require explicit variable selection.” -> Updated panels to avoid grouping by rollout_id by default, and made rollout_id variable opt-in with a strict regex + helper text. Thomas Baird: “The histogram_quantile query is missing a sum by (le) in one panel; also please keep the range window consistent (5m) unless we have a reason.” -> Fixed p95 query and standardized default windows; left p95 regression alert at 10m with rationale in comments. Sean Gallagher: “Threshold defaults look aggressive for low-traffic routes. Can we bake in a minimum RPS gate?” -> Added min canary RPS clause and documented it in docs; also changed alert severity to warning by default. Logan Wright: “Metric names differ between hosted gateway and data plane; please add a compatibility note.” -> Added docs note and a dashboard variable toggle for legacy names.

CI: Initial run failed promtool due to YAML indentation in rollouts_metrics_missing.yaml; fixed and re-ran. Final checks: jsonnet-lint PASS, render-dashboards PASS, promtool PASS, unit PASS.

Related: This is intended to be consumed alongside redwood PR-28819 (telemetry tagging rollout_id/cohort) and redwood PR-28910 (dashboards/alerts templates in monorepo); this PR provides the shared baseline pack versioned separately.
commits: mixin(rollouts): add cohort comparison dashboard shell and variables
panels(rollouts): add latency cohort panels + delta/ratio views
panels(rollouts): add errors + timeouts cohort panels
alerts(rollouts): add regression templates + missing-metrics rule
docs(rollouts): cohort dashboard usage + cardinality guidance
ci: validate rendered dashboards + promtool checks
source.release_notes: Observability pack: adds standard rollout cohort comparison dashboard (baseline vs canary) and warning-level alert templates for canary regressions (5xx/timeouts/p95 latency) with minimum-traffic guards.

Files changed (high-level): grafana/dashboards/hosted_rollouts_cohort_comparison.jsonnet; grafana/lib/panels/rollout_cohort_latency.libsonnet; grafana/lib/panels/rollout_cohort_errors.libsonnet; prometheus/alerts/rollouts_cohort_regressions.yaml; prometheus/alerts/rollouts_metrics_missing.yaml; docs/rollouts/COHORT_DASHBOARDS.md; mixin/rollouts-mixin.jsonnet; mixin/rollouts-mixin.libsonnet; scripts/render_dashboards.sh (small updates). Screenshots: Included in docs/rollouts/COHORT_DASHBOARDS.md (rendered examples for us-east and eu-west). Rollout plan: ship in observability-pack v0.31.0; Redwood monorepo will vendor this version as part of the canary GA release checklist. Testing: jsonnet fmt + jsonnet render validation; promtool check rules; Grafana dashboard JSON schema validation in CI. Follow-ups (not in this PR): (a) Add a small “quality metrics” section when those signals become stable; (b) Expand panels for tokens/sec and cost per 1k tokens once telemetry joins those dimensions; (c) Confirm final threshold defaults after beta exit data is compiled.

Commits: 1) mixin(rollouts): add cohort comparison dashboard shell and variables 2) panels(rollouts): add latency cohort panels + delta/ratio views 3) panels(rollouts): add errors + timeouts cohort panels 4) alerts(rollouts): add regression templates + missing-metrics rule 5) docs(rollouts): cohort dashboard usage + cardinality guidance 6) ci: validate rendered dashboards + promtool checks

Review notes / conversation: Allison Grant: “Please make sure the default dashboard doesn’t accidentally enumerate rollout_id across tenants; require explicit variable selection.” -> Updated panels to avoid grouping by rollout_id by default, and made rollout_id variable opt-in with a strict regex + helper text. Thomas Baird: “The histogram_quantile query is missing a sum by (le) in one panel; also please keep the range window consistent (5m) unless we have a reason.” -> Fixed p95 query and standardized default windows; left p95 regression alert at 10m with rationale in comments. Sean Gallagher: “Threshold defaults look aggressive for low-traffic routes. Can we bake in a minimum RPS gate?” -> Added min canary RPS clause and documented it in docs; also changed alert severity to warning by default. Logan Wright: “Metric names differ between hosted gateway and data plane; please add a compatibility note.” -> Added docs note and a dashboard variable toggle for legacy names.

CI: Initial run failed promtool due to YAML indentation in rollouts_metrics_missing.yaml; fixed and re-ran. Final checks: jsonnet-lint PASS, render-dashboards PASS, promtool PASS, unit PASS.

Related: This is intended to be consumed alongside redwood PR-28819 (telemetry tagging rollout_id/cohort) and redwood PR-28910 (dashboards/alerts templates in monorepo); this PR provides the shared baseline pack versioned separately.

source.title: Add Grafana dashboard: Route Experiment Guardrails (latency/error/cost + attribution drop)
source.author: Allison Grant
source.release_notes: Adds a standard Grafana dashboard for route experiment guardrails (p95/p99 latency, error rate, cost/token) and attribution drop rate, scoped by {route, experiment_id, variant, region}.
source.body:
## Summary This PR adds a standard Grafana dashboard for Route-level Experiments guardrails and data quality signals. The dashboard is intended to be deployed alongside the route experiments beta/GA so on-call and customers (where applicable) can quickly answer: (1) Is any variant regressing on latency, error rate, or cost? (2) Are we missing experiment attribution (drop rate) such that results are untrustworthy? (3) Is traffic split behaving as expected (e.g., variant getting ~0% traffic)?

This dashboard complements the control-plane/Console views by providing a fast, infrastructure-centric view over raw telemetry, and is designed to work with the canonical attribution dimensions from ADR-048 (route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region, model_id) while limiting cardinality blast-radius.

## What’s included ### Dashboard: "Route Experiments / Guardrails" Panels (high-level): - Overview row: total RPS, total requests, error rate (5xx + 429 separately), and attribution coverage.
- Latency guardrails: p50/p95/p99 request latency by variant (timeseries + stat) with a "% delta vs control" table.
- Error guardrails: error rate by variant (5xx and 429 broken out), plus "top error codes" if available.
- Cost guardrails: $/1K output tokens and $/request by variant (based on Redwood cost attribution metrics).
- Throughput: tokens/sec and requests/sec by variant (useful for distinguishing capacity issues from model behavior).
- Traffic split sanity: % of traffic per variant; includes a "variant ~0 traffic" panel to catch allocation/eligibility mistakes.
- Data quality: experiment attribution drop rate (requests missing experiment_id/variant_id when they should be present), plus a "streaming retry attribution" hint panel (links to runbook section).

### Template variables - env (prod/staging) - region - route_id (normalized route template id; NOT raw path) - experiment_id - variant_id - model_id (optional; default All)

### Data sources / metric expectations This dashboard assumes the router/runtime emits experiment dimensions on key metrics and/or spans, as implemented in the core monorepo (see redwood PRs: request context plumbing + OTel tagging). The queries here are written to tolerate partial rollout by falling back to "All variants" if variant tags are absent.

Primary metrics used (names reflect current conventions in the pack; see README changes for exact mapping): - redwood_request_latency_ms_bucket{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region} - redwood_requests_total{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region, status_class} - redwood_tokens_output_total{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region} - redwood_cost_usd_total{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region} - redwood_experiment_attribution_missing_total{route_id, region, reason} and redwood_requests_total (used as denominator)

## Why / context - We’ve had multiple beta issues where Console results were confusing or appeared stale, and on-call needed a quick way to validate (a) attribution integrity and (b) whether a regression was real vs. a data artifact (ref: SUP-24910).
- SRE asked for a standardized dashboard that can be installed for every region/environment with consistent panels and variable naming so runbooks/alerts can deep-link reliably.
- The attribution drop rate panels are explicitly included because missing experiment_id/variant_id has been a recurring failure mode (streaming retries, async spans, and header/sticky-key issues).

## Implementation notes - Cardinality: the dashboard does NOT group by raw request path. It uses normalized route_id (template) and requires users to select an experiment_id for most by-variant panels. We avoid “group by {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region}” in single queries unless the user is already scoped down.
- Cost metrics: panels use $/1K output tokens and $/request. This avoids misleading comparisons for variants with different output length distributions.
- Control variant: the “delta vs control” table assumes variant_id="control" when present; otherwise it picks the lowest-sorted variant_id as a fallback. This is called out in the panel description so it doesn’t silently mislead.

## Changed files - grafana/dashboards/route-experiments/route-experiments-guardrails.jsonnet: new dashboard definition - grafana/dashboards/route-experiments/route-experiments-guardrails.dashboard.json: rendered dashboard - grafana/dashboards/route-experiments/README.md: usage notes, required labels, variable conventions - grafana/library-panels/latency-percentiles-by-variant.jsonnet: extracted reusable panel - grafana/library-panels/attribution-drop-rate.jsonnet: extracted reusable panel - CHANGELOG.md: entry for the new dashboard

## Screenshots (described) - "Latency (p95/p99) by variant" timeseries: color-coded variants, with control highlighted - "Attribution drop rate" stat: shows % missing attribution + reason breakdown in a table

## Test plan - Rendered dashboards via jsonnet build (make dashboards) - Validated PromQL in staging against synthetic traffic + a known experiment_id (confirmed series appear with expected labels) - Verified variable dropdowns do not time out when unscoped (route_id and experiment_id are optional until selecting per-variant panels)

## Follow-ups (not in this PR) - Alerts live in a separate PR (pr-1942) to keep review surface smaller and allow independent tuning.
- Add runbook deep links once URLs are finalized (placeholder links included as annotations for now).

## Checklist - [x] Dashboard renders (jsonnet + rendered json committed) - [x] Variables align with attribution schema (route_id/experiment_id/variant_id/region) - [x] Panel descriptions call out assumptions (control selection, partial rollout behavior) - [x] Changelog entry added
commits: grafana: add route experiments guardrails dashboard (jsonnet + rendered json)
grafana: extract latency percentiles by variant library panel
grafana: add attribution drop rate library panel + README notes
docs: update changelog for route experiments dashboard
review_conversation: Logan Wright (Telemetry): PromQL looks good, but can we avoid grouping by model_id by default? That dimension is optional and can explode in some tenants.
Allison Grant (Author): Good call. I updated the dashboard so model_id is a variable defaulting to All, and the by-variant panels do not group by model_id unless explicitly selected.
Rafael Mendes (SRE): Please add an explicit panel for “variant 0 traffic” / “unexpected split” so we catch misconfig + eligibility issues quickly.
Allison Grant (Author): Added a Traffic Split row with % per variant and a "near-zero traffic" stat that flags any variant below 0.5% (configurable via dashboard constant).
Aisha Bello (SRE): For attribution drop rate, can we show both absolute missing req/s and %? Percent alone hides volume spikes.
Allison Grant (Author): Done. Added a small timeseries for missing req/s and kept the % stat as the headline.
Caleb Johnson (Ingestion): Please document the expected metric names/labels in README and note that some panels will be empty until router tagging is fully rolled out.
Allison Grant (Author): Added a "Required metrics" section + a "Partial rollout" note in README and panel descriptions.
Logan Wright (Telemetry): Approved.
Rafael Mendes (SRE): Approved after latest push.
Aisha Bello (SRE): Approved.

source.title: Add ingestion-side cardinality guards for cache telemetry
source.author: Caleb Johnson
source.release_notes:
- Enforced cardinality policy for cache telemetry at ingestion (drop/aggregate disallowed label keys/values)
- Added visibility metrics for dropped/rewritten series and a small dashboard panel stub for monitoring guard impact
source.body:
## Summary
This PR implements ingestion-side cardinality guards for the new prefix/KV cache telemetry emitted by the runtime and tagged by the gateway. The goal is to prevent accidental series explosions (per-tenant/per-route/per-request-shape label combinations) while preserving the ability to break down cache signals by the dimensions we actually operate on (model, route, deployment mode, region, and prompt-length/token bins).

This is the ingestion counterpart to runtime tagging + allowlists (see redwood/pr-18426) and ensures that even if a client or misconfigured gateway emits unexpected label keys/values, we either (a) rewrite to a safe aggregated bucket, or (b) drop with explicit counters so we can diagnose.

## Context / Motivation
During early canary of cache telemetry dashboards we saw a few failure modes:
- `tenant_id` and raw `route` occasionally showing up due to legacy gateway mappings -> high cardinality.
- model/version fields not consistently normalized -> multiple spellings causing unnecessary series multiplication.
- prompt/token histogram tags are safe only when bucketed; unbucketed token counts are not.

This PR codifies the policy from the cache metrics standards doc and makes the enforcement explicit in the ingestion pipeline. It also addresses SUP-3927 (customer saw "zero hits" because the old temporary guard was dropping whole samples without surfacing why).

## What this PR does
- Adds a cache-telemetry-specific label guard stage in the metrics ingestion pipeline (remote-write receiver path).
- Enforces an allowlist of label keys for cache metrics (by metric name prefix `redwood_cache_*`).
- Enforces normalization/rewrites for a subset of label values:
  - `deployment_mode`: normalize to `hosted|dedicated|private|unknown`
  - `region`: normalize to our region IDs; unknown -> `unknown`
  - `model_family` / `model`: require match to known catalog pattern; unknown -> `other`
  - `model_version`: if missing or malformed -> `unknown` (do not attempt to parse arbitrary hashes)
  - `route_id`: allow only gateway-generated stable IDs (format `rt_*`); otherwise -> `unknown`
  - `prompt_len_bucket`, `prefill_tokens_bucket`, `decode_tokens_bucket`: must be one of the configured buckets; otherwise -> `overflow`
- Drops samples when disallowed label keys are present (e.g. `tenant_id`, `user_id`, `request_id`, `prompt_hash`, `raw_route`) rather than attempting to partially salvage (to avoid implicit joins that look valid but aren’t).
- Adds explicit ingestion visibility metrics:
  - `redwood_metrics_guard_dropped_samples_total{reason,metric}`
  - `redwood_metrics_guard_rewritten_samples_total{rewrite,metric}`
  - `redwood_metrics_guard_active{guard="cache_cardinality"}=1`
- Adds a small dashboard snippet (panel-only) for guard drop/rewrites so SRE can quickly determine whether a hit-rate dip is real vs missing telemetry.

## Policy encoded (high level)
- Cache metrics are allowed to be sliced by: `model`, `model_family`, `model_version` (coarse), `route_id` (stable), `deployment_mode`, `region`, and bucketed request shape dimensions.
- Cache metrics are NOT allowed to include per-tenant labels at ingestion. Tenant/route/customer views must be served via analytics aggregates or controlled rollups, not raw high-cardinality metrics.

## Implementation notes
- Guard runs as part of the ingest pipeline stage `LabelGuardProcessor` before relabeling + storage write.
- Rules are applied only to a configured metric name prefix list (currently cache metrics only). Non-cache metrics are unaffected.
- Drop vs rewrite strategy:
  - Disallowed label keys -> drop sample (reason: `forbidden_label_key`)
  - Missing required labels -> rewrite to `unknown` where safe; do not drop (reason: `missing_required_label` increments rewrite counter)
  - Out-of-range bucket values -> rewrite to `overflow`

## Tests
- Unit tests for:
  - forbidden keys cause drops + correct reason counter
  - value normalization + rewrite counters
  - bucket enforcement
  - metric scoping (only applies to `redwood_cache_*`)
- Golden tests for a few representative runtime metric payloads (KV hit/miss, prefix reuse histograms, eviction pressure gauges).

## Rollout / Safety
- Default enabled in staging; prod rollout is controlled via values.yaml flag `ingest.guards.cacheCardinality.enabled`.
- Adds a hard cap for drop logging to avoid log storms.
- Includes a runbook note link in the dashboard panel description.

## Follow-ups
- Align with gateway migration plan to ensure `route_id` is always stable and backfilled for older routes (ENG-4823).
- Consider adding a controlled, sampled per-tenant view in analytics for Dedicated customers (separate effort).

## Screenshots
N/A (panel stub only; full dashboard work is in pr-612).

## Commits (squash summary)
- ingest: add cache metric label allowlist + enforcement hooks
- ingest: add drop/rewrite counters and reasons
- charts: wire guard config flags + defaults for staging
- tests: add unit/golden coverage for cache guard
- dashboards: add guard health panel stub

## Changed areas / files (high level)
- services/metrics-ingest/guard/cache_guard.go
- services/metrics-ingest/guard/cache_guard_test.go
- services/metrics-ingest/config/guards.yaml
- charts/metrics-ingest/values.yaml
- charts/metrics-ingest/templates/configmap.yaml
- dashboards/cache/cache-telemetry-guard-overview.json

---
## Review conversation (highlights)
**Nadia Rahman:** Can we avoid dropping the whole sample when a forbidden key exists? Maybe strip it and continue.
**Caleb Johnson (author):** I’d prefer drop. Stripping silently makes the metric look valid but changes join semantics; also it encourages accidental leakage. Added explicit counters + panel so we can detect and fix upstream quickly.

**Allison Grant:** Please add a single panel we can pin to the cache dashboards to answer "are we dropping cache telemetry right now?"
**Caleb:** Added `cache-telemetry-guard-overview` panel with drop/rewrite rate and top reasons.

**Sean Gallagher:** Make sure unknown region/model collapse doesn’t mask real regressions.
**Caleb:** Agree; rewrites are only for malformed values, not for legitimate new regions/models. Also emitting rewritten counters so we can see when we’re collapsing.

**Logan Wright:** Need a specific reason for SUP-3927: label-drop misconfig caused all cache metrics to disappear.
**Caleb:** Added reason taxonomy and ensured we never drop due to "missing model_version" — we rewrite to `unknown` so the metric still shows up.

Approvals:
- Nadia Rahman: Approved (requested dashboard panel; addressed)
- Allison Grant: Approved
- Sean Gallagher: Approved (rollout flag + visibility metrics)
- Logan Wright: Approved

## CI
- unit-tests: pass
- lint: pass
- helm-template: pass
- integration-smoke (staging): pass

Merged via squash to `main`.

  - `prompt_len_bucket`, `prefill_tokens_bucket`, `decode_tokens_bucket`: must be one of the configured buckets; otherwise -> `overflow`
- Drops samples when disallowed label keys are present (e.g. `tenant_id`, `user_id`, `request_id`, `prompt_hash`, `raw_route`) rather than attempting to partially salvage (to avoid implicit joins that look valid but aren’t).
- Adds explicit ingestion visibility metrics:
  - `redwood_metrics_guard_dropped_samples_total{reason,metric}`
  - `redwood_metrics_guard_rewritten_samples_total{rewrite,metric}`
  - `redwood_metrics_guard_active{guard="cache_cardinality"}=1`
- Adds a small dashboard snippet (panel-only) for guard drop/rewrites so SRE can quickly determine whether a hit-rate dip is real vs missing telemetry.

## Policy encoded (high level)
- Cache metrics are allowed to be sliced by: `model`, `model_family`, `model_version` (coarse), `route_id` (stable), `deployment_mode`, `region`, and bucketed request shape dimensions.
- Cache metrics are NOT allowed to include per-tenant labels at ingestion. Tenant/route/customer views must be served via analytics aggregates or controlled rollups, not raw high-cardinality metrics.

## Implementation notes
- Guard runs as part of the ingest pipeline stage `LabelGuardProcessor` before relabeling + storage write.
- Rules are applied only to a configured metric name prefix list (currently cache metrics only). Non-cache metrics are unaffected.
- Drop vs rewrite strategy:
  - Disallowed label keys -> drop sample (reason: `forbidden_label_key`)
  - Missing required labels -> rewrite to `unknown` where safe; do not drop (reason: `missing_required_label` increments rewrite counter)
  - Out-of-range bucket values -> rewrite to `overflow`

## Tests
- Unit tests for:
  - forbidden keys cause drops + correct reason counter
  - value normalization + rewrite counters
  - bucket enforcement
  - metric scoping (only applies to `redwood_cache_*`)
- Golden tests for a few representative runtime metric payloads (KV hit/miss, prefix reuse histograms, eviction pressure gauges).

## Rollout / Safety
- Default enabled in staging; prod rollout is controlled via values.yaml flag `ingest.guards.cacheCardinality.enabled`.
- Adds a hard cap for drop logging to avoid log storms.
- Includes a runbook note link in the dashboard panel description.

## Follow-ups
- Align with gateway migration plan to ensure `route_id` is always stable and backfilled for older routes (ENG-4823).
- Consider adding a controlled, sampled per-tenant view in analytics for Dedicated customers (separate effort).

## Screenshots
N/A (panel stub only; full dashboard work is in pr-612).

## Commits (squash summary)
- ingest: add cache metric label allowlist + enforcement hooks
- ingest: add drop/rewrite counters and reasons
- charts: wire guard config flags + defaults for staging
- tests: add unit/golden coverage for cache guard
- dashboards: add guard health panel stub

## Changed areas / files (high level)
- services/metrics-ingest/guard/cache_guard.go
- services/metrics-ingest/guard/cache_guard_test.go
- services/metrics-ingest/config/guards.yaml
- charts/metrics-ingest/values.yaml
- charts/metrics-ingest/templates/configmap.yaml
- dashboards/cache/cache-telemetry-guard-overview.json

---
## Review conversation (highlights)
**Nadia Rahman:** Can we avoid dropping the whole sample when a forbidden key exists? Maybe strip it and continue.
**Caleb Johnson (author):** I’d prefer drop. Stripping silently makes the metric look valid but changes join semantics; also it encourages accidental leakage. Added explicit counters + panel so we can detect and fix upstream quickly.

**Allison Grant:** Please add a single panel we can pin to the cache dashboards to answer "are we dropping cache telemetry right now?"
**Caleb:** Added `cache-telemetry-guard-overview` panel with drop/rewrite rate and top reasons.

**Sean Gallagher:** Make sure unknown region/model collapse doesn’t mask real regressions.
**Caleb:** Agree; rewrites are only for malformed values, not for legitimate new regions/models. Also emitting rewritten counters so we can see when we’re collapsing.

**Logan Wright:** Need a specific reason for SUP-3927: label-drop misconfig caused all cache metrics to disappear.
**Caleb:** Added reason taxonomy and ensured we never drop due to "missing model_version" — we rewrite to `unknown` so the metric still shows up.

Approvals:
- Nadia Rahman: Approved (requested dashboard panel; addressed)
- Allison Grant: Approved
- Sean Gallagher: Approved (rollout flag + visibility metrics)
- Logan Wright: Approved

## CI
- unit-tests: pass
- lint: pass
- helm-template: pass
- integration-smoke (staging): pass

Merged via squash to `main`.
text:
## Summary
This PR implements ingestion-side cardinality guards for the new prefix/KV cache telemetry emitted by the runtime and tagged by the gateway. The goal is to prevent accidental series explosions (per-tenant/per-route/per-request-shape label combinations) while preserving the ability to break down cache signals by the dimensions we actually operate on (model, route, deployment mode, region, and prompt-length/token bins).

This is the ingestion counterpart to runtime tagging + allowlists (see redwood/pr-18426) and ensures that even if a client or misconfigured gateway emits unexpected label keys/values, we either (a) rewrite to a safe aggregated bucket, or (b) drop with explicit counters so we can diagnose.

## Context / Motivation
During early canary of cache telemetry dashboards we saw a few failure modes:
- `tenant_id` and raw `route` occasionally showing up due to legacy gateway mappings -> high cardinality.
- model/version fields not consistently normalized -> multiple spellings causing unnecessary series multiplication.
- prompt/token histogram tags are safe only when bucketed; unbucketed token counts are not.

This PR codifies the policy from the cache metrics standards doc and makes the enforcement explicit in the ingestion pipeline. It also addresses SUP-3927 (customer saw "zero hits" because the old temporary guard was dropping whole samples without surfacing why).

## What this PR does
- Adds a cache-telemetry-specific label guard stage in the metrics ingestion pipeline (remote-write receiver path).
- Enforces an allowlist of label keys for cache metrics (by metric name prefix `redwood_cache_*`).
- Enforces normalization/rewrites for a subset of label values:
  - `deployment_mode`: normalize to `hosted|dedicated|private|unknown`
  - `region`: normalize to our region IDs; unknown -> `unknown`
  - `model_family` / `model`: require match to known catalog pattern; unknown -> `other`
  - `model_version`: if missing or malformed -> `unknown` (do not attempt to parse arbitrary hashes)
  - `route_id`: allow only gateway-generated stable IDs (format `rt_*`); otherwise -> `unknown`
  - `prompt_len_bucket`, `prefill_tokens_bucket`, `decode_tokens_bucket`: must be one of the configured buckets; otherwise -> `overflow`
- Drops samples when disallowed label keys are present (e.g. `tenant_id`, `user_id`, `request_id`, `prompt_hash`, `raw_route`) rather than attempting to partially salvage (to avoid implicit joins that look valid but aren’t).
- Adds explicit ingestion visibility metrics:
  - `redwood_metrics_guard_dropped_samples_total{reason,metric}`
  - `redwood_metrics_guard_rewritten_samples_total{rewrite,metric}`
  - `redwood_metrics_guard_active{guard="cache_cardinality"}=1`
- Adds a small dashboard snippet (panel-only) for guard drop/rewrites so SRE can quickly determine whether a hit-rate dip is real vs missing telemetry.

## Policy encoded (high level)
- Cache metrics are allowed to be sliced by: `model`, `model_family`, `model_version` (coarse), `route_id` (stable), `deployment_mode`, `region`, and bucketed request shape dimensions.
- Cache metrics are NOT allowed to include per-tenant labels at ingestion. Tenant/route/customer views must be served via analytics aggregates or controlled rollups, not raw high-cardinality metrics.

## Implementation notes
- Guard runs as part of the ingest pipeline stage `LabelGuardProcessor` before relabeling + storage write.
- Rules are applied only to a configured metric name prefix list (currently cache metrics only). Non-cache metrics are unaffected.
- Drop vs rewrite strategy:
  - Disallowed label keys -> drop sample (reason: `forbidden_label_key`)
  - Missing required labels -> rewrite to `unknown` where safe; do not drop (reason: `missing_required_label` increments rewrite counter)
  - Out-of-range bucket values -> rewrite to `overflow`

## Tests
- Unit tests for:
  - forbidden keys cause drops + correct reason counter
  - value normalization + rewrite counters
  - bucket enforcement
  - metric scoping (only applies to `redwood_cache_*`)
- Golden tests for a few representative runtime metric payloads (KV hit/miss, prefix reuse histograms, eviction pressure gauges).

## Rollout / Safety
- Default enabled in staging; prod rollout is controlled via values.yaml flag `ingest.guards.cacheCardinality.enabled`.
- Adds a hard cap for drop logging to avoid log storms.
- Includes a runbook note link in the dashboard panel description.

## Follow-ups
- Align with gateway migration plan to ensure `route_id` is always stable and backfilled for older routes (ENG-4823).
- Consider adding a controlled, sampled per-tenant view in analytics for Dedicated customers (separate effort).

## Screenshots
N/A (panel stub only; full dashboard work is in pr-612).

## Commits (squash summary)
- ingest: add cache metric label allowlist + enforcement hooks
- ingest: add drop/rewrite counters and reasons
- charts: wire guard config flags + defaults for staging
- tests: add unit/golden coverage for cache guard
- dashboards: add guard health panel stub

## Changed areas / files (high level)
- services/metrics-ingest/guard/cache_guard.go
- services/metrics-ingest/guard/cache_guard_test.go
- services/metrics-ingest/config/guards.yaml
- charts/metrics-ingest/values.yaml
- charts/metrics-ingest/templates/configmap.yaml
- dashboards/cache/cache-telemetry-guard-overview.json

---
## Review conversation (highlights)
**Nadia Rahman:** Can we avoid dropping the whole sample when a forbidden key exists? Maybe strip it and continue.
**Caleb Johnson (author):** I’d prefer drop. Stripping silently makes the metric look valid but changes join semantics; also it encourages accidental leakage. Added explicit counters + panel so we can detect and fix upstream quickly.

**Allison Grant:** Please add a single panel we can pin to the cache dashboards to answer "are we dropping cache telemetry right now?"
**Caleb:** Added `cache-telemetry-guard-overview` panel with drop/rewrite rate and top reasons.

**Sean Gallagher:** Make sure unknown region/model collapse doesn’t mask real regressions.
**Caleb:** Agree; rewrites are only for malformed values, not for legitimate new regions/models. Also emitting rewritten counters so we can see when we’re collapsing.

**Logan Wright:** Need a specific reason for SUP-3927: label-drop misconfig caused all cache metrics to disappear.
**Caleb:** Added reason taxonomy and ensured we never drop due to "missing model_version" — we rewrite to `unknown` so the metric still shows up.

Approvals:
- Nadia Rahman: Approved (requested dashboard panel; addressed)
- Allison Grant: Approved
- Sean Gallagher: Approved (rollout flag + visibility metrics)
- Logan Wright: Approved

## CI
- unit-tests: pass
- lint: pass
- helm-template: pass
- integration-smoke (staging): pass

Merged via squash to `main`.

source.title: Add standardized incident-context dashboards (deploy timeline, saturation overview, error budget burn)
source.author: Allison Grant
source.description: This PR adds a standardized set of dashboards intended to be linked directly from Incident Bot v2 context cards. The goal is to make the bot output actionable in the first 5 minutes by providing consistent deep links (per service/region/route/time window) for deploy correlation, saturation triage, and SLO/error-budget burn. The dashboards are designed to be safe/default-bounded (limited time range defaults, low-cardinality variables, and guardrails around tenant labels) and to work across Hosted + Dedicated pools where the standard metrics exist. Included dashboards: (1) Deploy Timeline (service/region) — deploy events overlay (canary vs full rollout), build SHA, and error/latency overlays; (2) Saturation Overview (pool/region) — GPU util, queue depth, KV cache pressure, scheduler backpressure/admission, and request concurrency; (3) Error Budget Burn (service/route/region) — 1h/6h burn rates, SLO indicator panels, and links back to the SLO Toolkit generator. Deep-link contract: all dashboards accept variables service, region, env, route (optional), pool (optional), and incident_window (defaults to last 60m). This PR intentionally does not add new metrics; it assumes the standardized saturation metrics and deploy events exist (or will be rolled out) and focuses on consistent visualization + linkability. Follow-ups: we may need to adjust recording rules once the bot beta cohort expands and we get real-world query load; also expect small iterations after the bot UX card text is finalized (panel names are aligned with current card headings).
commits: dashboards: add incident_context_deploy_timeline (template + panels)
dashboards: add incident_context_saturation_overview (GPU/queue/KV/scheduler)
dashboards: add incident_context_error_budget_burn (burn rates + SLO links)
recording: add low-cardinality rollups for saturation panels
docs: add deep-link variable contract + usage notes for incident-bot
conversation: Allison Grant (author): Primary intent is to match Incident Bot v2 card headings and ensure links land on the right default time range. I kept variables minimal to avoid cardinality explosions; tenant is intentionally not a variable. If you need tenant scoping, use the Console deep link for tenant-level views.

Logan Wright (review): Love the alignment with the bot cards. Two requests: (1) please avoid using `pod` / `instance` in any default query (seen this bite us), and (2) ensure queue depth panels use the standardized `scheduler_queue_depth` metric name rather than legacy `queue_len` where possible.

Allison Grant (author): Updated all panels to use recording rules; removed any instance-level selectors and replaced with pool/region rollups. Also swapped to `redwood_scheduler_queue_depth` + added a fallback expression only when the new series is missing (explicitly labeled as legacy).

Aisha Bello (review): On error budget dashboard: can we standardize the burn panels to 1h and 6h only (consistent with SRE playbook), and include the current SLO target (e.g., 99.9%) as a stat panel? Also please confirm the default time range is 60m to match the incident context card.

Allison Grant (author): Done. Burn panels are 1h/6h, and added an SLO target stat (pulled from the SLO recording series where available). Default time range set to 60m; the README documents `incident_window` mapping to `from/to` overrides.

Miles Thornton (review): For bot integration: panel titles should match card labels exactly (e.g., "Recent deploys" card links to "Deploy timeline"). Can you make sure the dashboard title prefix is stable? We’re going to hardcode the slug in the bot link generator for now.

Allison Grant (author): Renamed dashboards with stable prefix `Incident Context / ...` and documented slugs + UID mapping in README. Also kept panel headings consistent with the current card copy from the UX mocks.

Caleb Johnson (review): Recording rules: please confirm label set is bounded. I see `route` in a couple rollups. That’s OK if we cap it; can we add a note that route breakdown is optional and should default off in high-volume services?

Allison Grant (author): Good catch. Updated route-level panels to be collapsed by default and added a top-of-dashboard note: enable route variable only when needed. Recording rules keep `route` only for a curated allowlist of gateway routes (documented in the YAML comments).

Connor O'Brien (review): Deploy timeline dashboard: where are deploy events coming from? We’ve had gaps in some regions. Can you guard the overlay so the dashboard still renders without deploy markers?

Allison Grant (author): Yes — deploy markers are optional annotations. If deploy events are missing the timeline still renders latency/5xx panels; annotation query is isolated so failures don’t break the rest of the dashboard.
Logan Wright (review): Approving. Thanks for the bounded queries + documentation. Please follow up with a quick load test once this is used by the SRE rotation.
Aisha Bello (review): Approved once the README includes the deep link examples (service+region+last_60m).
Allison Grant (author): Added deep link examples and parameter table. Marking as ready.
source.release_notes: Internal-only: Adds Incident Context dashboards intended for Incident Bot v2 deep links (Deploy timeline, Saturation overview, Error budget burn).

source.title: Alerts: experiment config propagation lag + attribution anomalies (route experiments)
source.author: Allison Grant
source.description: Context: Route-level experiments rely on control-plane config propagation into the routing/policy layer. During beta we observed occasional propagation lag and a few classes of attribution anomalies (missing experiment_id/variant_id tags, variant receiving ~0 traffic due to config/eligibility issues, and split skew early in rollout). This PR adds Prometheus/Alertmanager rules and annotations for the standard route experiments dashboards, with SRE-reviewed runbook links and recommended severities. Scope: (1) propagation lag/staleness alerts tied to the new propagation lag metric emitted by the redwood monorepo, (2) attribution drop-rate and missing-dimension alerts for experiment metrics/traces aggregation, (3) “variant 0 traffic” detection to catch misconfigured allocations or eligibility rules. Notes: rules are written to avoid cardinality blow-ups (no per-route fanout by default); high-cardinality drilldowns remain in Grafana dashboards. Related: redwood PR-28631 (adds config propagation lag metric), redwood PR-28734 (runbook URLs in alert payloads), observability-pack PR-1934 (dashboards). Threads: slack/eng-sre/1736455521-alert-thresholds-experiment-propagation-lag.json and slack/eng-sre/1738362044-attribution-drop-rate-alert-noise.json informed thresholds/windows. Rollout: enable in staging immediately; prod enabled behind the existing observability-pack release gate (see release notes section).
commits: prometheus: add route experiments alert groups for propagation lag + attribution anomalies
alert annotations: add runbook + console deep-link templates
tuning: adjust windows/severity for attribution drop rate to reduce noise
docs: add brief README snippet for route experiments alert ownership + escalation
source.checks: buildkite/observability-pack-test: PASS; buildkite/lint-yaml: PASS; buildkite/validate-prometheus-rules: PASS; buildkite/render-alertmanager-templates: PASS; grafana-jsonnet-validate: PASS
source.review_conversation: Sean Gallagher (review): Thresholds look reasonable, but please ensure paging severity only fires when we have evidence of user-impact. For propagation lag: can we differentiate config push stalled vs read-side staleness? Also please include runbook links as full URLs (not relative) and confirm the runbook has a clear “disable experiments feature flag / soft-stop” mitigation. Allison Grant (author): Updated severities: propagation lag is page only for >10m sustained + >X% of regions impacted; otherwise ticket. Added separate warning for read-side staleness vs publish-side lag based on the two metrics exposed in redwood PR-28631. Runbook link updated to full Confluence URL and added a ‘Mitigation: set experiments evaluator to safe-default + disable auto-stop kill switch’ section reference. Rafael Mendes (review): Attribution drop-rate alert window was too short; in previous incident it flapped on deploys. Suggest 15m window and a higher threshold for warning, plus a distinct alert for “attribution missing on streaming retries” if we can approximate it. Also please add an annotation pointing to the assignment debugging endpoint docs. Allison Grant (author): Adjusted to 15m window; warning at 2.5% sustained, critical at 7% sustained (per staging baselines). Added annotation to internal debug endpoint docs and the assignment debugging runbook. We can’t reliably isolate streaming retries here without new labels; added TODO + linked ENG-9026 follow-up. Logan Wright (review): Please confirm recording rule names align with metrics taxonomy notes and do not include route_id by default. Allison Grant (author): Confirmed: recording rules aggregate by {experiment_id, variant_id, region} and optionally by route_id only in dashboard queries. Also added comment blocks warning against expanding label sets in alerts. Final: Sean + Rafael approved after a quick live review of runbook steps and alert routing.
source.alert_rules_summary: Added alert groups: RouteExperimentsControlPlane and RouteExperimentsAttribution. New alerts: (1) RouteExperimentsConfigPropagationLagHigh (warning) and RouteExperimentsConfigPropagationLagCritical (page) based on propagation_lag_seconds p95 and percentiles across regions; (2) RouteExperimentsConfigStalenessHigh (warning) when evaluator uses stale config versions beyond SLO; (3) RouteExperimentsAttributionDropRateHigh (warning/critical) when attribution_drop_rate > thresholds; (4) RouteExperimentsVariantZeroTraffic (warning) when a variant receives <0.5% traffic for >20m while allocation is >=5%; (5) RouteExperimentsSplitSkewSuspected (ticket) when observed vs expected allocation diverges beyond a tolerance band for >30m (best-effort heuristic). All alerts include annotations: runbook_url, owner, and console_experiment_link template.
source.release_notes: Observability: Added standard Alertmanager rules for route experiments propagation lag and attribution anomalies, with tuned thresholds and runbook links (beta readiness).
