source.title: Add recommended alert rules + SLO examples for Quickstart Template apps
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.release_notes:
- Added opt-in alerting rules for quickstart template apps (RAG + agents)
- Added SLO example recording rules and burn-rate alerts
- Added guidance on avoiding noisy/high-cardinality alerting defaults
source.description: Context: As part of the Quickstart Templates launch, we want to ship a recommended alerting baseline that users can enable without getting paged to death. This PR adds an opt-in alert rule group for template apps (RAG/agents) plus example SLOs and burn-rate alerts. The rules are designed around the metrics emitted by the templates + OTel collector configs from PR-156/158, and they intentionally avoid high-cardinality dimensions (no user_id, prompt hash, raw path, or tool args). What’s included: (1) SLO examples: availability + latency SLO recording rules with suggested targets and notes on how to adapt per workload; (2) Alert rules: multi-window burn-rate (fast/slow) alerts for the SLOs, plus a small set of “hard failures” (collector down, exporter errors) and “symptom alerts” (tool failure spikes, streaming disconnect spikes, retrieval empty-result spikes) with conservative defaults; (3) Noise avoidance guidance: documentation on paging vs ticketing alerts, minimum traffic gating, for/hold durations, and recommended grouping to avoid alert storms during deploys/traffic spikes. Rollout: Alerts are opt-in via the template dashboards pack jsonnet entrypoint; we’re not enabling anything by default in the published Grafana Cloud pack. Related: ENG-4184 (observability pack integration), ENG-4186 (smoke tests incl. streaming stability), and the templates’ error taxonomy work (draft).
commits: alerts: add template SLO recording rules + burn rate helper macros
alerts: add template-app rule group (availability/latency/tool/retrieval/streaming)
docs: add guidance for enabling alerts + noise controls
ci: validate alert rule files with promtool in workflow
source.conversation:
Allison Grant (review): Please make sure none of the alert expressions include high-cardinality labels. In particular, I see `tool_name` in the tool failure alert. That’s borderline—can we drop it from the alert labels and keep it only in annotations (or not at all) to avoid per-tool alert fanout?

Sergio Costa (author): Good catch. Updated to remove `tool_name` from alert grouping/labels. Keeping a top-N panel in the dashboard for tool_name breakdown; alerts now fire per service/env only. Added a note in README about safe label sets.

Christine Wong (review): Thresholds: the latency burn-rate is currently based on p95>2s for 10m. That’s going to be noisy for small traffic. Can we gate on request volume and use multi-window burn rate instead of a single window? Also, please add explicit severities and a runbook link annotation.

Sergio Costa (author): Updated to multi-window burn rate (5m/1h page + 30m/6h ticket) using the SLO recording rule error budget. Added `min_requests` gating (skip firing if < 50 req in the fast window). Added `severity` labels and `runbook_url` annotations across the group.

Caleb Johnson (review): For the collector/exporter alert, consider differentiating between "collector down" vs "exporter failing". Collector down is usually a scrape issue; exporter failing is often auth/endpoint misconfig. Also please avoid alerting on transient 401s at startup.

Sergio Costa (author): Split into two alerts: `OTelCollectorDown` uses `up{job="otel-collector"}==0` with a 10m `for`. Exporter failing uses a rate of `otelcol_exporter_send_failed_spans` with `for: 15m` and volume gating. Also added a suppression note for first 5 minutes after rollout in the runbook (recommended to use your deploy annotation + Alertmanager inhibition if available).

Logan Wright (review): Can we align naming conventions with the template metrics mapping sheet? Specifically `service.name` vs `app` label usage. Also, the annotation text should mention the template tag filter users can apply in Console/Grafana.

Sergio Costa (author): Normalized to `service.name` and `deployment.environment` and removed legacy `app` references. Added note on filtering to template traffic using `redwood.template_id` (if present) but kept alert rules not depending on it.

Allison Grant (final): LGTM after last push. Thanks for being conservative on defaults.

Christine Wong (final): Approved. Please make sure the docs call out "start as ticketing" for most alerts.

Sergio Costa (author): Done—README now includes a "Paging vs ticketing" section and recommends enabling only SLO page alerts initially.
source.content:
Title: Add recommended alert rules + SLO examples for Quickstart Template apps

Summary
This PR adds a set of opt-in Prometheus/Grafana alert rules and SLO example recording rules intended for Redwood Quickstart Template applications (RAG + agents). The goal is to provide a production-minded baseline that surfaces real user-impact while avoiding noisy or high-cardinality alerting defaults.

Key principles
- Prefer SLO burn-rate alerts over raw symptom thresholds for paging.
- Gate alerts on minimum traffic to avoid flapping in dev / low volume.
- Avoid high-cardinality labels in alert grouping (no request_id, route, tool_name, model, prompt, tenant, user).
- Separate paging vs ticketing: only a small number of alerts are intended to page.

What ships in this PR
1) SLO recording rules (examples)
- Availability SLO (example): 99.5% over 30d for template app requests.
- Latency SLO (example): 95% of requests under 2s (customize per app type; RAG is often higher).
Recording rules are provided so the burn-rate math is consistent and cheap to evaluate.

2) Burn-rate alert rules (recommended)
- TemplateAvailabilityBurnRatePage: fast burn (5m/1h) for paging.
- TemplateAvailabilityBurnRateTicket: slow burn (30m/6h) for ticketing.
- TemplateLatencyBurnRatePage/Ticket: same structure, based on latency SLO error budget.
Each alert includes `severity` and a `runbook_url` annotation.

3) Symptom alerts (conservative + ticketing by default)
- StreamingDisconnectRateHigh (ticket): detects spikes in server-side streaming disconnect classification.
- ToolFailureRateHigh (ticket): high rate of tool invocation failures (aggregated; no per-tool fanout).
- RetrievalEmptyResultsHigh (ticket): retrieval returning empty when queries are present (helps detect indexing/config issues).
- OTelCollectorDown (page in some deployments, default ticket): collector not reachable/scraped.
- OTelExporterFailing (ticket): exporter failing to send spans/metrics, with volume gating.

Example snippets (illustrative)
- Multi-window burn-rate pattern:
  - Page: short_window=5m, long_window=1h, threshold tuned for 2% error budget consumption.
  - Ticket: short_window=30m, long_window=6h, lower urgency.
- Traffic gating (fast window): do not fire if fewer than 50 requests in 5m.

Operational guidance (docs updates)
- Start with SLO paging alerts only (availability + latency), keep symptom alerts as tickets.
- Avoid routing these alerts to on-call for low-traffic dev/staging environments.
- Use deploy annotations + inhibition rules to reduce noise during rollouts (recommended, not enforced).
- If users enable template-origin traffic tagging, they can build separate panels; alert rules intentionally don’t depend on those tags.

Testing
- Added promtool validation to CI to prevent invalid rule pushes.
- Local validation instructions included in alerts/README.md.

Notes / follow-ups
- We intentionally did not include per-model or per-tool alert fanout; those are best handled as dashboard drilldowns.
- Once templates error taxonomy is finalized, we should align symptom alerts to standardized error classes (network/auth/quota/tool/retrieval).

title: Add recommended alert rules + SLO examples for Quickstart Template apps
preview: Context: As part of the Quickstart Templates launch, we want to ship a recommended alerting baseline that users can enable without getting paged to death. This PR adds an opt-in alert rule group for template apps (RAG/agents) plus example SLOs and burn-rate alerts. The rules are designed around the metrics emitted by the templates + OTel collector configs from PR-156/158, and they intentionally avoid high-cardinality dimensions (no user_id, prompt hash, raw path, or tool args). What’s included: (1) SLO examples: availability + latency SLO recording rules with suggested targets and notes on how to adapt per workload; (2) Alert rules: multi-window burn-rate (fast/slow) alerts for the SLOs, plus a small set of “hard failures” (collector down, exporter errors) and “symptom alerts” (tool failure spikes, streaming disconnect spikes, retrieval empty-result spikes) with conservative defaults; (3) Noise avoidance guidance: documentation on paging vs ticketing alerts, minimum traffic gating, for/hold durations, and recommended grouping to avoid alert storms during deploys/traffic spikes. Rollout: Alerts are opt-in via the template dashboards pack jsonnet entrypoint; we’re not enabling anything by default in the published Grafana Cloud pack. Related: ENG-4184 (observability pack integration), ENG-4186 (smoke tests incl. streaming stability), and the templates’ error taxonomy work (draft).

source.title: Add log/trace correlation deep links for incident context
source.author: Allison Grant
source.description: Summary: Adds standardized log/trace correlation shortcuts (deep links) that incident-bot v2 and Console can embed for fast drill-down during incidents. This PR introduces a small, versioned link-template bundle in observability-pack plus validation tests to ensure URLs remain stable across releases. Motivation: In beta incident-bot v2 threads we repeatedly saw responders copy/paste request IDs and hunt manually across systems. We already link to dashboards (burn rate, deploy timeline, saturation), but we were missing the 1-click “take me to logs for this trace/request in the right time window” experience. This closes that gap while keeping URLs tenant-safe. Details: (1) Adds link templates for Traces and Logs with consistent parameter names and time windows, with support for service/route/region, optional trace_id, and optional request_id. (2) Adds a tiny resolver helper (used by bot + Console) that renders templates and URL-encodes values consistently (spaces, slashes, and ':' were common footguns). (3) Provides a default time window policy: incident_ts +/- 15m for traces and incident_ts +/- 30m for logs, configurable per caller. (4) Adds redaction/guardrails: tenant identifiers are not included in links by default; if provided, they must be hashed (tenant_hash) and only used in pre-approved queries. This mirrors the incident-bot v2 permissions/audit model and keeps deep links safe to paste into Slack. (5) Documents the deep-link contract in README so incident-bot and Console stay aligned. Example rendered links (staging): Trace UI: https://observability.redwood.example/traces?from=1719830400000&to=1719832200000&service=hosted-api&region=us-east-1&trace_id=7f3b0d2f3c5a4f01 Logs UI: https://observability.redwood.example/logs?from=1719830100000&to=1719832500000&service=hosted-api&region=us-east-1&query=request_id%3D%22req_01HT...%22 Notes for incident-bot integration: incident-bot should prefer trace_id when present (highest signal, lowest ambiguity) and fall back to request_id. The query string is intentionally constrained to avoid high-cardinality fields and accidental PII. Follow-ups: (a) once incident-bot PR-1862 lands, wire these links into the context payload schema under links.logs and links.traces; (b) after GA, we can add a third shortcut for “logs around rollout events” keyed by deploy_id (needs release-engineering join).
source.commits: 1) Add correlation link templates + schema validation 2) Implement URL rendering helper with canonical encoding 3) Add unit tests + golden snapshots for common incident inputs 4) Docs: deep-link contract + examples for incident-bot/Console consumers
source.ci_summary: Checks: lint (pass), unit-tests (pass), typecheck (pass), pack-validate (pass), link-schema-validate (pass)
source.review_conversation: Logan Wright: Can we ensure these links don’t accidentally introduce expensive queries (e.g., regex on message) when the bot pastes them repeatedly? Author (Allison): Yes—templates only allow exact-match filters on request_id/trace_id and stable dimensions (service/region). No free-form user query; fixtures cover encoding and quoting. Caleb Johnson: The trace link should not include request_id unless trace_id is missing, otherwise we end up with confusing multi-filter results. Author: Agreed; updated docs to say callers should prefer trace_id and only pass request_id as fallback. Also updated resolver helper example accordingly. Miles Thornton: Please confirm tenant safety. We had feedback that links pasted into #incidents must not include raw tenant IDs. Author: Added guardrail: tenant_id is rejected by schema; only tenant_hash is permitted and is optional. Docs note that bot should omit tenant filters unless strictly needed. Chloe Martin: Console needs stable parameter names; can we lock “from/to/service/region/trace_id/request_id”? Author: Locked in v1 schema and added a changelog note that breaking changes require v2 templates. Added golden test to ensure parameter names are consistent.
source.merge_outcome: Merged (squash) by Allison Grant into main
source.release_notes: Observability-pack now ships versioned correlation deep links for logs/traces (v1) suitable for embedding in incident-bot context cards and Console incident views.

source.title: Template dashboards v1: RAG + Agents + recommended alert rules (naming + publish steps)
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.release_notes:
- Adds prebuilt dashboards for Quickstart Templates (RAG + Agents): latency breakdown, retrieval quality signals, tool loop health, token economics.
- Adds recommended alert rules + SLO examples with conservative defaults (opt-in).
- Documents metric naming + tag cardinality guidance and publish/versioning steps.
source.description: Context: DevEx Quickstart Templates (RAG + agents) are standardizing OTel instrumentation and a small set of Redwood-specific metrics/attributes. This PR ships the initial dashboard + alerts pack in `observability-pack` so template apps have a first-run observability experience and internal Support/SRE can quickly triage common failures (401/429/timeouts/tool errors/retrieval empty). Scope: (1) dashboards (Grafana JSON) and (2) alert rules (Prometheus-style) + (3) documentation for metric naming conventions, required labels, and publish steps for release tagging. Dashboards included: (a) `Templates / RAG + Agents Overview` (golden signals + error taxonomy rollup) (b) `Templates / RAG Latency Breakdown` (gateway + model + retrieval + tool time; p50/p95; streaming disconnect rate) (c) `Templates / Retrieval Health` (retrieval hit rate, empty result rate, top-k distribution, chunking defaults sanity) (d) `Templates / Agent Tools` (tool call rate, tool error rate by tool_name, tool output truncation, max-iterations hits) (e) `Templates / Token Economics` (input/output tokens, cache hit %, effective $/1k tokens proxy, saturation signals). Alerts included (opt-in, documented as recommendations not defaults): (1) High 5xx rate for template-tagged traffic (2) Elevated 429 rate (rate limit pressure) (3) Streaming disconnects above threshold (4) Tool failure rate sustained (5) Retrieval empty results sustained (guard against misconfigured index/env). Naming conventions / cardinality: aligns with `observability-hooks-standard` and `quickstart-tracing-conventions`: metric namespace `redwood.template.*` (NOT `redwood.quickstart.*`), required low-cardinality labels only: `service.name`, `deployment.environment`, `template.name`, `template.version`, `model`, `region` (where available), `http.status_code` (bucketed), and `error.type` from the template taxonomy. `tool.name` is allowed but must be explicitly enumerated in the template (no user-provided arbitrary tool names), and dashboards/alerts include a note to disable tool_name breakdown if a customer adds dynamic tools. Publish steps: adds `docs/publishing.md` describing version bump, changelog entry, dashboard schema validation, and how DevEx references the pack from template READMEs. Notes: This PR intentionally avoids adoption telemetry (template header/tagging) since that lives in gateway + console filtering; dashboards include an optional variable `template_tag` to filter traffic when the tag is present, but they do not require it to function.
commits: dashboards: add Templates / RAG + Agents Overview
dashboards: add RAG latency breakdown + streaming panels
dashboards: add Retrieval Health dashboard
dashboards: add Agent Tools dashboard + tool error taxonomy mapping
dashboards: add Token Economics dashboard + cache panels
alerts: add recommended SLO-based alert rules for template apps
docs: metric naming conventions + publish/versioning steps
ci: add dashboard JSON schema validation and lint
source.conversation: Allison Grant (review): The dashboards look good, but we need to lock the metric namespace and labels before this ships. Please align to `redwood.template.*` and ensure we do not encourage high-cardinality labels. Specifically: remove any panel that groups by `request_id`/`trace_id`, avoid `user_id`, and confirm `tool_name` is safe (enumerated). Also, can you add a short section documenting which labels are required vs optional? Sergio Costa (author): Updated metric namespace across all queries to `redwood.template.*`. Removed the one panel that grouped by `http.target` (it was too spiky); replaced with route templates and status code buckets. Added `docs/metrics_naming_conventions.md` with required/optional labels and a callout that request_id/trace_id are logs/traces only. For `tool.name`, added a note in the dashboard and docs: only safe if tools are statically defined; otherwise disable the breakdown variable. Christine Wong (SRE review): Alert thresholds feel aggressive for a template. We don’t want noisy defaults. Can we clearly mark these as recommended/opt-in and set conservative windows (10–15m) with burn-rate examples instead of tight p95 thresholds? Also please include a runbook link placeholder. Sergio Costa (author): Adjusted alerts to be opt-in; widened windows to 15m, added burn-rate style examples and `runbook_url` placeholders, and updated `alerts/README.md` to stress ‘copy/adapt’ guidance. Logan Wright (telemetry/analytics): In the Overview dashboard, the filter variable `template_tag` is good. Please ensure dashboards work without it; some proxies strip unknown headers and not all users will have the tag. Sergio Costa (author): Confirmed dashboards key off emitted metrics/traces only; tag filter is optional. Added a note that template tagging is optional and might be disabled in some environments. Caleb Johnson (ingestion): Please make sure queries use stable OTel semantic conventions where possible (`service.name`, `deployment.environment`). If using custom attributes, prefix with `redwood.*`. Sergio Costa (author): Updated label names accordingly. Any custom label in examples is now `redwood.template.name` / `redwood.template.version` and documented. Monica Patel (DevEx): Can we add a short ‘Verification’ section: what a user should see locally when running docker-compose collector + template? Helps support deflection. Sergio Costa (author): Added ‘Verification’ section to `README.md` with expected panels and a minimal checklist. Allison Grant (final): Approved after naming/cardinality and opt-in alerting changes. Please make sure we cut a release tag and update the DevEx docs PR to point at the versioned artifact. Sergio Costa: Will do—added publish steps doc and will cut `observability-pack@v0.9.0` after merge.
source.merge_outcome: Merged to main; release tag to be cut after merge per docs/publishing.md

source.title: Add TP stall dashboard panels + alert rules (heartbeat gaps, stall reasons, timeout correlation)
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.release_notes: Adds Grafana panels + Prometheus alert rules for TP stalls (heartbeat gaps + stall reasons) and correlates them with request timeouts. Routes pages to eng-sre with runbook linkage.
source.description:
Context
We’ve had repeated Dedicated + high-throughput Hosted incidents where TP replicas appear healthy at the process level but are effectively wedged: requests hang, streaming timeouts rise, and autoscaling thrashes. Runtime-side instrumentation is landing in parallel (ENG-4823 / runtime PRs), but we need a first-class dashboard + alerting layer to detect silent stalls early and make triage actionable.

This PR adds:
- Grafana dashboard panels for TP heartbeat gaps (per rank + aggregated), stall reason counts, and request timeout correlation by model/cluster/replica.
- Prometheus alert rules with sane defaults + paging policy alignment (page only on sustained stall/timeout correlation, ticket otherwise).
- Links from alerts to the on-call runbook and to the most relevant filters (cluster/account/model).

What’s included (panels)
1) TP Heartbeat
- Panel: “TP heartbeat age (p50/p95/p99) by model + cluster”
- Panel: “Max heartbeat age per replica (topk)” to quickly identify the worst offenders
- Panel: “Heartbeat gap events / min” derived from heartbeat age crossing threshold
- Variables: env, region, cluster, namespace, model, tp_degree, replica_id

2) Stall reasons
- Panel: “TP stall reasons (rate)” stacked by reason_code (compute | comms | scheduler_starvation | kv_compaction | unknown)
- Panel: “In-flight collectives by type” to distinguish ‘stuck in collective’ vs ‘no progress / no collectives’
- Panel: “Scheduler queue depth + oldest age” for starvation diagnosis

3) Request impact + correlation
- Panel: “Request timeout rate (5xx + upstream timeout) by route/model”
- Panel: “Streaming disconnects/timeouts vs TP heartbeat gaps” (dual-axis)
- Panel: “Top replicas by timeout correlation score” (uses a simple z-score vs baseline window; see implementation note)

Alert rules
- TPStallHeartbeatGapPage (PAGE)
  - Trigger: heartbeat_age_seconds p99 > 30s for 10m AND request_timeouts_rate > baseline+X for same model/cluster
  - Routing: pager to eng-sre, notify eng-runtime channel
  - Runbook: runtime-tp-hang-mitigation (Confluence)
- TPStallHeartbeatGapTicket (TICKET)
  - Trigger: heartbeat_age_seconds p95 > 15s for 20m without timeout correlation (early warning)
- TPStallReasonSpike (TICKET)
  - Trigger: stall_reason_rate{reason_code!="unknown"} spikes above threshold for 15m

Implementation notes
- This assumes the runtime exports (or will soon export) the following series (names aligned with the metric naming thread):
  - redwood_runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_age_seconds{cluster,model,replica_id,rank}
  - redwood_runtime_tp_stall_reason_total{cluster,model,replica_id,reason_code}
  - redwood_runtime_tp_collectives_in_flight{cluster,model,replica_id,collective}
  - redwood_runtime_scheduler_queue_depth{cluster,model,replica_id,queue}
- For correlation panels we use existing gateway/request metrics:
  - redwood_gateway_request_timeouts_total{route,model,cluster}
  - redwood_gateway_streaming_disconnects_total{route,model,cluster,reason}
- If a metric isn’t present in a given environment, panels will render “No data” rather than hard failing (Grafana transformations updated accordingly).

Files / areas changed
- dashboards/tp-stall-and-timeout.jsonnet: new dashboard + variables + panels
- alerts/tp-stall-alerts.yaml: new alert groups and annotations
- mixins/runtime-mixin.libsonnet: wires dashboard + rules into mixin build
- docs/dashboards/tp-stall-and-timeout.md: short operator docs + common queries
- docs/alerts/paging-policy.md: adds rationale for page vs ticket split

Screenshots
- Added screenshots in the PR comments (Grafana ‘view’ links) for the main dashboard and the three alert panels.

Testing / verification
- jsonnetfmt + grafonnet compile

source.review_conversation:
Aisha Bello: Alerting looks good overall. Can we ensure we don’t page on ‘heartbeat gap’ alone without customer-visible impact? I want the page rule gated on timeout correlation (or at least elevated error/timeout rate).

Sergio Costa (author): Agreed. Updated TPStallHeartbeatGapPage to require both p99 heartbeat_age_seconds > 30s and request_timeouts_rate above baseline window for same model/cluster. Added a separate ticket-only early warning rule.

Allison Grant: Please confirm metric names match the runtime naming proposal. Also add reason_code label values to the panel legend explicitly.

Sergio Costa (author): Aligned queries to redwood_runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_age_seconds and redwood_runtime_tp_stall_reason_total. Updated legends to show reason_code and included a short mapping table in docs.

Rafael Mendes: Runbook link should point to the on-call mitigation page, not the design doc. Also, annotation should include replica_id if available.

Sergio Costa (author): Fixed runbook link, added replica_id to alert labels/annotations, and included a dashboard deep link pre-filtered to cluster/model/replica_id.

Jacob Stein: Correlation panel uses a z-score transform—can we document how it behaves when baseline is missing?

Sergio Costa (author): Added docs note: if baseline window has <N samples, panel falls back to raw timeout rate ranking and shows ‘baseline insufficient’ in panel description.

Approvals
- Aisha Bello: Approved
- Rafael Mendes: Approved
- Allison Grant: Approved

title: Add TP stall dashboard panels + alert rules (heartbeat gaps, stall reasons, timeout correlation)
preview:
Context
We’ve had repeated Dedicated + high-throughput Hosted incidents where TP replicas appear healthy at the process level but are effectively wedged: requests hang, streaming timeouts rise, and autoscaling thrashes. Runtime-side instrumentation is landing in parallel (ENG-4823 / runtime PRs), but we need a first-class dashboard + alerting layer to detect silent stalls early and make triage actionable.

This PR adds:
- Grafana dashboard panels for TP heartbeat gaps (per rank + aggregated), stall reason counts, and request timeout correlation by model/cluster/replica.
- Prometheus alert rules with sane defaults + paging policy alignment (page only on sustained stall/timeout correlation, ticket otherwise).
- Links from alerts to the on-call runbook and to the most relevant filters (cluster/account/model).

What’s included (panels)
1) TP Heartbeat
- Panel: “TP heartbeat age (p50/p95/p99) by model + cluster”
- Panel: “Max heartbeat age per replica (topk)” to quickly identify the worst offenders
- Panel: “Heartbeat gap events / min” derived from heartbeat age crossing threshold
- Variables: env, region, cluster, namespace, model, tp_degree, replica_id

2) Stall reasons
- Panel: “TP stall reasons (rate)” stacked by reason_code (compute | comms | scheduler_starvation | kv_compaction | unknown)
- Panel: “In-flight collectives by type” to distinguish ‘stuck in collective’ vs ‘no progress / no collectives’
- Panel: “Scheduler queue depth + oldest age” for starvation diagnosis

3) Request impact + correlation
- Panel: “Request timeout rate (5xx + upstream timeout) by route/model”
- Panel: “Streaming disconnects/timeouts vs TP heartbeat gaps” (dual-axis)
- Panel: “Top replicas by timeout correlation score” (uses a simple z-score vs baseline window; see implementation note)

Alert rules
- TPStallHeartbeatGapPage (PAGE)
  - Trigger: heartbeat_age_seconds p99 > 30s for 10m AND request_timeouts_rate > baseline+X for same model/cluster
  - Routing: pager to eng-sre, notify eng-runtime channel
  - Runbook: runtime-tp-hang-mitigation (Confluence)
- TPStallHeartbeatGapTicket (TICKET)
  - Trigger: heartbeat_age_seconds p95 > 15s for 20m without timeout correlation (early warning)
- TPStallReasonSpike (TICKET)
  - Trigger: stall_reason_rate{reason_code!="unknown"} spikes above threshold for 15m

Implementation notes
- This assumes the runtime exports (or will soon export) the following series (names aligned with the metric naming thread):
  - redwood_runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_age_seconds{cluster,model,replica_id,rank}
  - redwood_runtime_tp_stall_reason_total{cluster,model,replica_id,reason_code}
  - redwood_runtime_tp_collectives_in_flight{cluster,model,replica_id,collective}
  - redwood_runtime_scheduler_queue_depth{cluster,model,replica_id,queue}
- For correlation panels we use existing gateway/request metrics:
  - redwood_gateway_request_timeouts_total{route,model,cluster}
  - redwood_gateway_streaming_disconnects_total{route,model,cluster,reason}
- If a metric isn’t present in a given environment, panels will render “No data” rather than hard failing (Grafana transformations updated accordingly).

Files / areas changed
- dashboards/tp-stall-and-timeout.jsonnet: new dashboard + variables + panels
- alerts/tp-stall-alerts.yaml: new alert groups and annotations
- mixins/runtime-mixin.libsonnet: wires dashboard + rules into mixin build
- docs/dashboards/tp-stall-and-timeout.md: short operator docs + common queries
- docs/alerts/paging-policy.md: adds rationale for page vs ticket split

Screenshots
- Added screenshots in the PR comments (Grafana ‘view’ links) for the main dashboard and the three alert panels.

Testing / verification
- jsonnetfmt + grafonnet compile
- promtool check rules
- Deployed to staging Grafana (obs-staging) and validated:
  - variables filter as expected
  - alert expressions return expected series for a known test TP job
  - annotations render with runbook + dashboard links

Rollout plan
- Merge -> auto-publish to obs-staging
- After 24h of staging validation, promote to prod dashboards
- Enable paging rule initially in ‘warn’ mode for 48h (no page), then flip to page once thresholds are confirmed with SRE (Rafael/Aisha)

Links
- Linear: ENG-4823 (metrics), ENG-4828 (perf canary scenarios), ENG-4821 (initiative)
- Internal support: INT-2219 (enable extra debug logging during canary; referenced for runbook correlation)
- Runbook: Confluence /eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-tp-hang-mitigation

Checklist
- [x] Dashboard renders in staging
- [x] Alert rules validated with promtool
- [x] Annotations include runbook + dashboard deep links
- [x] Paging policy reviewed with SRE

Notes / follow-ups
- Once runtime PR adds collective counters to tracing spans (ENG-4823 follow-on), we should add a trace-link panel to jump from a stall window to exemplar trace IDs (Jacob: tracking separately).

source.title: Add retention verification + compliance pack generation dashboards/alerts
source.author: Allison Grant
source.description:
Summary: This PR adds the first version of the retention/compliance dashboards and alerting rules needed for the “Retention controls + compliance reporting pack” program. It introduces (1) Retention Enforcement Verification dashboards (TTL lag, purge throughput/failures, backlog) and (2) Compliance Report Pack Generation SLIs (success rate + latency) with corresponding alerts. It also updates the repo README to document metric prerequisites and rollout guidance.

Context / why: We’ve had multiple incidents/tickets where enforcement was “working” but not observable at the right level (e.g., policy applied but TTL lag growing in a single region; purge jobs silently throttling after maintenance). Security/GRC also needs a customer-ready way to evidence enforcement (at least internally) and to demonstrate the compliance pack generator’s reliability as we start using it for enterprise security reviews.

What’s included:
- Dashboards:
  - “Retention Controls: Verification (Overview)”
    - TTL lag (p50/p90/p99) by store + region
    - Purge throughput (rows deleted / min) and runtime
    - Purge failures (rate + top error reasons when exported)
    - Backlog / lag trend line for the last 7d
  - “Retention Controls: Purge Jobs (Deep Dive)”
    - Per-job panels (request-logs, audit-logs, traces, metrics, artifacts where applicable)
    - Retry volume + idempotent re-run indicators (based on job outcome labels)
  - “Compliance Pack: Generation SLI/SLO”
    - SLI: report_pack_generation_success_rate (derived)
    - Latency: report_pack_generation_duration_seconds (p50/p90/p99)
    - Queue/backlog (if enabled) and saturation signals
- Alerts (Prometheus rules):
  - RetentionTTLTooHigh (warning/page)
    - Warning: ttl_lag_seconds{store=~"request_logs|audit_logs"} p99 > 6h for 30m
    - Page: p99 > 24h for 15m (per region) OR backlog increasing for 60m
  - RetentionPurgeFailuresHigh (page)
    - purge_failures_total increase > 10 in 15m per job_type/region
  - RetentionPurgeNoProgress (warning)
    - purge_rows_deleted_total no increase for 2h while ttl_lag_seconds p90 > 2h
  - CompliancePackGenerationSLOViolation (warning/page)
    - Warning: success rate < 99.5% over 1h
    - Page: success rate < 98% over 30m OR p90 duration > 15m over 1h
- README updates:
  - Documents required metrics + labels (store, region, job_type, tenant_tier)
  - Notes on cardinality: alerts intentionally avoid tenant_id; dashboards allow optional filtering but default views are store/region only
  - Rollout instructions: enable dashboards first, then alerts at warning-only for 1 week before paging

Metric dependencies (expected to exist from the product/runtime PRs):
- ttl_lag_seconds{store,region}
- purge_rows_deleted_total{store,region,job_type}
- purge_failures_total{store,region,job_type,reason?}
- purge_job_duration_seconds{store,region,job_type}
- report_pack_generation_duration_seconds{region,outcome}
- report_pack_generation_total{region,outcome}

Implementation notes:
- Alert thresholds were chosen to match SRE guidance in the retention purge runbook: lag > 24h is a customer-impacting condition (policy drift / failure to meet retention), while shorter lags are warning signals that typically correlate with DB throttling or a stuck worker.
- We added a small “debug” row in the deep-dive dashboard for correlating lag spikes to purge job runtime, failures, and DB maintenance windows (annotation placeholder; actual annotation source is separate).
- Dashboards follow the existing Jsonnet conventions in this repo; panel UIDs are stable and include ‘retention-*’ prefixes for easy lookup.

Testing:
- Rendered dashboards locally (make dashboards) and validated against a staging Prometheus snapshot.
- promtool rule checks pass.
- Verified that rule expressions don’t use tenant_id and don’t blow up cardinality with region x store x job_type only.

Rollout plan:
- Ship dashboards immediately (safe).
- Enable alerts in warning-only in staging for 1 week; then promote to production warning-only for 1 week; then turn on paging routes.

References:
- Linear: ENG-18452 (verification dashboards/alerts), PM-842 (initiative), ENG-18441 (report pack generator)
- Related product metrics PRs: redwood/pr-28540-add-retention-verification-metrics, audit-log-exporter/pr-612-add-time-windowed-exports-and-filters

Screenshots: Added to the README section “Retention & Compliance Dashboards” (rendered preview images) and attached in the PR for review.
commits: dashboards: add retention verification overview dashboard
dashboards: add purge jobs deep-dive dashboard
dashboards: add compliance pack generation SLI dashboard
alerts: add retention TTL lag + purge failure rules
alerts: add compliance pack generation SLO rules
docs: update README with prerequisites + rollout guidance
chore: normalize panel tags and stable UIDs
source.review_conversation:
Sean Gallagher (SRE): The dashboard looks good, but we should avoid any alert dimensions that could explode cardinality. Please confirm no tenant_id usage anywhere in rule expressions. Also, can we add a “no progress” alert? That would have caught the post-maintenance backlog we saw last week.

Allison Grant (author): Confirmed: no tenant_id in any rule. Dashboards have tenant_tier as an optional filter but rules are strictly store/region/job_type. Added RetentionPurgeNoProgress (warning) based on ‘rows deleted’ not increasing while lag is elevated.

Logan Wright (Telemetry): For ttl_lag_seconds, some stores won’t emit p99 depending on scrape interval; please use histogram_quantile on the bucket metric where available, or fall back to max over time. Right now it looks like you’re assuming precomputed p99.

Allison Grant: Good catch. Updated rules to use histogram_quantile(0.99, sum by (le,store,region)(rate(ttl_lag_seconds_bucket[5m]))) where the bucket exists; added a compatibility path guarded by recording rules in lib/metrics/retention.libsonnet. README updated to note both options.

Nadia Rahman (Observability): Can we standardize the panel naming to match the rest of the pack (“Overview / Deep Dive / SLO”) and add tags so they show up in search?

Allison Grant: Renamed dashboards and added tags: [retention, compliance, security, verification].

Kevin Osei (Retention/Audit exports): For compliance pack, I’d like an explicit panel for ‘percent generated within 15m’ since that’s what we’ll be quoting to GRC. Also, please keep the alert window >= 30m to avoid paging during deploys.

Allison Grant: Added “<=15m” SLI panel and kept paging criteria at 30m/1h windows.

Sean Gallagher: Approved once you flip the initial paging severity to warning-only (we’ll route to #oncall-retention for the first week).

Allison Grant: Done. Paging labels are present but default route config is warning-only; README includes staged enablement steps.

Approvals:
- Sean Gallagher: Approved
- Logan Wright: Approved
- Nadia Rahman: Approved
source.release_notes: Observability-pack now includes Grafana dashboards and Prometheus alert rules for retention enforcement verification and compliance report pack generation SLIs; README documents prerequisites and rollout guidance.

source.title: Add Dedicated staged rollout dashboards + alert templates (stage p95/p99, error rate, GPU saturation, promotion annotations)
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.release_notes: Adds Grafana dashboards and Prometheus alert templates for Dedicated staged rollouts. Includes per-stage latency/error/saturation panels, region breakdowns, and rollout promotion/abort annotations. Adds initial SRE-tuned alert thresholds aligned with Dedicated rollout gates (v1).
source.description:
Summary: This PR adds a first cut of dashboard + alert templates for the Dedicated staged rollout system. The dashboards are stage-aware (stage_id / stage_name) and are designed to support the rollout gate model in the policy engine (latency, errors, saturation/headroom, GPU health proxies). It also adds annotations for stage promotion/abort/hold events so operators can correlate metric shifts with rollout actions.

Context / dependencies: This is intended to ship alongside the redwood monorepo changes that (a) emit stage tags in metrics/traces and (b) provide audit events for promote/abort/hold. Dashboards assume the following labels are present on the relevant metrics: dedicated_pool_id, rollout_plan_id, stage_id, stage_name, region. For cardinality safety, the dashboards intentionally avoid any per-request labels and only group-by the allowlisted stage identifiers.

What’s included:
- Grafana dashboards (templated) for Dedicated staged rollouts:
  1) "Dedicated Rollouts: Stage Overview" (default landing)
     - Stage timeline annotations (promote/abort/hold)
     - p95/p99 latency per stage (overall + per region)
     - request rate + error rate (5xx + 429 separately)
     - saturation/headroom proxy panels (GPU utilization, queue depth)
     - OOM / throttling proxy panels (where available)
  2) "Dedicated Rollouts: Stage Drilldown" (single stage focus)
     - gating window panels (5m + 15m, side-by-side)
     - sample size panel (req/min, tokens/sec) to reduce false confidence
     - region comparison table with pass/fail indicator driven by threshold variables
  3) "Dedicated Rollouts: Fleet Signals" (operator/SRE)
     - node/GPU health summary by pool + region
     - ECC error rate proxy + throttling counters (when present)
- Alert templates (Prometheus rules) aligned to v1 gates:
  - GateCandidateHighErrorRate (per stage)
  - GateCandidateP99LatencyRegression (per stage)
  - GateCandidateHigh429Rate (per stage; motivated by early canary starving capacity)
  - GateCandidateGPUSaturation (per stage + per region)
  - GateCandidateQueueDepthSpike (per stage)
  - TelemetryStaleForStage (marks "unknown" gate evidence scenarios; ticket-level)
- Annotations:
  - Adds Grafana annotation queries backed by the audit event stream (via Loki index) for: rollout.plan.created, rollout.stage.promoted, rollout.stage.aborted, rollout.stage.held, rollout.stage.force_promoted.
  - Annotation text includes actor + reason (if provided) and links back to the Console rollout page (URL templated).

Notes on thresholds (v1):
- Thresholds are intentionally conservative and are parameterized as Grafana variables where possible so we can tune by tier without re-cutting dashboards.
- We split 429 from 5xx because 429 is the most common early-stage failure signature when partition sizing is too small.
- Missing data: dashboards show "no data" explicitly; alerts include a separate TelemetryStaleForStage rule instead of failing other rules open/closed.

Validation:
- Tested with staging data using synthetic stage tags and replayed a known canary incident window from SUP-28522 to confirm panels highlight 429 + queue depth.
- Verified annotations render correctly with sample audit events and do not explode cardinality.

Rollout plan:
- Land dashboards behind the dedicated_rollouts folder; do not set as default home.
- SRE to enable alert rules for staging first, then production after one week of tuning.

Follow-ups (not in this PR):
- Add a customer-facing pared-down dashboard variant (hides infra-only signals).
- Add quality regression panels (eval scores) once the stage-tagged eval pipeline is finalized.
- Tighten region impairment visualization (heatmap) after we confirm multi-region rollout behavior in beta.

Screenshots (staging):
- https://grafana.staging.redwood.example/d/dedicated-rollouts-stage-overview
- https://grafana.staging.redwood.example/d/dedicated-rollouts-stage-drilldown

References:
- Linear: ENG-5966 (stage-tagged metrics), PM-827 (default thresholds)
- Support: SUP-28522 (canary 429s), SUP-28605 (missing gate evidence)
- Related code: redwood PRs for stage tags + audit events (see internal rollout doc for exact SHAs).
commits: dashboards: add dedicated rollout stage overview + drilldown dashboards
alerts: add gate-aligned alert templates for stage error/latency/saturation/429
annotations: add audit-event-backed annotation queries for promote/abort/hold
docs: add README for dedicated rollouts dashboards + label expectations
source.conversation:
Allison Grant (review): Can you confirm we’re not grouping by any high-cardinality labels? I’m worried stage_id + rollout_plan_id could explode on large customers. Also, please add explicit guidance in the README about the allowlist and expected label set.

Sergio Costa (author): Confirmed. All queries only group by {dedicated_pool_id, stage_id, region} and use rollout_plan_id only for annotations (log search), not metrics. Added README section "Cardinality guardrails" and removed an accidental by (rollout_plan_id) on the error panel.

Logan Wright (review): Telemetry staleness rule: please make it ticket-level (not page) and ensure it keys off ingest lag rather than absolute absence (some stages truly have low traffic). Also, can we add a min sample size hint panel? We’ve seen false positives when canary stage is quiet.

Sergio Costa (author): Updated staleness rule to require both (a) metrics missing and (b) ingest lag > 3m from the pipeline heartbeat metric. Set severity=ticket. Added "Requests/min" and "Tokens/sec" panels and a note: 'Gate results unreliable under low sample size'.

Peter Holtz (review): The 429 alert threshold seems low for spiky workloads. Can we either use burn-rate style logic or a longer window so we don’t flap? We don’t want rollout alerts paging every time a customer load test starts.

Sergio Costa (author): Switched 429 rule to two-window approach (5m + 30m) and added a 'sustained' requirement. Also changed severity to page only when 429 coincides with queue depth spike + saturation (otherwise ticket).

Rafael Mendes (review): Please align the p99 regression logic with SLO gate windows (5m and 15m) and add a panel that shows 'baseline stage' vs 'candidate stage'. Otherwise it’s hard for oncall to reason about regression.

Sergio Costa (author): Added baseline-vs-candidate comparison panel using variables baseline_stage_name and candidate_stage_name (defaults baseline=previous stage). Alerts use 15m window for p99 regression; dashboards show both 5m and 15m.

CI: First run failed due to jsonnetfmt mismatch; reran after formatting. Final checks: lint, jsonnet build, promtool check rules, dashboard render tests.

Outcome: Approved by Allison/Logan/Peter/Rafael and merged (squash).

source.title: Dedicated throughput SLO: add dashboard panels for floor tracking, saturation, and drilldowns
source.author: Sergio Costa
release_notes: Adds tier-aware Dedicated throughput panels (floor vs actual) with per-customer and per-deployment drilldowns.
Adds saturation signals (admission control shed rate, queue depth, GPU utilization proxies) to speed up triage.
Standardizes variables and link-outs to runbooks and SLO evaluator summaries.
source.description:
Context: We’re rolling out a formal Dedicated throughput SLO and need a consistent, low-friction dashboard experience for SRE/CS and (later) a curated subset for customers. This PR extends the dedicated mixin dashboards with panels that (a) track throughput floors over time per customer/deployment, (b) show saturation signals that correlate with SLO misses, and (c) provide drilldowns into the exact deployment/model/region slice.

What’s included: 1) “Throughput floor vs actual” panels: tokens/sec (per deployment_id) overlaid with computed floor (tier + shape) and SLO evaluator compliance; includes 5m/30m rollups with clear “below floor” highlighting. 2) “Saturation signals” row: admission-control shed rate, queue depth, in-flight requests, batcher wait time, KV cache hit ratio (when available), and GPU utilization proxy (SM occupancy / active time from node exporter where present). 3) Drilldowns: links from customer-level panels → deployment-level panels, and deployment-level → explore queries + logs/traces (requires `deployment_id` label) with pre-filled time range and filters. 4) Variables & filtering: standardized template variables (`customer_id`, `deployment_id`, `tier`, `region`, `model_family`) and a default view that emphasizes Dedicated pools only. 5) Runbook hooks: adds inline links to the Dedicated throughput SLO escalation runbook and the tier-aware routing/overload protection runbook.

Query semantics: Uses the canonical per-deployment throughput metric emitted by runtime (`redwood_inference_tokens_total` / `rate`), with streaming-aware token accounting (relies on the runtime fix merged separately; this PR avoids older `stream_tokens_total` series). Floor overlay is sourced from the SLO evaluator summary series (`dedicated_throughput_floor_tokens_per_sec`) when available; falls back to a static tier mapping panel note when the evaluator series is missing in a region.

Screenshots: Not attached; dashboards can be rendered locally via `make dashboards` and compared in Grafana using the “mixin-preview” folder output.

Rollout: Safe to merge; panels are behind the existing Dedicated dashboard folder and only appear where the required metrics exist. Follow-up: once the customer-facing Console widget lands, we’ll likely want a simplified “status” view fed by the same evaluator series.
commits: mixin(dedicated): add variables for customer_id/deployment_id/tier (b8c1f2d)
dashboards: add throughput floor overlay panels + thresholds (c2a7e19)
dashboards: add saturation signals row (shed rate, queue, batch wait) (d4b6a02)
dashboards: add drilldown links to explore/logs/traces (f09ad33)
chore: jsonnetfmt + grafana-lint fixes (a3e7710)
conversation: Allison Grant (review): The variable naming is drifting from our conventions. Can we use `customer_id`/`deployment_id` consistently across all Dedicated dashboards, and avoid `acct`? Also please ensure panel titles are actionable (include units + window).

Sergio Costa (author): Updated vars to `customer_id`/`deployment_id` everywhere and removed `acct`. Panel titles now include `tokens/sec (5m rate)` or `tokens/sec (30m avg)` where relevant. Pushed commit a3e7710.

Sean Gallagher (review): For the floor overlay: please make it explicit that the floor is computed from evaluator series and not derived in-dashboard. We don’t want ad-hoc PromQL copies of the SLO logic. Also double-check you’re not using the old streaming counters.

Sergio Costa (author): Agree. The overlay pulls `dedicated_throughput_floor_tokens_per_sec` + `dedicated_throughput_compliance` (when present). No ad-hoc floor math in PromQL; I added a short panel description to that effect. Queries are on `redwood_inference_tokens_total` and not the older streaming series.

Aisha Bello (review): The saturation row is useful, but the GPU utilization proxy will be missing in some private clusters. Can we make it not look “broken” when absent, and add a note on expected availability? Also, can we add a panel for admission-control queue time / shed reason if available?

Sergio Costa (author): Made the GPU proxy panel show a neutral empty-state (no thresholds) and added a note: “present in hosted/dedicated regions with node exporter”. Added shed rate + queue depth; we don’t yet have shed reason labels consistently, so I left that as a follow-up (noted in panel description).

Rafael Mendes (review): Drilldowns: link-out query params look good, but please ensure we’re not leaking customer identifiers into shared URLs outside our Grafana domain. Also, time range should default to last 6h rather than 1h for incident triage.
Sergio Costa (author): Links are relative to Grafana and Explore only; no external destinations. Updated default drilldown to last 6h. Thanks.
Allison Grant (approval): Looks aligned with the Dedicated dashboard standards now. Approved.
Sean Gallagher (approval): Approved. Thanks for keeping floor logic centralized in the evaluator series.
Rafael Mendes (approval): Approved. Merge when green.

source.title: Add Private backup/restore dashboards + alert rules (backup success, duration, restore drill outcomes)
source.author: Sergio Costa
source.release_notes: - Adds Grafana dashboards for Redwood Private backup jobs + restore drills (success, duration, bytes, last success). - Adds Prometheus alert rules for stale backups and failing restore drills with tuning knobs + maintenance-window guidance.
source.description: Context: As part of the Private Day-2 backup/restore toolkit, we need an opinionated observability pack that SRE/Solutions can deploy into customer VPC/on-prem environments to answer two questions quickly: (1) are backups happening and completing successfully on schedule, and (2) are restore drills passing and meeting RTO targets in staging. This PR adds a set of dashboards and alert rules designed to work across hosted VPC installs and constrained on-prem/air-gapped setups (where available metrics are limited and log shipping may be disabled). Scope: (A) dashboards for backup job success rate, last_success_ts, duration, bytes, and error categories; (B) dashboards for restore drill outcomes (pass/fail, duration, validation phase breakdown) and drill cadence; (C) alert rules with conservative defaults plus documented tuning for noisy environments (slow object storage, intermittent KMS throttling, planned maintenance). Metrics assumed: redwood_private_backup_job_success_total, redwood_private_backup_job_duration_seconds, redwood_private_backup_bytes_total, redwood_private_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds, redwood_private_restore_drill_success_total, redwood_private_restore_drill_duration_seconds, redwood_private_restore_validation_phase_duration_seconds, and redwood_private_backup_error_total{category=...} (emitted by control-plane + installer hooks in related PRs). Dashboards/alerts are grouped under a new folder 'Redwood Private / Backup & DR'. Tuning notes: alert thresholds use per-install labels (deployment_id, environment, cluster) and are designed to avoid paging during planned maintenance by supporting a maintenance label/recording rule override; duration alerts are based on p95 over a 6h window to reduce sensitivity to occasional NFS/S3 jitter; stale backup alert uses last_success_ts with configurable expected_interval (default 24h) and grace window (default +6h). Out of scope: defining the core metrics emission (handled in redwood monorepo), paging policy selection (SRE runbook), and customer-specific routing/notification integrations. Validation: Tested in staging with a simulated failing backup job and an intentionally broken restore drill; ensured alerts fire and resolve; verified dashboard variables work when some metric series are absent (air-gapped mode without bytes_total). Screenshots are attached in the PR comments (not committed).
commits: dashboards(private): add Backup Overview dashboard (success/last success/duration/bytes/error categories)
dashboards(private): add Restore Drill dashboard (outcomes, duration, phase breakdown)
alerts(private): add stale backup + backup failure rate rules with labels + annotations
alerts(private): add restore drill failing + restore drill stale rules (staging-focused defaults)
docs: add tuning notes for noisy storage/KMS + maintenance window suppression guidance
chore: normalize dashboard UIDs + folder structure; update jsonnet lint
notable_snippets: Alert rule (stale backup): alert: RedwoodPrivateBackupStale expr: (time() - redwood_private_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds) > (redwood_private_backup_expected_interval_seconds + redwood_private_backup_grace_seconds) for: 30m labels: severity: page annotations: summary: "Backup stale for {{ $labels.cluster }}" description: "No successful backup within expected interval (+grace). Check backup CronJob, storage backend, KMS availability, and permissions."
Duration panel query: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, cluster, environment) (rate(redwood_private_backup_job_duration_seconds_bucket[6h])))
source.testing: CI: jsonnet-lint, grafana-dashboard-schema, promtool check rules (PASS). Manual: staged alert firing via metric injection + disabled restore drill runner to confirm stale alert. Verified dashboards render with missing optional series.
review_conversation: Allison Grant (review): The dashboard layout looks good. Two requests: (1) please avoid alerting on raw 'increase(failures[5m]) > 0' because it’ll be noisy for KMS hiccups; prefer error rate over a longer window + add a severity split. (2) make sure all panels have consistent label keys (cluster/environment/deployment_id) and default variables work if deployment_id is missing (older installs).

Sergio Costa (author): Updated alerts to use a 30m window for failure-rate plus a 'warning' tier, and added a fallback variable mapping when deployment_id is absent. Also standardized label joins in queries so panels don’t drop series.

Sean Gallagher (review): Please add explicit guidance on maintenance windows. We keep getting paged during upgrade windows in staging. Can we support a suppression label or a recording rule that on-call can flip?

Sergio Costa (author): Added docs section 'Maintenance windows / planned downtime' with two options: Alertmanager silence + optional recording rule redwood_private_maintenance_window{cluster=...}=1 to gate paging alerts. Also added alert expr guard 'unless on(cluster) redwood_private_maintenance_window == 1' when the series exists.
Neha Kapoor (review): Restore drill alert defaults should be non-paging by default outside staging. Can we set severity=warn unless environment=staging, and add annotation to point to the DR runbook?

Sergio Costa (author): Done. Restore drill failing is warn by default and upgrades to page only for environment=staging (or when redwood_private_restore_drill_paging_enabled==1). Added runbook link annotation and consistent 'next steps' text.
Rafael Mendes (review): One more thing: add a panel showing drill cadence (days since last successful drill) and include it on the overview dashboard. We use that in game day readiness reviews.
Sergio Costa (author): Added 'Days since last successful restore drill' stat + timeseries; also included it on Backup Overview as a secondary row.
Allison Grant (approve): LGTM after the alert tuning changes and label normalization. Thanks for including the maintenance window guidance.
Sean Gallagher (approve): Approved. This matches what on-call needs; please ensure release notes call out the new severity behavior for restore drill alerts.

title: Add Private backup/restore dashboards + alert rules (backup success, duration, restore drill outcomes)
preview: Context: As part of the Private Day-2 backup/restore toolkit, we need an opinionated observability pack that SRE/Solutions can deploy into customer VPC/on-prem environments to answer two questions quickly: (1) are backups happening and completing successfully on schedule, and (2) are restore drills passing and meeting RTO targets in staging. This PR adds a set of dashboards and alert rules designed to work across hosted VPC installs and constrained on-prem/air-gapped setups (where available metrics are limited and log shipping may be disabled). Scope: (A) dashboards for backup job success rate, last_success_ts, duration, bytes, and error categories; (B) dashboards for restore drill outcomes (pass/fail, duration, validation phase breakdown) and drill cadence; (C) alert rules with conservative defaults plus documented tuning for noisy environments (slow object storage, intermittent KMS throttling, planned maintenance). Metrics assumed: redwood_private_backup_job_success_total, redwood_private_backup_job_duration_seconds, redwood_private_backup_bytes_total, redwood_private_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds, redwood_private_restore_drill_success_total, redwood_private_restore_drill_duration_seconds, redwood_private_restore_validation_phase_duration_seconds, and redwood_private_backup_error_total{category=...} (emitted by control-plane + installer hooks in related PRs). Dashboards/alerts are grouped under a new folder 'Redwood Private / Backup & DR'. Tuning notes: alert thresholds use per-install labels (deployment_id, environment, cluster) and are designed to avoid paging during planned maintenance by supporting a maintenance label/recording rule override; duration alerts are based on p95 over a 6h window to reduce sensitivity to occasional NFS/S3 jitter; stale backup alert uses last_success_ts with configurable expected_interval (default 24h) and grace window (default +6h). Out of scope: defining the core metrics emission (handled in redwood monorepo), paging policy selection (SRE runbook), and customer-specific routing/notification integrations. Validation: Tested in staging with a simulated failing backup job and an intentionally broken restore drill; ensured alerts fire and resolve; verified dashboard variables work when some metric series are absent (air-gapped mode without bytes_total). Screenshots are attached in the PR comments (not committed).
